title: Sam Klar on GMO Domestic Resilience: Quality, Value, and the Reindustrialization Trade
metaDescription: Sam Klar of GMO breaks down the thesis behind DRES — a concentrated active ETF targeting U.S. reindustrialization with a quality and value framework, ~90% do...
content:
Sam Klar has spent nearly 20 years at GMO, starting straight out of undergrad and working his way through global equity and event-driven merger arbitrage. Now he's the portfolio manager behind DRES, the GMO Domestic Resilience ETF, which launched in October 2025. The fund is built on a simple but underappreciated fact: less than 60% of S&P 500 company revenue actually comes from the United States. DRES flips that ratio to about 90% domestic revenue and concentrates in 35 to 40 names tied directly to America's reindustrialization wave.

The strategy grew out of a multi-year research project at GMO on deglobalization. Sam and his team identified four proprietary sector buckets that traditional GICS classifications miss: manufacturing and automation, transportation and logistics, energy and materials, and defense. They start with roughly 200 companies long the reindustrialization call option, then apply GMO's quality and value filter to get down to the final portfolio. The value discipline is real. Sam wanted Fastenal at launch but couldn't get comfortable with the price. When it reported a disappointing quarter and sold off 20%, he bought it. The conviction was there from day one; patience made the entry work.

Union Pacific sits as the top holding, and Sam's thesis goes beyond the obvious macro tailwind. UP is in the process of acquiring Norfolk Southern, which would create an end-to-end transcontinental railroad. Sam thinks the transformative potential of that combination is not priced in because regulatory uncertainty keeps the market from giving it credit. In steel, they hold Nucor and Steel Dynamics, choosing the best-capitalized operators specifically because the sector is cyclical and quality provides resilience through the cycle. The broader logic across transportation and logistics is picks-and-shovels: own the infrastructure that benefits from reindustrialization regardless of which specific factories get built.

One of the more interesting points: the reindustrialization theme is genuinely bipartisan. Sam says quotes from Democratic and Republican leaders on domestic resilience are almost indistinguishable if you cover the attribution. DRES is not a bet on any single administration. At 50 basis points with only one overlap in XLI's top holdings, it's designed as a satellite complement to tech-heavy, multinational core positions.

Key Takeaways

  • DRES holds 35 to 40 concentrated positions with roughly 90% U.S. revenue exposure, compared to less than 60% for the S&P 500
  • Four proprietary sector buckets replace traditional GICS: manufacturing/automation, transportation/logistics, energy/materials, and defense
  • Quality and value discipline is enforced at the stock level. Fastenal was held in research until a 20% selloff made the valuation work
  • Union Pacific's pending Norfolk Southern acquisition could create a transcontinental railroad, and Sam believes the market isn't pricing the upside
  • The reindustrialization trade is bipartisan and durational, not dependent on any single policy cycle

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: David Nicholas on Nicholas X Funds: Building a Commodity-Equity-Options Suite from Scratch
metaDescription: David Nicholas, founder of Nicholas X Funds, breaks down the architecture behind his ETF suite — 50% commodity, 50% ecosystem companies, plus an active optio...
content:
David Nicholas still runs a private wealth practice in Atlanta alongside his ETF business. His fund journey started during COVID when he saw an opportunity to recreate the insurance company balance sheet in a liquid ETF wrapper: 90 to 95% fixed income, with interest income funding long call options for equity upside. That became FIX. From there came GIAX, then BLOX (the crypto fund, now approaching $250 million in assets), and now a wave of thematic commodity funds covering silver, gold, defense, nuclear, and additional Bitcoin products. In this conversation, David walks through the common architecture across the suite, his options philosophy, and what it actually takes to scale an ETF business by betting revenue back into new launches.

The common thread across X Funds is a three-part structure: roughly 50% commodity or core asset exposure, 50% equity positions in companies that benefit from that commodity, and an active options overlay on both sleeves. In SLVX, that means silver spot exposure paired with silver miners and royalty companies like First Majestic, Pan American Silver, and Wheaton Precious Metals. WEAPON pairs rare earth materials with defense companies. The nuclear fund pairs uranium with nuclear operators. David calls it owning the whole ecosystem rather than just the commodity.

The options piece is where David gets most animated. His core argument: covered call strategies almost never beat the underlying in rising markets because the short call caps upside. X Funds use short put spreads on equity positions instead, collecting premium while preserving full participation in gains. On the commodity sleeves where they do sell call spreads, they also buy long calls above the spread to recapture sharp moves higher. Nothing is indexed. All funds are actively managed with the ability to shift from puts to calls, add protective hedges, or remove them at any time.

David confronts the income fund critique directly: high distributions can mask declining NAV. His response draws a hard line between over-distribution (bad) and underlying positions going down (acceptable in volatile asset classes). BLOX generates roughly 50% options yield but distributes only 36%, targeting an 80% success rate on trades. Bitcoin is down 20 to 30% since inception; BLOX is roughly flat. Competitors declare 40 to 50% yield. David says he could match that tomorrow but considers it irresponsible if the options income doesn't justify it.

Key Takeaways

  • All X Funds share a three-part architecture: ~50% commodity/core asset, ~50% ecosystem equity companies, plus an active options overlay on both sleeves
  • Short put spreads on equity positions preserve full upside participation while collecting income, unlike covered calls which cap gains
  • BLOX distributes 36% of roughly 50% options yield, tying distribution directly to actual options income rather than an arbitrary yield target
  • The crawl-walk-run path: one fund per year for the first three, then all revenue reinvested into launching six new products at once after BLOX crossed $100 million
  • NAV decline from over-distribution is avoidable and unacceptable. NAV decline from underlying positions going down is expected in volatile asset classes like crypto and commodities

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Bob Elliott on Unlimited Funds: Strategy, Process, and What Sets It Apart
metaDescription: Bob Elliott, co-founder and CIO of Unlimited, returns to break down how his firm is bringing Vanguard-style indexing to the hedge fund world. After sp
content:
Bob Elliott, co-founder and CIO of Unlimited, returns to Behind the Ticker to break down how his firm replicates hedge fund strategies in liquid ETFs at a fraction of the cost. After spending the bulk of his career at Bridgewater building proprietary strategies, Bob launched Unlimited on two truths the industry wouldn't say out loud: institutional hedge funds are largely no better than their peers over time, and managers capture nearly all the alpha they generate through fees. His solution is to diversify across managers, cut fees to roughly one-tenth of traditional levels, and deliver it all through ETFs.

Unlimited's proprietary third-generation replication technology uses Bayesian machine learning to infer how hedge fund managers are positioned in near-real-time. The key insight is that managers can't flip positions instantaneously, so positioning is path-dependent. That constraint narrows the universe of plausible portfolios at any given moment. Unlike older rolling regression approaches that average months or years of data, Unlimited's model solves for today's portfolio in the context of previous ones. Bob is direct about the research discipline: 95% of their explorations to improve the process fail, which he views as a positive signal that the original approach wasn't over-fit.

The suite now includes HFND (broad hedge fund index), HFEQ (equity long-short), HFMF (managed futures), and HFGM (global macro). HFGM crossed $100 million within nine months of launch. The 2X target return products deliver hedge fund exposure at equity-index-level volatility. Bob makes the case for moving from 60-40 to 50-30-20, with 20% in alternatives. His argument: the biggest indictment of liquid alts has been low volatility and high fees, but it doesn't have to be that way.

On growing the business: "The biggest risk you have as a startup ETF issuer is that you blow yourself up spending too much money on sales and distribution." Unlimited's approach is guerrilla marketing. They have live data on every RIA's holdings and channel information to advisors with the highest probability of interest rather than competing on billboard spend with BlackRock and Vanguard.

Key Takeaways

  • Third-generation Bayesian machine learning replication infers hedge fund positioning in near-real-time, capturing adaptive alpha that older regression methods miss
  • 2X target return products deliver hedge fund exposure at equity-index-level volatility and roughly one-tenth of traditional hedge fund fees
  • HFGM (global macro) is the recommended entry point for most advisors, offering all-weather alpha potential with diversification
  • Bob advocates 50-30-20 (equities, fixed income, alternatives) over traditional 60-40. The time to allocate to alternatives is when you don't think you need them
  • 95% of explorations to improve the process fail, which signals the original approach wasn't over-fit

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: David Auerbach on Hoya Capital's HOMZ & RIET: Strategy, Process, and What Sets It Apart
metaDescription: David Auerbach, CIO of Hoya Capital, spent over a decade trading REITs at Green Street Advisors in Dallas before getting a front-row seat to the ETF i
content:
David Auerbach, CIO of Hoya Capital, spent over a decade trading REITs at Green Street Advisors before getting a front-row seat to ETF industry growth at Esposito Securities. Now he runs two REIT-focused ETFs, HOMZ and RIET, managing over $135 million. In this conversation, David tackles the two biggest misconceptions advisors have about REITs: that they're all high yield and that they're all interest rate sensitive. His point is that most passive REIT ETFs are far more concentrated than people realize, with a handful of large-cap names yielding just 2 to 3% dominating the top holdings.

RIET deliberately inverts the weighting of traditional REIT benchmarks like VNQ. It overweights small caps, mid caps, mortgage REITs, and REIT preferred stocks to deliver a 10%+ annualized monthly dividend across roughly 100 holdings with no single position above 1.5%. The preferred stock allocation is unique: about 10% of the portfolio in liquid REIT preferreds, something David believes no other REIT ETF currently offers, adding several percentage points of yield. The selection process incorporates a quality score across management, operational history, communication, and transparency.

HOMZ takes the other side, capturing the structural U.S. housing supply shortage through homebuilders, residential REITs, service providers, brokers, and retailers like Home Depot and Lowe's. David's housing thesis is straightforward: until builders deliver a lighter product (a $300,000 home instead of $500,000) and manufactured housing gets more respect, the supply-demand imbalance persists.

David uses a great analogy for REIT portfolio management: "It plays like the NCAA tournament. Last in, first out. The guys toward the top aren't going anywhere. It's the 5 to 10 names at the bottom range that we need to look at closely." He also identified 4% on the 10-year Treasury as the key threshold for REIT sentiment. And the M&A wave is significant: 17 different REITs have merged or pursued strategic alternatives in just four months, driven by private equity finding it cheaper to acquire existing platforms at discounts to NAV than to build from scratch.

Key Takeaways

  • RIET inverts traditional REIT benchmark weighting, overweighting small/mid caps, mortgage REITs, and preferred stocks for 10%+ annualized monthly dividends across ~100 holdings
  • No single RIET position exceeds 1.5%, and it's the only REIT ETF combining common and preferred stocks in one wrapper
  • HOMZ captures the structural U.S. housing supply shortage through homebuilders, residential REITs, service providers, and retailers
  • 4% on the 10-year Treasury is the inflection point for REIT investor sentiment. Below 4% favors REITs, above 4% creates headwinds
  • 17 REITs have merged or pursued strategic alternatives in four months, driven by private equity acquiring platforms below NAV

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Raymond Micaletti on the MOOD ETF: Strategy, Process, and What Sets It Apart
metaDescription: Raymond Micaletti built the MOOD ETF around a single idea: institutions consistently outperform retail traders, and you can measure the difference. Af
content:
Raymond Micaletti built the MOOD ETF around a single idea: institutions consistently outperform retail traders, and you can measure the difference. After watching a relative sentiment indicator correctly call two major market bottoms while all his other signals failed, he stopped ignoring it and started building. In this conversation, Ray breaks down how he uses Commitments of Traders data and sentiment surveys to construct signals across equities, bonds, commodities, and currencies, and why pairing relative sentiment with trend following creates a strategy where each approach offsets the other's weaknesses.

The structural advantage institutions have over retail is the core of Ray's thesis. MOOD is a fund-of-ETFs, using the most liquid, lowest-cost ETFs to represent various asset class exposures. The allocation process is rules-based at its foundation, though Ray has added discretionary elements over time. On the equity side, one model looks at how investors are positioned directly in equities (S&P, Nasdaq, Russell 2000 aggregate data), combined with how they're positioned in long-duration bonds. The interplay between equity and bond positioning turns out to be more predictive than looking at either in isolation.

The rebalancing approach is deliberately patient. If the target is 56% equities this week but MOOD is currently at 50%, Ray leaves it. If it drops to 42%, still 50%. Only when the signal moves outside a 10-percentage-point band does he trade. The logic is simple: anything less than 10 percentage points on the equity side doesn't move the needle over short horizons, and it keeps turnover in check. Ray is also candid about where the approach can get you in trouble, and makes the case that combining relative sentiment with trend following creates natural balance since the two strategies tend to fail at different times.

Key Takeaways

  • MOOD's signals are built on Commitments of Traders data and sentiment surveys, measuring the gap between institutional and retail positioning
  • The fund uses a 10-percentage-point band around target equity allocation to trigger rebalancing, keeping turnover low
  • Combining how investors are positioned in equities AND long-duration bonds produces better predictive power than either alone
  • Relative sentiment and trend following naturally offset each other's weaknesses, making them strong complements in the same portfolio
  • The strategy started fully rules-based but has added discretionary elements, particularly on the non-equity side

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Sam Rahman and John McNamara on Delivering Institutional-Grade Growth Investing Through Hedgeye Asset Management
metaDescription: Sam Rahman and John McNamara on how Hedgeye Asset Management's quality growth ETF bridges passive benchmark-huggers and high-risk thematic strategies.
content:
Hedgeye Asset Management brings two guests to Behind the Ticker: John McNamara, who helped build the firm's asset management division starting in 2018, and Sam Rahman, who spent more than 15 years managing public equity for Ned Johnson's family office at Fidelity. Together they walk through the Hedgeye Quality Growth ETF—a concentrated large cap strategy designed to fill the gap between overdiversified benchmark-huggers and high-volatility thematic funds—and the investment philosophy behind the portfolio construction approach.

About Sam Rahman, John McNamara, and Hedgeye Asset Management

John McNamara came to Hedgeye through the research itself. He was working at a macro long/short equity fund where Hedgeye was the largest research input, and when considering his next move he approached the firm about building a formal asset management business. The thesis was straightforward: Hedgeye had been providing institutional-grade research since Keith McCullough founded the firm in 2008, and the logical extension was to bring institutional-grade investment strategies to the same audience.

Sam Rahman brings a fundamentally different kind of pedigree. He began at Baring Asset Management in London in 1993. His early years were spent alongside Baring's global CIO, learning how bonds, equities, currencies, and commodities interact across geographies. He moved to Boston as a small cap analyst, worked his way up to head of the US team, and in 2008 received a call about managing public equity for the Fidelity family office. The client was Ned Johnson—founder of Fidelity. Rahman describes his final interview: "I literally can't talk for the first couple of minutes. But we eventually loosened up... all he wanted to talk about was different companies, different stocks, how they work. He was clearly probing to see kind of like how I think." Rahman was hired, stayed for over 15 years, and ended up running roughly 80% of Johnson's public equity assets—a tenure that reshaped how he thinks about investment time horizons.

Hedgeye itself has evolved significantly since McCullough founded the firm. What began as a macro blog now includes TV studios, podcast studios, 40-plus stock analysts covering thousands of companies globally, and a macroeconomic research framework the firm calls the quad. The quad measures the rate of change of growth and inflation across more than 50 economies, providing guidance around asset allocation overweights and underweights. Proprietary signals driven by price, volume, and volatility provide more granular buy and sell guidance across multiple durations. McNamara summarized it: the quad framework is the map; the signals are the GPS.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The Hedgeye Quality Growth ETF was built around a perceived gap in the large cap growth market. On one end, major providers offer funds with 75 or 100-plus holdings that effectively hug their benchmarks—largely quant-driven, with little meaningful active management visible in their holdings. On the other end, thematic concentrated funds take large idiosyncratic bets with minimal portfolio construction discipline. Neither product gave advisors access to a thoughtfully built, risk-aware large cap growth option with genuine institutional-grade decision-making behind it.

The fund holds 40 to 50 stocks, benchmarked against the S&P 500. The choice of benchmark was deliberate. The Russell 1000 Growth is heavily concentrated in two or three sectors. Against the S&P 500, the managers retain the ability to find ideas across technology, energy, financials, utilities, and industrials. As Rahman noted, those sectors are naturally anti-correlated, providing meaningful diversification even within a concentrated portfolio of 40 to 50 names.

Three investment themes structure the portfolio. The first is deep moat compounders—companies with durable competitive advantages that reinvest free cash flow to deepen those advantages rather than simply returning capital. The second is innovators and disrupters extending their reach through technology or business model innovation. The third is special situations, providing opportunistic exposure when specific circumstances create asymmetric potential. Rahman described these as a forcing mechanism: "These are the three types of stocks I want to focus my portfolio on... it provides a funnel or lens."

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

What Managing a Family Office Taught Sam Rahman About Investment Duration

One of the most candid threads in the conversation was Rahman's account of how the Johnson family office fundamentally changed how he thinks about managing money. Institutional portfolio managers operate within a certain timeframe—quarterly benchmarks, mandate constraints, peer comparisons. Family offices operate on a completely different horizon. "When you go to a family office, it's a whole different universe," Rahman said. "And that mindset shift was—it took me a little bit of time. It took me a couple of quarters to figure it out." He also described joining in April 2008 and watching the financial system nearly collapse five months later—debating, as he put it, whether civilization as they knew it would continue. Those experiences compounded into a longer-duration, more patient philosophy that he brings directly to this strategy.

Portfolio Construction—Building for All Sorts of Markets

Rahman is explicit that a long-only portfolio will struggle in down markets. The goal is something more durable: a portfolio that can weather varied conditions without the drawdowns thematic funds can produce. Sector diversification across naturally anti-correlated holdings provides a structural buffer. Avoiding pure-momentum names reduces left-tail risk. "To deliver a portfolio that weathers all sorts of markets and you can still invest in these big themes that everybody's talking about without having to take on tremendous risk like some of these thematic portfolios—that's kind of what we are trying to do," Rahman said. That durability frames this as a core holding rather than a satellite position.

Democratizing Institutional Investment Management

McNamara returned several times to a central idea: the same caliber of investment management reserved for ultra-high-net-worth families should be accessible to any advisor or individual investor. Rahman managed billions for one of the most powerful families in asset management. The Hedgeye Quality Growth ETF is designed to package the same rigor and portfolio construction discipline—without minimums, lockups, or institutional relationships required. McNamara framed it directly: "What we're kind of doing at Hedgeye Asset Management is building upon that goal of providing institutional grade research to the masses by providing institutional grade investment strategies to the masses."

Notable Insights

"All he wanted to talk about was different companies, different stocks, how they work... he was clearly probing to see kind of like how I think, how I think about different investment ideas, different investment themes. And luckily I got the job."

"To deliver a portfolio that weathers all sorts of markets and you can still invest in these big themes that everybody's talking about without having to take on tremendous risk like some of these thematic portfolios—that's kind of what we are trying to do."

"What we're kind of doing at Hedgeye Asset Management is building upon that goal of providing institutional grade research to the masses by providing institutional grade investment strategies to the masses."

Key Takeaways

  • Sam Rahman spent more than 15 years running roughly 80% of Ned Johnson's public equity assets, experience that directly shapes the investment philosophy and time horizon behind this strategy
  • The fund targets the gap between overdiversified benchmark-huggers and concentrated thematic funds, offering 40 to 50 stocks with real portfolio construction discipline
  • Benchmarking against the S&P 500 rather than a growth-specific index gives the managers flexibility to invest across all major sectors, not just two or three
  • Three investment themes—deep moat compounders, innovators and disrupters, and special situations—serve as a forcing mechanism to define what belongs in the portfolio
  • Hedgeye's quad framework measures rate of change of growth and inflation across 50-plus economies, providing the macro backdrop for the overall strategy
  • Sector diversification across anti-correlated industries is a deliberate construction choice providing diversification benefits even within a 40 to 50 stock portfolio

What This Means for Advisors

Advisors evaluating large cap growth face a genuine tradeoff: low-cost passive products with minimal active management or high-conviction thematic funds with elevated concentration risk. The Hedgeye Quality Growth ETF positions itself as neither. For advisors who want meaningful active management run by someone with a track record managing serious institutional money, this conversation provides a useful frame. The combination of Hedgeye's macro research infrastructure and Rahman's family office experience is an unusual pairing of top-down and bottom-up investing. The risk-adjusted orientation makes it worth evaluating as a core large cap growth holding. More information is available at the Hedgeye Asset Management website.

Listen to the full conversation with Sam Rahman and John McNamara on Behind the Ticker, available wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Seth Cogswell of Running Oak: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In this episode of Behind the Ticker, Brad Roth speaks with Seth Cogswell, Portfolio Manager at Running Oak Capital, about the firm’s flagship ETF—RUN
content:
Seth Cogswell, Portfolio Manager at Running Oak Capital, brings a background in fundamental equity research and derivatives trading to RUNN, the Running Oak Efficient Growth ETF. The strategy originated in 2009 as a separately managed account built for advisors who wanted equity growth with a smoother ride through market cycles. The core idea: combine a concentrated equity portfolio with a flexible allocation to fixed income and cash, and shift between them based on conditions rather than predictions.

RUNN holds 30 to 50 U.S. large-cap stocks selected through a completely rules-based process focused on three principles: maximize earnings growth, buy at attractive valuations, and minimize downside risk. The fund treats each holding as a coin flip, a probability-weighted opportunity to deliver those qualities, rather than building around a small number of high-conviction names. Historically, the portfolio has averaged 12 to 13% earnings growth versus 6 to 7% for the S&P 500. That 6 to 7 percentage point gap is what they expect to drive performance over time. The portfolio is equal-weighted to take advantage of market noise rather than concentrating in the largest names.

What makes RUNN different from a standard equity fund is the dynamic allocation. The fund can reduce equity exposure as low as 30% or push it up to 90%, based on a rules-based framework that evaluates macro indicators, valuation metrics, and market trends. The fixed income sleeve emphasizes high credit quality and shorter durations when risk is elevated. The whole thing is managed daily, but the philosophical starting point is humble: they don't believe they can predict the future, so the strategy is built on what they know (earnings growth, valuations, downside risk) versus what they don't know.

Key Takeaways

  • RUNN holds 30 to 50 equal-weighted U.S. large-cap stocks, targeting 12 to 13% portfolio earnings growth versus 6 to 7% for the S&P 500
  • Equity allocation can range from 30% to 90% based on a rules-based framework, not manager discretion
  • The strategy originated as an SMA in 2009 and converted to a more tax-efficient ETF format
  • Every holding is treated as a probability-weighted opportunity rather than a high-conviction bet
  • The process is fully rules-based but actively managed daily, with the team adding value at the selection and reweighting stages

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Eric Lutton of Sound Income Strategies: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In this episode, Brad sits down with Eric Lutton, CIO of Sound Income Strategies, to discuss the firm’s actively managed ETF: FXED, and its unique app
content:
Eric Lutton, CIO of Sound Income Strategies, started his career trading in the pits of Chicago before moving through private banking, institutional fixed income, and the sell side helping RIAs build bond portfolios. Over 10 years ago, he connected with Dave Strand, who wanted to start an RIA. Sound Income has since grown from $30 million with one advisor to nearly $4 billion with over 100 advisors, all organic. FXED, their actively managed fixed income ETF, was built to solve two problems: making the firm's core-plus strategy available to smaller accounts, and filling the gap left by fixed income ETFs that don't combine traditional bonds with higher-yielding alternatives.

FXED targets around 70 to 75% fixed coupon instruments and layers in non-traditional income generators: BDCs, equity REITs, preferreds, and specialty bond ETFs. The selection process is bottom-up. For BDCs, Eric focuses on management quality, leverage levels, and underwriting discipline. For equity REITs, he sticks to 11 or 12 subsectors but avoids mortgage REITs because he doesn't want to bet on how well management plays the spread game. The non-traditional holdings have lower correlation to investment-grade bonds, which improves the risk-adjusted return profile versus just buying the Agg.

Eric makes the practical case clearly: if a client needs a 6% return, the Agg isn't going to get them there. FXED targets the upper 6% yield range. That higher yield means clients can commit less of their portfolio to fixed income, freeing up room for growth investments or cash buffers elsewhere. The fund stays fully invested because holders depend on that biweekly dividend. Even when Eric would love to sit in 30% cash and go play golf because conditions look bad, he can't cut the dividend. The discipline is to find the best companies available during tough periods and stay in the game.

Key Takeaways

  • FXED blends 70 to 75% fixed coupon instruments with BDCs, equity REITs, preferreds, and specialty bond ETFs for a target yield in the upper 6% range
  • Sound Income grew from $30 million to nearly $4 billion entirely through organic growth with over 100 advisors
  • Active management in fixed income still has an edge: overweight stronger names, avoid the losers. Passive indexes can't do that
  • The fund stays fully invested to maintain its biweekly dividend, even in periods where the team would prefer to hold more cash
  • Higher yield from FXED means clients can allocate less to fixed income overall, freeing portfolio space for growth or cash buffers

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Ryan Thomes on SMID Value Investing: How Hotchkis & Wiley Blends Quantitative Models with Human Judgment
metaDescription: Ryan Thomes explains how Hotchkis & Wiley's SMID value ETF uses proprietary quantitative models and seasoned analyst judgment to find undervalued stocks.
content:
Ryan Thomes, portfolio manager at Hotchkis & Wiley, joins Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker to walk through the firm's newly launched SMID Cap Diversified Value strategy—a fund that gives advisors and investors access to a bottom-up research process the firm has been refining for 20 years. The conversation covers the three-step investment process, why normalizing earnings matters more than looking at current results, and why the valuation gap between large cap growth and SMID value may be among the more interesting setups in today's equity market.

About Ryan Thomes and Hotchkis & Wiley

Ryan Thomes came to Hotchkis & Wiley through an unusual path. He was working in investment consulting at Sloum and Associates—a firm now under the Mercer umbrella—where one of his primary responsibilities was conducting due diligence on equity asset managers. Hotchkis & Wiley was one of those firms, and Thomes was impressed enough that he advocated for his clients to use them. That advocacy turned into an employment offer, and Thomes joined the firm in 2008.

Over the following 17 years, his role evolved steadily. He became co-portfolio manager on the firm's small cap diversified value strategy alongside Jud Peters—who had been the sole manager for the prior decade—and more recently helped extend that same investment process to an international small cap strategy and the SMID strategy that underpins this fund.

Hotchkis & Wiley was founded in 1980 by John Hotchkis and George Wiley. The firm today manages between $30 and $35 billion across a suite of value equity strategies and a high yield credit business. It is majority employee-owned. What distinguishes the firm structurally is a deep commitment to research: more than half of all firm headcount is dedicated solely to the research effort—analysts, research associates, and traders focused entirely on investment work. The investment team numbers 24 professionals, with average industry experience of 25 years and average firm tenure of nearly 20 years.

As Thomes put it: "I've never been the smartest person in the room in those 17 years... I'm constantly impressed by really the intellectual horsepower of the team."

Investment Strategy and Approach

The investment process behind the SMID Cap Diversified Value strategy runs through three steps: proprietary quantitative models, analyst review, and portfolio construction.

The models are not standard screening tools. Rather than assigning weighted scores to companies based on valuation metrics like price-to-earnings or price-to-book, Hotchkis & Wiley's models are designed to replicate what a firm analyst would conclude from a hands-on fundamental review. The central concept is normal earnings power—what a company should earn in a midcycle equilibrium environment, not based on current margins or returns on capital. Those margins and returns tend to revert toward normal levels over time, while market prices typically reflect current earnings. The gap between those two figures is where the opportunity lives. The models also make accounting adjustments to more accurately capture the true cash economics of a business.

The second step is where the firm considers its primary competitive advantage to reside. Analysts review model outputs, override any element based on their subjective fundamental view of the industry and the business, and those adjustments are saved back into the database for future model runs. This means institutional knowledge compounds into the model over time rather than being lost between cycles.

Portfolio construction produces a diversified portfolio of 150 to 200 holdings—currently closer to 150—representing roughly 10% of the eligible universe. The weighting structure is tiered: approximately 50 names at 1%, 50 at 0.6%, and 50 at 0.4%. Active share runs in the mid-to-high 80s. A major rebalance runs twice per year and takes six to eight weeks; minor rebalances occur at least monthly to trade back toward target weights and capture volatility-driven opportunities. A firm-wide fundamental risk rating system scores each holding on three pillars—business quality, balance sheet strength, and corporate governance—on a scale of one to five, with better scores permitting the team to pay higher valuations.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

Why Smaller and Midcap Stocks Are the Target Universe

The SMID segment is intentional target territory for this process. Smaller and midcap stocks attract less Wall Street coverage and less buy-side attention, which creates the mispricings a research-intensive process can exploit. The universe also contains a large number of companies the firm would not want to own—negative earners, highly leveraged businesses, managements that aren't shareholder-friendly—which makes exclusion as valuable as selection. Thomes was direct: "Active management is of course about what you own but it's also about what you don't own because we are compared to passive indexes... these out-of-favor, underfollowed stocks just present themselves in greater magnitude in these small and mid segments of the equity markets."

The Valuation Gap Between Large Cap Growth and SMID Value

Large cap growth has returned roughly 350% over the past decade—approximately 15 to 17% annualized. The SMID value benchmark, by contrast, has returned around 100% over the same period. Thomes acknowledged that some of that gap reflects legitimate earnings growth from the largest technology companies. But a meaningful share reflects multiple expansion that has pushed valuations well above historical norms. The Russell 1000 Growth currently trades near 30 times forward earnings; its 20-year median is 20 times. The SMID value benchmark trades at or near its 20-year median. The implication, in Thomes's words: "If you believe that a valuation reversion is more likely than not—we would be in that camp—I think that would be good for small and midcaps and also good for value relative to growth."

How Human Judgment Gets Embedded in a Systematic Process

One of the more nuanced points in the conversation was how analyst adjustments interact with the quantitative models over time. The models serve primarily as a research prioritization tool—a way to surface candidates efficiently across a large universe. The real value is in the analyst adjustments: overrides grounded in deep fundamental experience, saved back into the system so that the next model run incorporates them automatically. Thomes framed it plainly: "The models are almost a research prioritization tool for our analyst team." That compounding of institutional judgment over two decades is the core asset the firm is building around the process.

Notable Insights

"Our models are basically designed to try and replicate what a Hotchkis and Wiley analyst would do if they were analyzing this company by hand."

"If you look at the Russell 1000 growth, it's like 30 times forward earnings while its 20-year median is 20 times. So well above its sort of long-term average and well above other parts of the US equity market."

"I've never been the smartest person in the room in those 17 years... I'm constantly impressed by really the intellectual horsepower of the team."

Key Takeaways

  • The process models normal earnings power in a midcycle environment, not current earnings—capturing reversion potential that market prices typically miss
  • Analyst adjustments are stored in the database and incorporated into future model runs, compounding institutional knowledge over time
  • A fundamental risk rating system across business quality, balance sheet strength, and corporate governance directly determines the valuation the firm is willing to pay
  • Tiered weighting concentrates roughly half the portfolio in the top 50 highest-conviction names, with progressively smaller weights for the remaining holdings
  • The SMID value benchmark trades near its 20-year forward earnings median, while large cap growth trades roughly 50% above its long-term average
  • With more than half of firm headcount dedicated to research and average team tenure of nearly 20 years, the accumulated knowledge base is the primary competitive asset

What This Means for Advisors

Advisors whose clients hold significant large cap growth exposure have captured substantial valuation expansion over the past decade—and that expansion is not guaranteed to continue. Adding a diversified SMID value allocation with high active share and a research-intensive bottom-up process provides exposure to a part of the market where relative valuations are more compelling on a historical basis. For advisors looking to simplify a lineup, Thomes suggested pairing this strategy with a SMID growth manager as a logical balance. And while the ETF itself is newly launched, the underlying small cap diversified value process has a 20-year track record that gives advisors meaningful performance history to evaluate. More information on the firm and its strategies is available at the Hotchkis & Wiley website.

Listen to the full conversation with Ryan Thomes on Behind the Ticker, available wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Kirk McDonald on Argent Capital: Why Midcap Is the Forgotten Asset Class
metaDescription: Kirk McDonald of Argent Capital on targeting enduring midcap businesses with a 25-factor model, concentrated portfolio, and 20% annual turnover.
content:
Kirk McDonald wanted to do two things with his career: fly airplanes in the Air Force and manage money. He accomplished both. After a decade piloting the C-5 Galaxy—the largest aircraft in the Air Force—he made his way into quantitative research and eventually to Argent Capital Management, where he has spent 14 years refining a strategy that blends systematic screening with fundamental research to find what the firm calls enduring businesses. The Argent Midcap ETF, launched to extend the firm's 24-year SMA track record to a broader audience, is the product of that work.

About Kirk McDonald and Argent Capital

Argent Capital was founded in 1998 by a financial adviser who wanted to build an investment manager that delivered both strong investment performance and genuinely attentive client service—a combination he found was rarely available from the outside managers he used in his own practice. The firm now runs five strategies spanning the market-cap spectrum from large to small cap, but every portfolio manager and research analyst is looking for the same type of company: an enduring business with growing cash flows, a durable competitive advantage, and a management team that allocates capital well and returns excess to shareholders through dividend growth and buybacks.

For the first 24 years, Argent operated exclusively as an SMA manager, requiring minimum investments of $100,000 to $250,000. The ETF launch was driven partly by a democratization thesis—the ETF structure allows any investor to access the same strategy, eliminates internal capital gains on trading within the portfolio, and carries lower operating costs than a mutual fund. McDonald oversees the midcap strategy, which he manages using a combination of the Argent Alpha Model, a proprietary 25-factor quantitative screen he built, and deep-dive fundamental research on individual companies.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The Argent Midcap ETF runs a concentrated portfolio of 40 to 50 securities—43 at the time of recording—with annual turnover of roughly 20%. That combination is deliberate. McDonald's view is that genuinely enduring businesses are rare, and building a portfolio of 150 midcap names would dilute the conviction that drives the strategy's returns. Historically, 80 to 90% of the portfolio's alpha has come from stock selection, not sector or factor positioning. Staying concentrated forces the team to be rigorous about every position and to understand each business deeply before committing capital.

The Argent Alpha Model assigns scores across 25 fundamental factors, each designed to identify characteristics of enduring businesses. One factor McDonald described in detail: "unexpected cash flow from operations," which flags companies growing their cash flow per unit of sales faster than their own historical rate. The model's factor weights update dynamically each month, though the changes are small enough that scores don't shift rapidly. The model functions as a check on fundamental analysis—McDonald looks for alignment between what the quantitative screen says and what the deep-dive research finds. When they disagree, that disagreement is itself informative.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

The Enduring Business: Copart as a Case Study

McDonald walked through Copart in detail as the clearest illustration of what Argent looks for. Copart started as a salvage auto auction business in Sacramento, California, where totaled cars were auctioned in open fields regardless of weather. In 1998, the founder's son-in-law proposed putting the auctions online. The result wasn't just more convenience—it opened a regional business to a worldwide buyer audience and drove prices up through broader competition. Higher prices attracted more supply from insurance companies, which Copart locked in through volume contracts. The firm then systematically acquired real estate close to major metropolitan areas, building a network of storage sites that competitors cannot recreate. That combination of continued technology investment and irreplaceable real estate has sustained Copart's competitive position as cars increasingly get totaled for electronics-related repair costs that make them uneconomical to fix. Simple business, durable advantage—that's the Argent template.

Risk Management from the Flight Deck

McDonald drew an explicit parallel between his Air Force training and how he manages portfolio risk. In aviation, risk management starts at mission planning—before anything moves. At Argent, every name considered for the portfolio goes through a risk assessment before it's added, evaluated against both the Argent risk model and the Bloomberg risk model. Position-level exposures and overall portfolio beta are kept within tight ranges, which matters particularly given how concentrated the fund is. McDonald also described a tactical approach for cycle transitions: at market inflection points—where the strategy typically underperforms for two to three months—he trims relative winners and adds to underperforming high-conviction positions, using the disruption to reset cost bases on names he intends to hold for years.

Rebalancing Mechanics: The Hog Analogy

McDonald offered an unusually clear description of how the portfolio turns over. Current holdings are like pigs at a trough eating as fast as they can grow—the long-term, high-conviction positions doing what enduring businesses do. Behind them is a group of younger, hungrier names that have been researched and are waiting for a spot. When one of the existing holdings rises to the top of the risk management process—flagged because its growth characteristics have peaked or its quantitative score has deteriorated—it gets pushed aside and a new name moves in. Monthly rescreening and dynamic factor updates keep the watch list current, so when a sale signal emerges, a replacement is already well understood. That discipline is what holds turnover to 20% annually even with a concentrated 40-to-50 stock portfolio.

Notable Insights

"I'm incredibly fortunate in that the only two things I wanted to do professionally were fly airplanes in the Air Force and be a portfolio manager. And I've been able to do both of those things."

"Risk management was invented for aviation and so we started the risk management process when we were planning a mission. We do the exact same thing with the Argent Midcap strategy."

"Midcap stocks are about 25% of the US market cap, but they're only 11% weighted to people's portfolios. So that's really how we position it: don't forget about this asset class that can give your clients the best performance."

Key Takeaways

  • Argent Capital has managed midcap SMAs for 24 years; the ETF extends the same strategy to any investor with added tax efficiency through the ETF structure.
  • The portfolio holds 40 to 50 concentrated positions with roughly 20% annual turnover, drawing 80–90% of alpha from stock selection.
  • The Argent Alpha Model uses 25 fundamental factors to identify enduring businesses; weights update monthly and inform but don't override the deep-dive fundamental research process.
  • The strategy typically underperforms at market cycle inflection points for two to three months, then resumes outperformance—McDonald uses those periods tactically to add to high-conviction names at better prices.
  • Midcap stocks represent approximately 25% of US market cap but only 11% of typical advisor portfolio allocations, creating a persistent structural underweight for advisors to address.
  • Risk is managed continuously through both the in-house Argent risk model and the Bloomberg risk model, with tight limits on factor exposures and overall portfolio beta relative to the Russell Midcap benchmark.

What This Means for Advisors

The positioning argument for midcap is straightforward: if this segment of the market represents roughly a quarter of US equity market cap but only a fraction of typical advisor portfolio allocations, there is a structural underweight worth addressing. McDonald argues that midcap stocks have historically been the best-performing group within the US domestic equity asset class over full market cycles, making the underallocation a compounding cost over time—and one that's easy to fix.

For advisors evaluating active equity strategies specifically, the Argent approach offers a clear edge to evaluate: a proprietary quantitative screen aligned with deep fundamental research, running a concentrated portfolio with low turnover. That is a different risk/reward profile than a diversified active fund or a factor ETF. It will underperform at cycle turns, and the concentration creates tracking error. But if the stock selection discipline holds—and the 24-year SMA track record suggests the philosophy is durable—the concentrated bet on enduring businesses has the potential to generate meaningful long-term outperformance relative to a benchmark-tracking midcap allocation. For advisors who have been overweight large-cap growth and underweight the middle of the market, this is a timely conversation.

To hear the full conversation between Brad Roth and Kirk McDonald, including their discussion of Copart and Fortive, how Argent builds and manages the watch list, and why the cycle-turn underperformance is a feature rather than a bug, listen to this episode of Behind the Ticker wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Rob Thummel of Tortoise Capital: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In this episode of Behind the Ticker, Brad Roth sits down with Rob Thummel, Senior Portfolio Manager at Tortoise Capital Advisors, to dive into the fi
content:
Rob Thummel has spent decades in the energy sector, starting as a teenager working in gas stations and oilfields before helping lead Tortoise Capital Advisors, one of the most focused energy investment firms in the country. TPZ, the Tortoise Essential Energy Fund, recently transitioned from a closed-end fund to an active ETF. In this conversation, Rob makes the case that energy is no longer just about oil. Natural gas and electricity are central to powering data centers and AI infrastructure, and TPZ is positioned to capture that convergence.

The portfolio maintains an 86% active share, which means it looks very different from the S&P Energy Index or XLE. TPZ typically holds 80 to 90% equities and 10 to 20% fixed income, with option overlays to generate income and reduce volatility. The approach combines top-down macro analysis with bottom-up fundamental research, and the team has the flexibility to move quickly as opportunities shift within energy. Rob points to the AI buildout as a genuine growth catalyst for the sector: there is no AI without energy infrastructure. That's on top of the income energy already provides. The combination of stable income plus a secular growth driver is what gets Rob excited about the setup.

Performance has backed the thesis. TPZ had a one-year return over 40% heading into this conversation. Rob attributes that to the energy sector being under-invested and under-appreciated for years going back to 2020, combined with Tortoise's ability to identify where the puck is going and get there before the broader market catches up. The fund also benefits from its closed-end fund conversion, which eliminated the discount that had plagued shareholders and consolidated into a single, more scalable active ETF.

Key Takeaways

  • TPZ carries an 86% active share with 80 to 90% equities, 10 to 20% fixed income, and option overlays for income and volatility reduction
  • The energy-AI convergence is the growth thesis: data centers and AI infrastructure require massive energy investment, creating a secular tailwind
  • The closed-end fund to ETF conversion eliminated the NAV discount and created a more efficient vehicle
  • One-year return exceeded 40%, driven by years of energy sector underinvestment and Tortoise's forward-looking positioning
  • Current dividend yield around 5%, positioning TPZ as both a total return and income play

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Bob Elliot on the HFGM ETF: Strategy, Process, and What Sets It Apart
metaDescription: In a recent episode of Behind the Ticker, Bob Elliott, co-founder and Chief Investment Officer of Unlimited, discussed the firm’s mission to democrati
content:
Bob Elliott, co-founder and CIO of Unlimited, spent nearly 15 years at Bridgewater Associates creating investment strategies across asset classes before launching his own firm. Unlimited's mission is to bring Vanguard-style indexing to hedge funds: diversify across managers, cut fees dramatically, and deliver it through liquid ETFs. This episode focused on HFGM, the firm's global macro product, which offers 2X exposure to the global macro hedge fund strategy at 95 basis points.

Global macro is one of the most diversifying hedge fund styles because it operates across currencies, commodities, rates, and equities with historically low correlation to traditional 60/40 portfolios. HFGM's machine learning process infers the positioning of roughly 500 macro hedge fund managers in near-real-time using public market data and return streams. The system is fully systematic with daily updates and weekly average rebalancing. It uses futures contracts for efficiency, allowing both long and short exposure across global markets while benefiting from tax-friendly ETF structuring.

Bob is deliberate about avoiding the "black box" label. The machine learning is grounded in intuitive, transparent modeling. Hedge fund managers can't instantaneously shift positions because of transaction costs, so you can infer their positioning from the path of returns and current market action. It's scaling the same logic any investor might use to reverse-engineer a manager's trades, just with far greater accuracy and breadth. He positions HFGM and the broader suite as a shift from 60/40 to 50/30/20, where 20% goes to alternatives. No K-1s, no paperwork, no LP structures. Just an ETF.

Key Takeaways

  • HFGM replicates global macro hedge fund positioning at 2X exposure for 95 basis points, roughly one-tenth of traditional 2-and-20 fees
  • Machine learning infers positioning of ~500 macro managers in near-real-time using the path-dependent nature of hedge fund trading
  • Fully systematic with daily updates, weekly rebalancing, and futures-based execution for both long and short exposure
  • No K-1s, no paperwork, no lockups. Hedge fund exposure in a liquid, tax-efficient ETF wrapper
  • Bob advocates the 50/30/20 framework: equities, fixed income, alternatives, with HFGM as the entry point for the alternatives sleeve

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Brad Neuman on the INVN ETF: Strategy, Process, and What Sets It Apart
metaDescription: In a recent episode of Behind the Ticker, Brad Neuman, Director of Market Strategy and Portfolio Manager at Alger, joined the show to discuss the firm
content:
Brad Neuman, Director of Market Strategy and Portfolio Manager at Alger, has 25 years of experience spanning bottom-up and top-down roles on both the buy and sell sides. Alger has been in the growth investing business since 1964, built on a philosophy they call "positive dynamic change," which is really about identifying growth amid disruption. INVN, the Alger Russell Innovation ETF, marks a shift for the firm: instead of their traditional bottom-up, actively managed approach, this fund uses a systematic process designed to isolate innovation as an investable factor.

The methodology is surprisingly clean. Start with the Russell 1000. Remove the bottom third ranked by free cash flow margin to filter out early-stage or inefficient businesses. From the remaining universe, select the top 50 companies based on R&D spending relative to enterprise value. Equal-weight them. Reconstitute quarterly. That's it. Brad calls the resulting portfolio "HIPP" stocks: Highly Innovative, but Prudently Priced. The average R&D-to-market-value in the portfolio runs about 6%. Companies like Apple don't make the cut, not because they aren't innovative, but because their innovation is already fully visible and priced in. Their R&D is massive in absolute terms but tiny relative to their $3+ trillion market cap.

The result is a mid-cap core exposure with a high-90s active share against the S&P 500, Nasdaq, and Russell 1000 Growth. It doesn't overlap with the usual suspects. Low turnover because R&D investment relative to market value is a slow-moving signal. And the valuation profile sits significantly below traditional growth indices. Brad positions INVN as a replacement for passive or active mid-cap strategies that solves for overconcentration in large-cap tech and inflated multiples.

Key Takeaways

  • INVN selects the top 50 companies from the Russell 1000 by R&D-to-enterprise-value after filtering out the bottom third by free cash flow margin
  • Equal-weighted, reconstituted quarterly, with active share in the high 90s against all major growth benchmarks
  • Apple, Microsoft, and other mega-caps don't qualify because their R&D isn't undervalued relative to their massive market caps
  • Innovation is treated as a slow-moving factor, keeping turnover low and the portfolio stable across quarters
  • The fund lands as a mid-cap core exposure with a valuation profile well below traditional growth indices

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Wayne Penello of NextGen EMP: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of Behind the Ticker, Wayne Penello, founder of NextGen EMP, shared the fascinating journey that led him to launch the NextGen Eff
content:
Wayne Penello spent 40 years as a commodity trader, including a decade on the floor of the New York Mercantile Exchange, before building a patented risk management system that earned his clients over $13 billion in hedging profits. That methodology became a Forbes book called Risk as an Asset. After selling his firm, he turned his attention to equities, frustrated by the industry's reliance on diversification as the only risk management tool. The result is EMPB, the NextGen Efficient Market Portfolio Plus ETF, a long/short equity fund designed to actively manage systemic market risk.

The core idea is counterintuitive: rather than trying to predict market direction or time the best 90 days, EMPB focuses on avoiding the worst periods. Wayne's proprietary algorithm (he calls it a "foggy ball" because it's imperfect but effective) identifies the weakest sectors and shorts them to dampen volatility. The fund holds about 16 sector or thematic ETFs, balancing long and short exposures with a target Sharpe ratio above 2 and maximum drawdown under 10%. It rebalances monthly and is fully systematic, removing emotional decision-making from the process.

A key part of Wayne's mission was accessibility. He built EMPB as an ETF rather than a private fund so anyone with $25 could access hedge-fund-caliber risk management. The advertised expense ratio is 2.21%, but Wayne argues the effective net cost is closer to 1% or less when you account for the offsetting interest income from short positions and dividends on long holdings. He positions it as a core equity solution rather than an alternatives sleeve, appealing both to younger investors seeking growth with controlled risk and retirees who can't afford major drawdowns.

Key Takeaways

  • EMPB is a long/short ETF that shorts the weakest market sectors to manage systemic risk, targeting a Sharpe ratio above 2 and max drawdown under 10%
  • Built by a career commodity trader whose patented risk management system generated $13 billion in hedging profits for clients
  • The strategy avoids the worst market periods rather than trying to predict the best ones
  • Effective cost is closer to 1% after accounting for short interest income and long dividends, despite the 2.21% advertised expense ratio
  • Available to anyone with $25, no accredited investor requirement. Designed as a core equity holding, not an alternatives satellite

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Rob Harvey of DFA: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of Behind the Ticker, Rob Harvey, Vice President at Dimensional Fund Advisors (DFA), shared his personal journey into asset manage
content:
Rob Harvey, Vice President at Dimensional Fund Advisors, first encountered DFA while managing external asset managers at Cisco Systems. Their emphasis on evidence-based investing and refusal to make market predictions stood out immediately. After attending a Dimensional conference where Eugene Fama and Ken French presented, he was convinced enough to join the firm and relocate to Austin. In this conversation, Rob walks through DFA's investment philosophy and their International Core Equity ETF, DFAI.

DFA's approach is built on academic research around factors like size, value, and profitability, applied systematically across all portfolios. They're disciplined about vetting new research before incorporating it. Profitability wasn't added until 2012, years after the original academic work, because it had to meet a high bar for persistence across time periods and markets. The portfolios are managed actively on a daily basis, which gives them flexibility that traditional index funds don't have. When defense stocks are ripping in Europe, for example, DFA can hold them if they meet the quality and factor criteria rather than being forced to wait for an index rebalance.

DFAI is a market-wide international equity strategy with low tracking error that lightly tilts toward small size, value, and profitability premiums. It includes a broader set of securities than standard international index funds, particularly micro-caps, and uses real-time momentum screens to avoid value traps. Rob positions it as a step up from passive international indexing without sacrificing diversification or cost efficiency. He's direct about setting expectations: if value got crushed versus growth last year, DFA's value strategy should have underperformed. That consistency in approach is a feature, not a bug.

Key Takeaways

  • DFAI tilts toward size, value, and profitability premiums with low tracking error, designed as a core international equity holding
  • DFA manages portfolios actively on a daily basis, giving flexibility that traditional index rebalancing can't match
  • New factors require years of vetting before incorporation. Profitability was added in 2012 after meeting a high bar for persistence
  • Broader security coverage than standard international indexes, including micro-caps, with momentum screens to avoid value traps
  • Consistency means underperforming when your factors are out of favor. DFA views that as a feature of disciplined investing

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Clark Allen on Horizon Investments: How Box Spreads Are Rewriting the Rules on Cash Management
metaDescription: Clark Allen of Horizon Investments on how box spreads deliver T-bill-plus returns in an ETF wrapper that removes taxable income for advisors.
content:
Clark Allen came to Horizon Investments by way of public accounting, institutional asset management at an insurance company, and a stint at a multi-billion-dollar single family office—a path that gave him an appreciation for both institutional rigor and the emotional reality of individual clients. As head of ETFs at Horizon, he's now taking that background into a crowded product space with a specific thesis: financial engineering can close gaps in advisor model portfolios that traditional fixed-income products leave open. The centerpiece of that argument is the Horizon Landmark ETF, a cash management strategy built around box spreads.

About Clark Allen and Horizon Investments

Horizon Investments traces its roots to a wealth manager founded around 1995. The firm pivoted into model portfolios and strategist services as that business scaled, then expanded into nine mutual funds and a series of active ETFs subadvised for First Trust. Today Horizon describes itself as a solutions provider rather than a traditional asset manager—a distinction Allen returns to repeatedly in the conversation. The firm builds around the advisor relationship and the end client's financial plan, not around pushing individual products.

Horizon also runs an OCIO business, a custom SMA and tax-managed portfolio capability, and proprietary in-house technology designed to connect investment products directly to financial plans. Allen was promoted to head of ETFs after leading quantitative research and portfolio management, bringing that background to bear on the firm's first two ETF launches. At the time of recording, Horizon had filed for seven additional ETFs with more filings planned for later in the year, operating through its own trust structure with a dedicated trading subadvisor handling ETF-specific execution—in-kind processes, authorized participant relationships, and exchange logistics that differ meaningfully from managing mutual funds or SMAs.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The Horizon Landmark ETF is designed to serve one specific function in Horizon's model portfolios: the liquidity bucket in a distribution portfolio. The firm runs equity-overweight distribution models that require a dedicated spending reserve, allowing the equity allocation to run through volatility without clients needing to liquidate at the wrong time. For that reserve to work, it needs to deliver reliable, liquid, near-cash returns without creating complications elsewhere in the portfolio.

Box spreads—a combination of options trades executed through the listed market—allow the fund to access T-bill-like returns, typically T-bill plus, without holding cash or short-term treasuries directly. The strategy targets 1-to-3-month maturities and lends, in effect, through the Options Clearing Corporation. Allen was straightforward about the counterparty risk: if the OCC were to fail, the strategy would fail alongside it—but as he put it, an OCC failure would represent a systemic event far beyond the scope of this particular product decision.

The structural innovation Allen spent the most time on isn't the yield—it's what the strategy doesn't generate. Because the box spread structure doesn't produce ordinary income in the same way a treasury or money market fund would, the ETF wrapper doesn't kick off regular income distributions. For advisors managing taxable accounts, this removes the need to think about tax location for the cash sleeve. That's the gap Allen is targeting: in current practice, many advisors at large RIAs route fixed income into qualified accounts and hold equity in non-qualified accounts to optimize for taxes. A cash management strategy that doesn't generate taxable income can sit in either account type without that constraint.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

The Goals-Based Framework Behind the Product

Allen returned repeatedly to Horizon's goals-based investment philosophy, which the firm has operated on since before it became an industry talking point. The core question has always been: how do you connect a product to a plan? That means each strategy needs a clearly defined role, with some expectational certainty around what it will deliver across accumulation, preservation, and distribution phases. Allen expressed genuine frustration with the alternative—advisors spending two hours on a financial plan and then holding a set of products with no clear connection to the plan's objectives. The Horizon Landmark ETF was built explicitly to fill a defined slot in the distribution model, not as a standalone product looking for a home.

What the Box Spread Market Actually Looks Like

Allen walked through how the box spread market behaves in practice. It doesn't move meaningfully with equity volatility, though market maker behavior can cause it to tighten in stressed conditions—he noted that the market had come in a bit more recently. It does track short-term rates, which means returns are largely pinned to Fed funds, creating some tactical opportunity around Fed expectations: extending duration if the Fed is expected to cut, or staying short to roll into a rate cut environment. Allen framed the market as relatively stable and suited to its intended purpose—not a vehicle for significant additional yield, but a reliable way to capture near-cash returns with the specific structural benefit of minimized income distributions.

The Broader Implication for Fixed Income in Model Portfolios

Allen extended the thesis beyond cash management. He sees the same tax-location logic applying to aggregate bond and high-yield strategies if similar structures can be implemented in those parts of the fixed income market. The implication, if it plays out, is significant: advisors currently treat tax location as a binding constraint in portfolio construction, keeping income-generating assets in qualified accounts. If ETF structures can remove that constraint at the cash level and eventually at the bond level, it changes how model portfolios get built from the ground up. Allen acknowledged the Horizon Landmark ETF is an early step—the fund had a small asset base at launch—but framed it as the opening product in a set built around this thesis.

Notable Insights

"What if you had ETFs that didn't have to kick off that income? So this is a way to kind of play into that direction."

"We're not like a traditional ETF provider that's just talking our single product traditionally. We're really talking about a solution set and a set of ETFs that really fulfill a need for an adviser and fulfilling financial plans."

"The thing that's really annoying to me is when somebody spends an hour or two on a financial plan and then you have this convoluted set of securities and active mutual funds and somebody's trying to explain how they connect to that financial plan and it doesn't make any sense."

Key Takeaways

  • Horizon Investments operates as a solutions provider with model strategist, OCIO, SMA, and ETF businesses built around a goals-based investment philosophy.
  • The Horizon Landmark ETF uses box spreads targeting 1-to-3-month maturities to deliver T-bill-like returns without generating ordinary income distributions.
  • The absence of regular income generation means the strategy can sit in taxable accounts without the tax-location constraints that apply to conventional cash or bond allocations.
  • The strategy is designed as the liquidity bucket in Horizon's distribution model portfolios, enabling an equity-overweight approach with a reliable spending reserve alongside it.
  • Allen sees the same structural approach extending to aggregate bond and high-yield strategies as the product lineup expands beyond cash management.
  • The ETF space is moving toward outcome-oriented products designed around expectational certainty—Horizon views its ETF lineup as purpose-built for that direction.

What This Means for Advisors

The most direct application is in distribution portfolios. For advisors managing clients in or near retirement using a spend-down model, the Horizon Landmark ETF offers a cash management option that generates near-T-bill returns without income drag in taxable accounts. For advisors who currently route fixed income into qualified accounts purely for tax reasons, this is worth examining as a potential solution for the liquidity sleeve—particularly for clients with significant taxable assets who need a spending reserve that doesn't create a tax event each year.

More broadly, Allen's framing of the goals-based ETF space offers useful context for where product development is heading. Buffer strategies established that financial engineering could produce defined outcomes in equity allocations. Allen's argument is that the same logic will work through fixed income—creating cash, bond, and credit strategies that let advisors build outcome-oriented models without the tax location tradeoffs that currently complicate portfolio construction. Whether Horizon executes on that vision across its planned product set remains to be seen, but the thesis is grounded in real advisor pain points that aren't going away.

To hear the full conversation between Brad Roth and Clark Allen, including their discussion of the OCIO business, the decision to launch Horizon's own trust, and the firm's plans for future ETF filings, listen to this episode of Behind the Ticker wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Paisley Nardini of Simplify: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent special edition of Behind the Ticker recorded live at the Exchange ETF Conference, Paisley Nardini, Vice President and Client Portfolio St
content:
Paisley Nardini, Vice President and Client Portfolio Strategist at Simplify Asset Management, recorded this episode live at the Exchange ETF Conference. Her career started on a bond trading desk, evolved through PIMCO and institutional portfolio management, and landed at Simplify, where she focuses on bringing hedge-fund-caliber strategies to a broader audience. The conversation centers on CTA, Simplify's Managed Futures Strategy ETF, which launched after a 2020 SEC rule change expanded the use of derivatives in ETFs. Simplify now manages over $7 billion across 35 ETFs.

CTA focuses exclusively on commodities and interest rate futures, deliberately excluding equities and currencies to offer a cleaner source of diversification. It runs on a multi-signal model with four drivers: trend (capturing directional moves across short, medium, and long-term timeframes), mean reversion (scaling exposure when trends get extended), intermarket (adjusting when cross-asset relationships shift, like equities selling off while bonds rally), and carry (evaluating the rate curve to avoid negative carry during inversions). The model is powered by research from Altis Partners, a UK-based CTA firm, and rebalances daily.

The risk framework is simple: one constraint, a 25% margin-to-equity ratio, imposed by an outside third party. No sector caps. No position caps. That's intentional. Paisley argues that alpha in active strategies typically comes from a small subset of trades, and capping positions dilutes the best ideas. In practice, margin-to-equity has been running 12 to 14%, well below the cap. CTA had a profitable run in cocoa in 2023 because the lack of constraints allowed a high-conviction position. With negative correlation to equities and the potential for equity-like returns, the fund is increasingly used as a core diversifier in model portfolios.

Key Takeaways

  • CTA uses four signals (trend, mean reversion, intermarket, carry) across commodities and interest rate futures only, excluding equities and currencies for cleaner diversification
  • One risk constraint: 25% margin-to-equity ratio, imposed externally. No sector or position caps, allowing high-conviction trades
  • Actual margin-to-equity runs 12 to 14%, well below the 25% cap
  • Negative correlation to equities with potential for equity-like returns makes CTA a structural portfolio diversifier
  • Daily rebalancing with research powered by Altis Partners, a UK-based CTA firm

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Matt Kaufman of Calamos: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent special edition of Behind the Ticker recorded live at the Exchange ETF Conference, Matt Kaufman, Head of ETFs at Calamos Investments, join
content:
Matt Kaufman, Head of ETFs at Calamos Investments, recorded this episode live at the Exchange ETF Conference. Calamos has spent 50 years building its reputation on risk-managed strategies, originally through convertible bonds: take the upside of a stock, attach it to a bond floor. That DNA now extends to one of the most volatile asset classes around. Calamos launched the first 100% principal-protected ETFs on the S&P 500, Russell 2000, and Nasdaq 100. Now they've taken the same framework and applied it to Bitcoin.

The protected Bitcoin ETFs come in three flavors: CBOJ (100% downside protection), CBTJ (10% at risk), and CBTX (20% at risk). The structure is straightforward. Most of the portfolio goes into zero-coupon U.S. Treasuries to secure principal (about 96% for the fully protected product). The remaining yield buys a call spread on a custom Bitcoin index developed with the CBOE that tracks all 11 spot Bitcoin ETPs. The result is a defined one-year outcome period with options-based exposure to Bitcoin's price movement. At the time of recording, upside caps were running as high as 50 to 60% for the 20% floor product because Bitcoin's volatility makes the options market rich.

Matt positions these as the entry point for advisors who want Bitcoin in client portfolios but can't stomach the drawdowns. You choose how much to put at risk. The 100% protected product is essentially a Treasury note with Bitcoin upside. The 20% floor product gives you more cap but accepts some downside. Either way, you know the worst-case scenario before you buy. Calamos has the flexibility to expand into other structured products. Matt mentioned a synthetic convertible strategy on the Nasdaq 100 that has outperformed the QQQ since inception with less risk.

Key Takeaways

  • Three protected Bitcoin ETFs with different risk profiles: CBOJ (100% protected), CBTJ (10% at risk), CBTX (20% at risk)
  • Structure: zero-coupon Treasuries for principal protection, remaining yield buys a call spread on a custom CBOE Bitcoin index
  • Upside caps as high as 50 to 60% on the 20% floor product due to Bitcoin's high implied volatility
  • Defined one-year outcome periods. You know the worst-case scenario before you buy
  • Built on 50 years of convertible bond and risk management DNA at Calamos

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Jeff Cullen of Schafer Cullen: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of Behind the Ticker, Jeff Cullen, Managing Director at Schafer Cullen Capital Management, sat down to discuss the firm’s first ET
content:
