Jeff Cullen, Managing Director at Schafer Cullen Capital Management, brings over 30 years of experience across mutual funds, SMAs, and ETFs. Schafer Cullen manages over $24 billion and has spent four decades as a value shop with a strong dividend focus. DIVP, the Cullen Enhanced Equity Income ETF, is their first ETF and a natural extension of a strategy they've been running successfully in SMA form for years.

DIVP holds 30 to 40 large-cap value stocks with dividend yields typically above 3%. The differentiator is a selective covered call overlay: short-dated, out-of-the-money calls (2 to 4% out, expiring in two weeks to one month) written on 25 to 40% of portfolio holdings. The calls are written on individual stocks, not an index, and chosen based on volatility conditions and the fundamental outlook for each name. Writing short-dated options on a fraction of the portfolio preserves most of the upside participation while adding meaningful income. The SMA version of the strategy has historically yielded between 7.2% and 8.3%, with more than half from qualified dividend income.

The stock selection process starts with valuation relative to a company's own history. Jeff and his team aren't comparing a utility's PE to a tech stock's. They're looking for names trading below their historical norms with earnings growth, dividend growth funded by that earnings growth (not financial engineering or debt), and sector diversification so the options overlay isn't concentrated in just two or three industries. The fund will lag in sharp bull runs or low-vol periods. Where it performs best is in sideways or volatile markets where premium harvesting and dividend yield do the heavy lifting.

Key Takeaways

  • 30 to 40 large-cap value stocks with 3%+ dividend yields, plus a selective covered call overlay on 25 to 40% of positions
  • Short-dated calls (2 to 4 weeks, 2 to 4% out-of-the-money) on individual stocks, not an index, preserving most upside participation
  • Historical SMA yield of 7.2 to 8.3%, with more than half from qualified dividend income
  • Stock selection based on PE relative to a company's own history, not cross-sector comparisons
  • Best suited for sideways or volatile markets. Will underperform in sharp rallies or low-volatility environments

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Dan Petersen of New York Life: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of Behind the Ticker, Dan Petersen, Head of Product Management at New York Life Investments, discussed the firm’s international eq
content:
Dan Petersen, Head of Product Management at New York Life Investments, has over two decades in the industry spanning financial advisory and ETF distribution. He played a key role in building IndexIQ before its acquisition by New York Life in 2015. In this conversation, Dan walks through HFXI, the NYLI FTSE International Equity Currency Neutral ETF, which takes a different approach to a problem most international investors just accept: currency risk.

Historically, if you bought international equities, you took full currency exposure by default. Fully hedged products emerged as an alternative, but timing when to hedge and when not to proved difficult. HFXI splits the difference by hedging exactly 50% of currency exposure using monthly-rolled forward contracts while maintaining full equity market exposure. Dan's argument is that currency can contribute or detract 600 to 1,000 basis points annually. Hedging half takes the extreme outcomes off the table while still allowing participation when foreign currencies strengthen.

The strategy is deliberately passive on the equity side. Market-cap weighted, no factor tilts, no smart beta. The only active decision is the 50% currency hedge. Dan frames this as the most neutral approach possible for investors who don't want to make a currency call but recognize that defaulting to full exposure carries real risk. It works as a standalone replacement for unhedged international ETFs or paired with traditional international funds to customize the currency exposure level. He also notes that international equities currently trade at historically attractive PE discounts to U.S. markets, which makes the allocation timing interesting independent of the currency question.

Key Takeaways

  • HFXI hedges exactly 50% of foreign currency exposure using monthly-rolled forward contracts, providing a middle ground between full and zero hedging
  • Currency fluctuations can swing international equity returns by 600 to 1,000 basis points annually
  • Full equity market exposure is maintained with market-cap weighting and no factor tilts
  • Can serve as a standalone international allocation or paired with unhedged funds to customize currency exposure
  • International equities currently trade at historically attractive valuation discounts to U.S. markets

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Kirsten Chang of VettaFi: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of Behind the Ticker, Kirsten Chang, Director of Editorial and Content at VettaFi, shared insights into her journey from CNBC to h
content:
Kirsten Chang, Director of Editorial and Content at VettaFi, spent over a decade at CNBC working on Squawk Box, covering markets from the NYSE floor with Bob Pisani, and helping launch ETF Edge. She moved to VettaFi to build out content and editorial in the ETF space. This episode is a preview of the Exchange ETF Conference, the largest ETF industry event of the year, heading to Las Vegas.

VettaFi itself is more than a conference organizer. It operates as an index provider, data analytics firm, and digital distribution company that bridges the gap between advisors and ETF issuers. With over 4,000 ETFs on the market, the research and educational content they produce helps advisors cut through the noise on flows, trends, and new product launches. The Exchange conference is where that all comes together in person, with sessions covering private credit ETFs, hedge-fund-led multi-asset strategies, the active ETF explosion, crypto, AI, and international equities.

Kirsten highlighted several standout panels: Bridgewater's all-weather ETF that just launched (the first to bring that hedge fund strategy to retail), an international equity session with Research Affiliates, and Eric Balchunas from Bloomberg leading the crypto discussion. Keynotes include David Kelly and Ian Bremmer. There's also a session called Money Lines and Markets drawing parallels between investing and sports betting with a quantitative analysis angle. The conference brings together the biggest issuers and hundreds of financial advisors, and Kirsten makes the case that what resonates at Exchange tends to drive advisor interest for the rest of the year.

Key Takeaways

  • Exchange ETF Conference is the largest annual ETF industry event, with sessions spanning private credit, active ETFs, crypto, AI, and international equities
  • VettaFi operates as an index provider, data analytics firm, and digital distribution company connecting advisors with ETF issuers
  • Notable panels: Bridgewater's new all-weather ETF launch, Research Affiliates on international equity, Bloomberg's Eric Balchunas on crypto
  • Record-breaking fixed income ETF flows and the active ETF explosion are top themes heading into the conference
  • Kirsten's CNBC experience spanning Squawk Box, ETF Edge, and NYSE floor coverage gives her a unique perspective on the industry's growth

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Adam Patti of VistaShares: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of Behind the Ticker, Adam Patti, founder and CEO of VistaShares, discussed the launch of the VistaShares Artificial Intelligence
content:
Adam Patti founded IndexIQ back in 2006 (one of the earliest liquid alternatives ETF issuers, later acquired by New York Life) and has been in the ETF industry since 2001 when he created the Fortune 500-based ETF with State Street. After a few years away, he partnered with John McNeil of DVX Ventures to launch VistaShares. Their first fund, AIS (the Artificial Intelligence Supercycle ETF), takes a supply-chain-driven approach to AI investing that looks nothing like the Magnificent Seven-heavy AI ETFs already on the market.

AIS starts with the bill of materials for AI data centers and semiconductors. What actually goes into building this infrastructure? GPUs, VRAMs, cooling systems, fiber optic networks. Then it identifies the companies with the most AI-driven revenue in each segment and weights them by market share within that segment. So if one cooling company has $50 million of a $100 million segment, it gets 50% of that segment's allocation. The methodology is rules-based (VistaShares has filed for a patent) with a semi-annual rebalance and an active overlay for emerging developments. The investment committee includes former Tesla president John McNeil and AI entrepreneur Sonny Madra, who help identify trends and new players before they're widely recognized.

The portfolio currently holds about 72 companies across various supply chain segments, with roughly 60% U.S.-based and the rest distributed across Taiwan, China, and Europe. Adam is clear about what AIS is and isn't: it's pure AI infrastructure exposure, not broad tech. As the AI buildout matures, VistaShares has the flexibility to expand into consumer-facing AI applications and energy. But for now, with record-breaking capex from major tech firms, the infrastructure layer is where the exponential growth is. Adam suggests a 3 to 5% allocation within a core equity strategy as a high-conviction growth satellite.

Key Takeaways

  • AIS invests in AI infrastructure through the actual supply chain: GPUs, VRAMs, cooling, fiber optics. Not the Magnificent Seven
  • Companies are weighted by market share within each supply chain segment, based on a patented rules-based methodology
  • ~72 holdings across the U.S. (60%), Taiwan, China, and Europe, with semi-annual rebalancing and an active overlay
  • Investment committee includes former Tesla president John McNeil and AI entrepreneur Sonny Madra
  • Suggested allocation: 3 to 5% as a high-conviction growth satellite within core equity

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Emma Harper of Sage Advisory: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: n a recent episode of Behind the Ticker, Emma Harper, a research analyst at Sage Advisory, shared insights from the firm’s 2024 ETF Stewardship Report
content:
Emma Harper, a research analyst at Sage Advisory, joined the show to discuss the firm's 2024 ETF Stewardship Report. Sage manages approximately $28 billion and their annual report examines something most ETF investors don't think about: how ETF providers actually exercise the ownership rights they hold on behalf of shareholders. Proxy voting, corporate engagement, governance structures, transparency. When you buy an ETF, you're delegating those rights to the fund issuer. This report grades how well they use them.

The findings are interesting. Larger providers like BlackRock, Vanguard, and State Street tend to support management more often than smaller firms. Large managers reported supporting management about 91% of the time versus 81 to 82% for mid-size and smaller managers. There's also a widening gap between active and passive ETF managers: active managers generally show stronger stewardship practices, while passive managers lean more on third-party proxy advisors and do less direct engagement with portfolio companies. One of the more notable trends in the 2024 report is declining transparency. Early versions of the report saw growing disclosures from asset managers, but recent years show a more cautious, legalistic approach, likely in response to regulatory scrutiny and political pressure around ESG.

Emma makes the practical case for why this matters: if ETF providers don't exercise their stewardship responsibilities diligently, there's a real risk of harming long-term value through corporate governance failures. Issues like AI governance, cybersecurity, and executive compensation are increasingly showing up on proxy ballots. Understanding whether your ETF issuer has a dedicated stewardship team and clearly defined voting policies is part of the fiduciary due diligence that advisors should be doing.

Key Takeaways

  • Large ETF providers support management ~91% of the time versus ~81-82% for smaller firms, raising questions about shareholder advocacy
  • Active ETF managers show stronger stewardship practices than passive managers, who rely more on third-party proxy advisors
  • Transparency in stewardship reporting is declining as providers respond to regulatory and political pressure
  • AI governance, cybersecurity, and executive compensation are increasingly appearing on proxy ballots
  • Sage manages $28 billion and has published this annual report tracking ETF provider stewardship practices across the industry

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Rob Arnott on Research Affiliates: Why the Index Deletion Trade Is the Best Bet Nobody's Making
metaDescription: Rob Arnott on how the Research Affiliates deletions strategy exploits index construction flaws, generating 74x returns vs. the S&P's 30x over 32 years.
content:
Rob Arnott has spent nearly 50 years systematically finding the places where conventional indexing creates mispricing. The founder of Research Affiliates made his reputation building Fundamental Index—a strategy that weights stocks by the size of their business rather than their market cap—and licensing it to the tune of $158 billion in assets across Schwab, Invesco, Pimco, and ProShares. But this conversation on Behind the Ticker was about something newer and arguably more compelling: buying the stocks that major indexes throw out at exactly the wrong time.

About Rob Arnott and Research Affiliates

Arnott founded Research Affiliates in 2002 while still running First Quadrant, managing both firms in parallel for two years before the research firm demanded his full attention. The business model was unusual from the start: Research Affiliates doesn't manage money directly. Instead, it creates strategies and licenses them to asset managers worldwide. That structure has allowed a relatively small team to oversee methodologies tied to roughly $158 billion in assets, with Schwab as the largest single relationship.

The firm is best known for Fundamental Index, which now underlies approximately $75 billion in ETFs across its licensing partners. The core idea: rather than weighting stocks by how expensive they are, weight them by the actual size of the underlying business. The result is a strategy with a persistent value tilt and a rebalancing discipline that buys what's out of favor and trims what's gotten expensive—automatically, without any discretionary call. That methodology has a 20-year live track record that, as Arnott noted on the show, has held up even through a prolonged stretch of value underperformance relative to growth.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The Research Affiliates Deletions strategy—the focus of this episode—builds on a structural insight Arnott has been working through for decades. Standard capitalization-weighted indexes have a mechanical flaw: they add stocks when their prices are high and drop them when prices have fallen. On average, stocks added to indexes are trading at roughly twice the market multiple. Stocks that get deleted are trading at half. Neither decision is made on the merits of the underlying business—only on the market's current opinion of its price.

Arnott's response was to build a strategy that does the opposite. The Research Affiliates Deletions ETF targets stocks that have recently fallen out of the top 500 or top 1,000 companies by market cap—constructed mechanically rather than relying on S&P or Russell's proprietary lists, which would require significant licensing fees. Any stock that drops more than 10% outside those thresholds qualifies as a deletion. The strategy then filters out the 20% most likely to be value traps using multiple quality metrics—profitability, debt-to-equity ratios, and debt coverage ratios—and holds the remaining names for five years. If a stock gets readded to a major index before the five-year window closes, it exits the deletions portfolio.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

The Data Behind the Deletion Trade

Arnott walked through the full historical record. From 1991 through 2023, stocks deleted from the S&P 500 or Russell 1000 and held for five years would have returned 74 times an investor's money. The S&P 500 itself returned just under 30 times over the same period. The outperformance is front-loaded: deleted stocks beat the market by an average of 7% per year in the first couple of years, tapering to roughly 3% per year by year five—totaling approximately 2,800 basis points of cumulative outperformance over the holding window. The anomaly is also self-reinforcing: years with more deletions produce even higher returns, because those are the years when indexes are most aggressively flushing out unloved stocks at the widest discounts.

Lumen and Dillard's: Proof in the Portfolio

Two holdings came up as concrete illustrations. Lumen Technologies was removed from the S&P 500 in March 2023 and from the Russell index in June 2023. Last year the stock rose more than 500% after Meta signed a deal to work with Lumen on its niche within AI infrastructure. Lumen is now expected to be readded to the Russell index and will exit the deletions portfolio when that happens. Dillard's, a regional department store chain, has been added to and removed from the Russell 1000 four times in 30 years—the index repeatedly buying it back when the business was performing and dropping it when the stock cratered. Since the last deletion in 2017, Dillard's is up more than 500%. The index has executed a buy-high, sell-low cycle on the same company four separate times.

Against Data Mining: How Research Affiliates Builds Strategies

Arnott was direct about the failure mode he works hardest to avoid. His view is that the quantitative community is largely addicted to data mining—building models that maximize historical backtests rather than testing theories. His framework inverts this: start with a commonsense theory, then use data to test it. The deletions strategy originated from an observation about how indexes behave. The data was used to confirm and calibrate that observation, not to generate it. Arnott also noted that he began running the deletions strategy in his personal account in 2018 using actual S&P deletions, which gave him direct experience with the strategy's behavior before formalizing it at scale with ETF Architect.

Notable Insights

"Companies that get kicked out of indexes, some of them go on to achieve great failure. But you only need a few to snap back and show their mettle."

"We are very strict about adhering to a Bayesian method where you start with a theory, you use the data to test the theory, and you don't use the data to tweak and improve the backtest. That's easy and it leads to products that are rubbish."

"Stocks deleted from S&P or Russell held for five years would have given you 74 times your money. What if you just put your money in the S&P? You would have had a little under 30 times your money. That's wonderful, but it's not as good as 74 times your money."

Key Takeaways

  • Research Affiliates licenses strategies to Schwab, Invesco, Pimco, and others, with $158 billion in assets tied to its methodologies worldwide.
  • The deletions strategy targets stocks removed from the top 500 or top 1,000 by market cap, filtering out likely value traps and holding survivors for five years.
  • Historical testing from 1991–2023 shows deleted stocks held five years returned 74 times capital versus under 30 times for the S&P 500.
  • The strategy currently holds approximately 150 small-cap value stocks, equal-weighted and rebalanced annually, with estimated capacity around $5 billion.
  • Value relative to growth is near its cheapest level since 2020—roughly an 8.5-to-1 price-to-book ratio—which Arnott views as a meaningful tailwind for the strategy going forward.
  • The Fundamental Index methodology has a 20-year live track record, with performance advantages that come from rebalancing discipline rather than stock selection alone.

What This Means for Advisors

Arnott suggested the deletions strategy is best used as a satellite allocation—a complement to a core diversified portfolio rather than a replacement for broad market exposure. With approximately 150 small-cap value names, it is genuinely niche, and that is part of the appeal. The strategy draws its edge from a mechanical flaw in index construction that is unlikely to be arbitraged away as long as cap-weighted indexing dominates institutional asset management. For advisors already using Fundamental Index products, this extends the same rebalancing logic to its most extreme expression: buying the names the market has most aggressively abandoned, precisely when conventional wisdom says to let them go.

The current environment adds a specific overlay. Arnott argued that value relative to growth is sitting near its cheapest levels in recent history, and the deletions strategy is a direct expression of deep value at that moment. The five-year holding window and annual equal-weight rebalance give the strategy room to wait for mean reversion rather than requiring a precise timing call. Whether that view plays out depends on when and how the value/growth relationship normalizes—but the structural thesis doesn't depend on it. Deleted stocks have outperformed through multiple cycles precisely because the mispricing is driven by index mechanics, not by market sentiment.

To hear the full conversation between Brad Roth and Rob Arnott, including their discussion of rebalancing frequency, sector concentration, and how the strategy compares to standard fundamental indexing, listen to this episode of Behind the Ticker wherever you get your podcasts.

title: John Davi of Astoria Advisors: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: n a recent episode of Behind the Ticker, John Davi, founder and CIO of Astoria Advisors, discussed the firm's latest ETF launch, the Astoria US Qualit
content:
John Davi, founder and CIO of Astoria Advisors, is a frequent guest on Behind the Ticker and a quant investor with over two decades of experience. Astoria manages about $2 billion serving financial advisors, institutions, and ultra-high-net-worth individuals. GQQQ, the Astoria US Quality Kings ETF, is the firm's third listing and was built to fill a specific gap: most growth ETFs are purely market-cap weighted with no quality filter. GQQQ combines growth with quantitative quality screening so you're not just buying the biggest names, you're buying the ones with the strongest fundamentals.

The quality filter focuses on return on equity, return on assets, and return on invested capital. Unlike the Nasdaq 100, which includes all non-financial large-caps without screening, GQQQ applies those filters and extends into mid-cap territory. That's where the edge shows up. AppLovin was in the launch portfolio in October 2023, months before it was added to the Nasdaq 100, giving GQQQ early participation in that run. The portfolio targets a tech sector weight between SPY and QQQ (about 45% tech, 14% communication services, 13% consumer discretionary) but the individual names tend to be lower in market cap than what you'd find in either of those benchmarks.

The fund rebalances annually with quarterly quantitative reviews that allow adjustments when stocks significantly de-rank in quality metrics. It's not a tactical allocation product, but the reviews catch deterioration before the next full rebalance. John positions GQQQ as complementary to existing growth exposure like QQQ or Vanguard growth ETFs, offering a more refined approach that captures the quality growth segment while reducing the concentration risk that comes with mega-cap-dominated indices. Astoria's track record backs the approach: PPI (their first ETF) sits in the second percentile over three years out of 330 funds in its category.

Key Takeaways

  • GQQQ screens for quality (ROE, ROA, ROIC) within a growth universe, extending into mid-caps where passive growth indices don't reach
  • AppLovin was in the GQQQ launch portfolio months before being added to the Nasdaq 100
  • Tech sector weight targets the midpoint between SPY and QQQ, but with lower market-cap names
  • Annual rebalance with quarterly quality reviews to catch deterioration between full reconstitutions
  • Astoria's first ETF (PPI) ranks in the second percentile over three years out of 330 category funds

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Mike Loukas on Q Bull and Q Bear: Building Portfolios with Quarterly Hedged Equity
metaDescription: Mike Loukas of TrueShares explains Q Bull and Q Bear, quarterly hedged equity ETFs offering asymmetric market exposure with treasury risk as the floor.
content:
Mike Loukas, founder and CEO of TrueShares, joins Brad Roth to break down Q Bull and Q Bear, two quarterly resetting hedged equity ETFs built for advisors and model builders who want more flexible portfolio construction tools. Loukas founded TrueShares in 2019 with a specific goal: bring institutional-grade hedging strategies into the ETF wrapper. These two products are the latest expression of that idea, and the conversation goes deep on how they work, why the quarterly reset matters, and where they fit in a real portfolio.

TrueShares launched its first ETFs in 2020, right before COVID triggered a moratorium on new listings. The lineup has since grown to 21 ETFs across all three major exchanges. The product mix includes active fundamental strategies, an AI and deep learning strategy concentrated around 20-21 holdings, a 12-product suite of structured outcome and buffered ETFs, income and yield products, and now Q Bull and Q Bear. Loukas is clear that TrueShares is not a white-label shop. Sub-advised products exist in the lineup but are expected to account for roughly 25% of future launches, with 75% managed internally.

Q Bull and Q Bear are mirror strategies that reset every quarter. Both use the same construction: client capital goes into treasury securities, and the anticipated yield funds the purchase of out-of-the-money options. Calls for Q Bull, puts for Q Bear. If the market rises more than approximately 5% in a quarter, Q Bull starts participating with increasing convexity. If the market falls more than 5%, Q Bear generates positive returns. In between, both strategies deliver the treasury return. That 5% threshold isn't fixed. It can move in a 4% to 6% range based on treasury yields and options pricing at each reset, with higher yields generally enabling more option coverage.

The quarterly cadence is a deliberate design choice. Most buffered and defined-outcome products reset annually, which creates extended periods where an investor's current value diverges significantly from expected return as options decay over 12 months. A shorter reset period closes that gap. Loukas also walked through the portfolio construction applications: in an all-weather model, owning both Q Bull and Q Bear together addresses both tails of the return distribution without requiring a directional market call. The base exposure is treasury risk, not equity risk. For managers who rotate into cash when their signals turn bearish, Q Bull is a more efficient alternative to sitting in money markets. If the market recovers, Q Bull participates. If not, you're holding treasury risk anyway.

Key Takeaways

  • Q Bull and Q Bear use treasury yield to fund out-of-the-money options, giving investors asymmetric market participation past a roughly 5% quarterly threshold without leverage or short positions.
  • The quarterly reset creates tighter correlation between product behavior and actual market moves compared to annual defined-outcome products, where options decay causes persistent divergence.
  • Both products can be held simultaneously to create positive convexity on both tails without requiring a directional view or taking outright short exposure.
  • The 5% threshold varies between 4% and 6% depending on treasury yields and options pricing at each reset.
  • For tactical managers who use cash as their risk-off instrument, Q Bull maintains the treasury downside floor while preserving upside participation if the market recovers before signals change.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Catherine LeGraw of GMO: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of Behind the Ticker, Catherine LeGraw, asset allocation strategist at GMO, shared insights into the firm’s approach to value inve
content:
Catherine LeGraw, asset allocation strategist at GMO, joins Brad Roth to talk about GMOV, the firm's U.S. value ETF, and why GMO's approach to value investing looks nothing like a passive value index. LeGraw joined GMO in 2013 after working at BlackRock and Barclays Global Investors, and she brings a top-down perspective to asset allocation that drives how the firm identifies opportunities across markets. This conversation is worth the time for anyone who thinks they already have value covered in their portfolio.

What makes GMOV different from something like VTV comes down to three things. First, GMO takes a top-down approach, looking for entire groups of mispriced stocks rather than picking individual names from the bottom up. Right now, the fund focuses on the cheapest 20% of the market. Second, GMO doesn't rely on standard accounting data. They restate financials to reflect true underlying value, treating things like R&D expenses as investments rather than operating costs. Third, they build forward-looking projections tailored to each company's specific characteristics instead of relying on backward-looking multiples. The result is a portfolio of about 150 names where sector weightings are determined by where the value actually is, not by market cap constraints.

One example LeGraw highlighted: GMOV has heavy exposure to financials because GMO is finding genuinely cheap banks trading at attractive valuations relative to fundamentals. But the fund has zero exposure to utilities, even though utilities are a typical overweight in value portfolios. If they're not finding companies that meet their definition of cheap, they just don't hold the sector. That kind of conviction is rare in a value fund. LeGraw also makes the case for GMOI, their international value ETF, arguing that international equities are even cheaper than U.S. value and carry a potential tailwind from currency valuation shifts.

Key Takeaways

  • GMOV focuses on the cheapest 20% of the U.S. market using restated financials and forward-looking projections, not standard accounting ratios.
  • The fund holds about 150 names with sector weights driven entirely by valuation attractiveness. Zero utilities exposure despite being a typical value overweight.
  • GMO uses proprietary red flag screening that examines accounting metrics, management behavior, and market signals to avoid value traps.
  • LeGraw argues value is priced for significant outperformance, with deep value at historically attractive levels.
  • GMOI (international value) may be an even bigger opportunity given the relative cheapness of international equities and potential currency tailwinds.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Kevin Carter on India: The Perfect Emerging Market and the Case for Indian Internet Stocks
metaDescription: Kevin Carter of EMQQ Global makes the case for India as the perfect emerging market and explains the India Stack most investors don't know.
content:
Kevin Carter, founder of EMQQ Global, has spent over 20 years focused on emerging market consumption themes. First through China's e-commerce buildout, now through India's. He joins Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker to lay out the full thesis behind the firm's India-focused internet ETF: why India qualifies, why now, and what most investors still don't understand about the country's digital infrastructure. Carter spent the month of November traveling through India before this recording, and the on-the-ground observations make this one of the more substantive emerging market conversations the show has aired.

Carter's career started in January 1992. He developed a fractional shares brokerage concept in 1999, sold it to E*Trade in 2000, then co-founded Active Index Advisors, an early pioneer in what the industry now calls direct indexing, which was sold to Natixis in 2004. His focus shifted entirely to emerging markets after connecting with Google employees who wanted China exposure, eventually founding EMQQ as a broad emerging market internet strategy covering roughly 45 countries. The India-only internet strategy launched once Indian internet companies began going public in sufficient numbers to make an index viable.

The India thesis is structural, not speculative. India is now the world's largest population, having passed China roughly two years before this recording. It has approximately 750 million people under 30 and GDP growing at roughly 6.5% annually. Excluding China, India is larger than every other emerging market combined. PM Modi's first 10 years of governance cut bureaucratic red tape, doubled physical infrastructure (roads, power grids, connectivity), and created the conditions for India's startup ecosystem to explode from roughly 500 companies seven years ago to approximately 120,000 at the time of recording.

But the piece most investors miss entirely is the India Stack. Carter describes it as a cheat code that no other country has. The foundation is Aadhaar, a national biometric identity program launched in 2010 and led by Nandan Nilekani, co-founder of Infosys. It's a voluntary system that gives every enrolled person a unique 12-digit ID with fingerprint and iris scan authentication. That layer enables identity verification at scale for near-zero cost, which underpins everything from fintech to e-commerce to government services delivery. Carter's point is direct: this infrastructure gives India's digital economy a structural durability that simple macro narratives about population and growth rates don't capture.

Key Takeaways

  • India holds the world's largest population, is the youngest major economy by demographic profile, and is growing GDP at roughly 6.5% annually.
  • Excluding China, India is larger than every other emerging market combined, with Indonesia as a distant second.
  • India's startup ecosystem expanded from roughly 500 companies seven years ago to 120,000, with talent increasingly sourced from second- and third-tier cities.
  • The India Stack, anchored by Aadhaar's biometric identity system, gives India digital public infrastructure that no other country has replicated.
  • Carter's investment case is early relative to where China's internet sector was at a comparable stage. The companies that will dominate India's digital economy over the next 20 years are forming now.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: David Dziekanski- Quantify Funds: ETF Strategy and Market Insights
metaDescription: In a recent episode of Behind the Ticker, David Dziekanski, founder and CEO of Quantify Funds, introduced his firm’s first ETF, the STKD Bitcoin and G
content:
David Dziekanski, founder and CEO of Quantify Funds, joins Brad Roth to introduce BTGD, the STKD Bitcoin and Gold ETF. Dziekanski spent 17 years in the ETF industry, including 11 years as a partner at Tidal Financial Group, before launching Quantify Funds. BTGD is a "stacked" ETF that gives you $1 of Bitcoin exposure and $1 of gold exposure for every $1 invested. It's a fundamentally different way to do leverage, and the conversation explains why that distinction matters.

The idea behind stacking two different assets instead of leveraging the same one is simple: you avoid the path dependency and decay problems that plague traditional 2x products. Bitcoin and gold are both scarcity assets. Gold mines at about 1.75% per year, Bitcoin at about 0.86%, while developed nations print 7-9% of currencies in circulation annually. Combining them in one vehicle means the higher volatility of Bitcoin gets balanced by gold's stability. Historical data shows gold performed well during past crypto winters, providing real diversification when you needed it most.

The fund uses a combination of futures and exchange-traded products, targeting a 5-7% rebalance drift to minimize trading costs while maintaining the 50/50 allocation. Dziekanski positions BTGD as a debasement hedge or a high-volatility alternative in portfolios, with suggested allocations of 2-5%. The real bet, as he put it, isn't what Bitcoin is correlated to today but what it will trend towards correlation-wise in the future. For advisors trying to get crypto exposure past compliance, these stacked products don't come with the daily-use leverage warnings that make traditional leveraged ETFs a compliance headache.

Key Takeaways

  • BTGD provides $1 of Bitcoin and $1 of gold exposure per $1 invested, creating leverage without the path dependency and decay of traditional 2x products.
  • Bitcoin mines at 0.86% annually, gold at 1.75%, versus 7-9% currency printing by developed nations. The core thesis is a bet on currency debasement.
  • The fund targets 5-7% rebalance drift and uses futures and ETPs for efficient exposure.
  • Gold's stability during crypto winters provides real diversification benefit within the stacked structure.
  • Suggested portfolio allocation is 2-5%, positioned as either a debasement hedge or high-volatility alternative.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Ai in Finance on the AI ETF: Strategy, Process, and What Sets It Apart
metaDescription: In a special edition of Behind the Ticker recorded at the UT CATT 2024 Global Analytics Conference, host Brad Roth moderated a panel discussion explor
company: UT CATT 2024 Global Analytics Conference - Kyle Wiggs, Suhir Holla, Tal Schwartz
content:
In a special edition of Behind the Ticker recorded live at the UT CATT 2024 Global Analytics Conference, Brad Roth moderated a panel on the future of AI in finance. The panelists were Kyle Wiggs (co-founder and CEO of UX Wealth Partners), Sudhir Holla (founder and CEO of MyStock DNA), and Tal Schwartz (founder of AI Funds). Three very different perspectives on how AI is actually being applied to portfolio management right now, not in theory.

Kyle Wiggs focused on the operational side. UX Wealth Partners bridges the gap between AI-driven strategies and practical implementation for advisors, handling trading, billing, and reporting across multiple custodians and account structures. His framing was "man and machine" working together. AI's role should complement human advisors, not replace them. The value is in scaling sophisticated strategies that would be impossible to execute manually across hundreds of accounts.

Sudhir Holla brought a different angle with MyStock DNA's philosophy of "winning by not losing." Their AI engine, Darwin, focuses on mitigating downside risk while managing the volumes of data and emotional biases that derail human decision-making. He introduced the concept of an "emotional risk frontier," suggesting AI could help create individualized portfolios tailored to each investor's actual emotional tolerance for market volatility. His analogy of AI as a self-driving car for portfolios was effective: the technology handles complex conditions while optimizing for safety.

Tal Schwartz made the most direct case for AI replacing human active management. His AI engine, BAILA (Bayesian AI Learning Algorithm), acts as both a macro strategist and portfolio optimizer, identifying market environments and adapting to changing conditions. His argument: AI can process more data, eliminate emotional bias, and make more sophisticated predictions than human managers. Statistically, active managers already underperform benchmarks. AI doesn't have to be perfect; it just has to be better than human managers, and Schwartz argues it already is.

Key Takeaways

  • Three distinct AI applications in finance: operational scaling (UX Wealth), downside risk management (MyStock DNA), and active portfolio management replacement (AI Funds).
  • The "emotional risk frontier" concept suggests AI can build truly personalized portfolios based on individual tolerance for volatility, not just standard risk questionnaires.
  • BAILA (Bayesian AI Learning Algorithm) functions as both macro strategist and portfolio optimizer, adapting to changing market environments automatically.
  • All three panelists agreed the biggest near-term opportunity for AI is on the active management side, where human managers already statistically underperform.
  • Key challenges remain: regulatory hurdles, biases in training data, and the need for human oversight during the transition period.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Al Chu of American Beacon GLG Natural Resources ETF: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of “Behind the Ticker,” Al Chu, portfolio manager at Man GLG, discussed his role managing the American Beacon GLG Natural Resource
company: American Beacon GLG Natural Resources ETF - MGNR
content:
Al Chu, portfolio manager at Man GLG, joins Brad Roth to talk about the American Beacon GLG Natural Resources ETF (MGNR). Chu has over two decades of experience in natural resources investing, from managing strategies at BNY Mellon to running positions at various hedge funds. With Man Group's backing as a UK-listed alpha-focused alternative manager, Chu's expertise is now available through an active ETF structure. This conversation is a good listen for anyone who thinks commodity exposure is just a broad beta play.

MGNR is a long-only equity product. No derivatives, no leverage. What makes it different from most commodity ETFs is that it owns the companies, not the futures. Oil producers, oilfield services, mining companies. The strategy is fully active and follows a structured process: identify favorable commodity cycles first, drill into specific subsectors second, and then pick companies with strong fundamentals, management, and assets. The portfolio holds 40 to 50 names with individual positions capped at 5% and sector concentrations limited to manage risk. Roughly 80-90% of the holdings are OECD listed (U.S., Canada, Australia, Western Europe), with very little emerging market and no frontier market exposure. Chu is explicit about why: he wants the risk concentrated in commodities, not in sovereign or political risk.

Chu pushes back on the idea that commodities all move together. Within the natural resources space, subsectors can behave very differently. Iron ore was down over 50% at one point while other areas were performing well. That dispersion is where the alpha opportunity lives, and it's why an active approach matters here. MGNR is positioned as an alternative to passive commodity exposure, providing real asset exposure with low correlation to traditional equities and bonds, plus the operational and financial upside that comes from owning the actual companies producing and extracting resources.

Key Takeaways

  • MGNR is a long-only equity fund holding 40-50 natural resources companies. No derivatives, no leverage, no futures.
  • The investment process is structured: commodity cycle identification first, subsector analysis second, individual company selection third.
  • Individual positions are capped at 5% with sector concentration limits. 80-90% OECD-listed holdings to avoid sovereign risk.
  • Commodity subsectors can diverge sharply (iron ore down 50%+ while others held up), which is where the active management adds value.
  • MGNR offers low correlation to traditional equities and bonds as a real asset allocation with the added benefit of company-level operational upside.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Raymond Holst on 351 Exchanges: The Tax Mechanism Behind ETF Seeding
metaDescription: Tax attorney Raymond Holst of Practus breaks down 351 exchanges for ETF seeding—what advisors and asset managers need to know.
content:
Raymond Holst is a tax attorney at Practus and one of the most experienced practitioners working in ETF formation law. On this episode of Behind the Ticker, he breaks down 351 exchanges from first principles: what the Internal Revenue Code provision actually says, what it requires from asset managers and their clients, and where the practical complexity really lives. Practus has completed close to 100 of these transactions, and that depth of execution surfaces edge cases that don't appear in the first dozen. If you're an advisor or manager considering moving SMA assets into an ETF, this is the episode to listen to.

Section 351 is straightforward in concept: when property is contributed to a new corporation in exchange for shares, the contributor doesn't recognize the built-in gain at the time of transfer. The basis carries over. In the ETF context, this means an asset manager who built a track record in separately managed accounts can move those assets into an ETF without generating a tax bill for clients. Two requirements must be met. First, a control test: transferors must own at least 80% of the new entity after the transfer (in practice, it's typically 100%). Second, a diversification test called the 25/50 rule: no single security can be more than 25% of an account, and the five largest can't exceed 50%. This test applies at the individual account level, not in aggregate.

Holst walked through a simple example of where this trips managers up. If a client holds only Apple at 50% and Nvidia at 50%, that account fails immediately. A strategy that looks diversified across all clients may still have individual accounts that can't qualify. The legal documentation itself is relatively simple. The real heavy lift is the operational coordination: every tax lot from every participating account must move correctly between custodians and the new fund administrator. Holding periods, basis information, and proper allocation to ETF shares all need to be hardwired before the trade executes. On the regulatory side, the SEC's minimum review period for an ETF prospectus is 75 days.

The 351 exchange addresses the seeding event specifically. The ETF's ongoing tax efficiency is a separate structural advantage under section 852(b)(6), which generally allows ETFs to rebalance without triggering a taxable event for shareholders. An SMA manager doing the same trade has to sell, recognize gain, and reinvest after-tax proceeds. For advisors with clients who have held a strategy for years with significant embedded gains, the ability to move into the ETF wrapper without a forced liquidation event is genuinely meaningful.

Key Takeaways

  • A 351 exchange lets managers seed an ETF from existing SMAs or private funds without triggering a taxable event for participating clients.
  • Two requirements: the control test (typically 100% participation) and the 25/50 diversification rule applied at the individual account level, not the aggregate book.
  • The heaviest operational lift is pre-trade coordination of tax lot information across custodians, not the legal paperwork itself.
  • SEC minimum review period for an ETF prospectus is 75 days. Build that into your launch timeline.
  • The ETF's ongoing tax efficiency (rebalancing without recognizing gain) is a separate structural advantage that operates after the fund is live.
  • Practus has completed close to 100 of these transactions. Working with experienced practitioners significantly reduces execution risk on the edge cases.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Garrett Stevens & Rich Malinowski of Exchange Traded Concepts: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of “Behind the Ticker,” Garrett Stevens and Rich Malinowski from Exchange Traded Concepts (ETC) discussed the firm’s unique positi
content:
Garrett Stevens and Rich Malinowski from Exchange Traded Concepts (ETC) join Brad Roth to discuss what it takes to actually launch and run an ETF. ETC was the first white-label ETF issuer and has been operating for 13 years, launching over 100 ETFs with a combined $7.5 billion in AUM. They provide everything from regulatory filings and portfolio management to marketing and website development. This is a behind-the-scenes conversation about the ETF industry's plumbing, and it's useful for anyone thinking about bringing a product to market.

Stevens highlighted a growing trend: wealth management firms launching their own ETFs based on existing strategies. These advisor-driven ETFs are often not marketed publicly. They're used as internal tools to enhance the client experience and differentiate the firm. The shift toward active is real. About 75% of new ETF launches are now active, moving away from the thematic and passive products that dominated early growth. But active ETFs require a longer runway because they depend on building a performance track record to attract investors, unlike thematic passive products that can gain traction on the idea alone.

Malinowski covered mutual fund-to-ETF conversions and semi-transparent ETF structures. Conversions have slowed because intermediaries and gatekeepers aren't fans of the process, and questions remain about who's holding the products post-conversion. Semi-transparent ETFs face hurdles with market makers and custodians who are less inclined to work with limited visibility products. But Malinowski expects gradual acceptance. The potential game-changer is the recent share class applications with the SEC to copy the Vanguard model, which could let current mutual funds bolt on ETF capabilities without a full conversion. Both Stevens and Malinowski stressed the importance of coming to market with real assets. Target a $30 million breakeven point and build strong relationships with service providers before launch, not after.

Key Takeaways

  • ETC has launched 100+ ETFs over 13 years as the first white-label ETF issuer, with $7.5B in combined AUM.
  • About 75% of new ETF launches are now active strategies, a major shift from the early thematic/passive growth phase.
  • Wealth management firms are increasingly launching proprietary ETFs as internal portfolio tools, not public products.
  • Mutual fund-to-ETF conversions have slowed due to intermediary resistance, but SEC share class applications could change the game.
  • Launch with real assets. Target $30M breakeven. Build service provider relationships before launch day.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Brett Eichenberger of Cohen & Co.: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of “Behind the Ticker,” Brett Eichenberger from Cohen & Company discussed the intricacies of auditing in the ETF and mutual fund i
content:
Brett Eichenberger from Cohen & Company joins Brad Roth for a conversation about what actually goes into auditing ETFs and mutual funds. Eichenberger has spent 19 years at Cohen & Company, which now audits over 1,800 registered funds, making it the fourth-largest auditor of registered funds in the U.S. and second-largest in the ETF space. This isn't a typical Behind the Ticker episode about investment strategy. It's about the infrastructure that keeps the whole thing running, and it's surprisingly relevant for anyone launching or managing a fund.

The key differences between auditing ETFs and mutual funds come down to a few things. ETFs report returns on both NAV and market value bases because they trade on secondary markets. Capital activity in ETFs happens through authorized participants and in-kind creation units, not individual shareholders buying and selling at NAV. That creates unique audit considerations around the in-kind exchange of securities. Valuation is the big focus area. Complex portfolios with derivatives, foreign securities, or illiquid assets require careful work to ensure every position is accurately valued. For ETFs specifically, maintaining diversification standards and testing qualified income are critical for regulatory compliance.

Eichenberger also discussed how Cohen & Company is moving toward 100% transaction testing through technology and automation, moving beyond traditional sampling methods. This means better accuracy and faster processing. For issuers choosing an auditor, the operational details matter: some shops do their own trading, some outsource it to sub-advisors, and the audit approach needs to account for that structure. Cohen handles the full range, from straightforward equity ETFs to complex products with derivatives, futures, and controlled foreign corporation blockers for commodity exposure.

Key Takeaways

  • Cohen & Company audits 1,800+ registered funds, making it the #4 fund auditor in the U.S. and #2 in ETFs specifically.
  • ETF audits differ from mutual fund audits primarily in dual NAV/market value reporting, in-kind creation unit capital activity, and qualified income testing.
  • Valuation accuracy is the primary audit focus, especially for complex portfolios with derivatives, foreign securities, or illiquid assets.
  • Technology is pushing fund auditing toward 100% transaction testing, replacing traditional sampling methods with full-coverage data-driven approaches.
  • ETF issuers need auditors who understand their specific operational structure, including whether trading is done internally or through sub-advisors.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Petra Bakosova of Hull adaptive: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of “Behind the Ticker,” Petra Bakosova from Hull adaptive discussed the unique approach behind the Hull adaptive Asset Allocation
content:
Petra Bakosova from Hull Tactical joins Brad Roth to discuss HTUS, the Hull Tactical Asset Allocation ETF. Bakosova has a background in applied mathematics and previously worked at proprietary trading firms before joining Hull Tactical at its inception. The fund's approach draws directly from founder Blair Hull's experience as both a blackjack card counter and a legendary options trader: make frequent, proportional bets based on your advantage, and never forget risk management. HTUS is essentially a hedge fund strategy in an ETF wrapper, and the conversation gets into the mechanics of how it actually works.

HTUS aims to outperform the S&P 500 without exceeding its volatility. The strategy collects approximately 40 publicly available indicators organized into four categories: macroeconomic factors, fundamentals, technical anomalies, and sentiment. Those indicators feed into quantitative models that generate a daily signal. If models don't show a strong signal either way, the fund sits at 100% S&P 500 exposure. When signals emerge, exposure adjusts. The typical range is 50% to 150%, though the fund has flexibility to go flat or short. Daily rebalancing keeps the fund responsive, and the actual instruments are straightforward: S&P 500 ETFs, E-mini futures, and SPX options ranging from zero DTE to about one month out.

The sentiment indicators are interesting. The fund tracks sources like MarketPsych, Halbert, and Ned Davis, monitoring social media and news sentiment alongside the macro and technical data. Each indicator contributes different insights across different time horizons, and the diversification across indicator types is deliberate. Bakosova emphasized that an investment committee reviews the optimized weights against current positioning before making changes, so there's human oversight built into the quantitative process. For advisors, HTUS sits in the large-cap core or alternatives bucket, offering S&P 500 exposure with active risk management at ETF-level fees.

Key Takeaways

  • HTUS uses approximately 40 publicly available indicators across macro, fundamental, technical, and sentiment categories to adjust S&P 500 exposure daily.
  • Default exposure is 100% S&P 500, with a typical range of 50% to 150%. The fund can go flat or short in extreme conditions.
  • The strategy draws from Blair Hull's card-counting philosophy: size bets proportional to your advantage, and prioritize risk management above everything.
  • Instruments include S&P 500 ETFs, E-mini futures, and SPX options from zero DTE to one-month expiration.
  • An investment committee provides human oversight on top of the quantitative signal before trades execute.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Joe Benoit of Grimes & Co.: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of “Behind the Ticker,” Joe Benoit, portfolio manager at Grimes & Company, discussed the firm’s collaboration with Little Harbor A
content:
Joe Benoit, portfolio manager at Grimes & Company, joins Brad Roth to discuss RMIF, the LHA Risk Managed Income ETF. Grimes is a Massachusetts-based RIA managing over $5 billion, and RMIF came out of their partnership with Little Harbor Advisors. The strategy originated after 2008 when Grimes needed alternatives to traditional high-quality bonds in a zero-rate world. RMIF is a fully tactical, unconstrained fixed income ETF that goes where the yield is, but gets out when the environment turns.

The mechanics are straightforward. RMIF holds a concentrated portfolio of typically five ETFs, equally weighted, selected based on which fixed income sectors show positive price trends and offer the highest yield. Right now that means bank loans and short-duration high yield bonds. The strategy is momentum-based: it uses price trends, volatility assessments, and yield comparisons to determine allocations. When volatility spikes, the fund de-risks quickly, shifting to short-duration treasuries or cash. In 2022, when fixed income trends were negative everywhere, RMIF sat in cash for an extended period. Benoit is upfront about the tradeoff: when you de-risk to cash and rates are zero (as they were for most of the last 10-15 years), you're accepting zero yield to avoid losing principal. With yields where they are now, that tradeoff looks much better.

The portfolio's goal is not to trade frequently. If trends are positive in higher-yielding areas, RMIF stays put. Turnover only happens when volatility picks up enough to trigger a de-risk or when the yield profile changes enough that a different sector overtakes the current holdings. Benoit positions RMIF in two ways: as a substitute for core fixed income, or as "conditional credit" that sits alongside traditional bonds. When credit markets are calm, it's invested in higher-yielding segments. When things deteriorate, the portfolio shifts automatically. You get the income when it's safe and the protection when it's not.

Key Takeaways

  • RMIF is a fully tactical fixed income ETF that allocates to the highest-yielding sectors with positive price trends and de-risks to cash or short-duration treasuries when volatility spikes.
  • Typically holds five equally weighted fixed income ETFs, concentrated in whatever sectors are offering the best risk-adjusted yield.
  • The fund sat in 100% cash during parts of 2022 when all fixed income trends were negative.
  • Designed as either a core fixed income substitute or "conditional credit" alongside traditional bond holdings.
  • Low turnover by design. The goal is not to trade but to stay in high-yield areas as long as trends remain positive.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: David Allen of Octane Investments: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of “Behind the Ticker,” David Allen, CFA and founder of Octane Investments, discussed his background in finance and the firm’s str
content:
David Allen, CFA and founder of Octane Investments, joins Brad Roth to talk about OCTA, an all-cap value energy ETF. Allen started his career in 1992 as a trader at Merrill Lynch, where he witnessed events like the breaking of the Bank of England, and developed an early interest in geopolitics and energy markets. He launched Octane specifically as a reaction to the wave of institutional divestment from traditional energy, which he sees as creating a structural supply of cheap, profitable companies that the market is ignoring for non-economic reasons.

Allen's thesis centers on what he calls the "carbon risk premium." When large pension funds and institutional investors wholesale exit traditional energy, the remaining companies get cheaper despite having strong fundamentals. OCTA is built to capture that. The portfolio construction uses a simple, transparent decision tree. Start with about 150 energy names. Screen the top third by forward P/E ratio. Then test for balance sheet strength through debt-to-equity ratios. Then check for capital return: buybacks, dividends, debt elimination. The final portfolio holds roughly 30 names across energy subsectors including refiners, tankers, and producers. Only developed market (OECD) companies make the cut because Allen doesn't want overnight sovereign risk blowing up the portfolio.

The fund rebalances weekly and limits exposure to any single company to manage concentration risk. Allen frames OCTA as addressing a structural underweight: energy represents less than 4% of the S&P 500, which means most portfolios already have significantly less energy exposure than the economy would suggest. He deliberately avoided a black box approach because he can't sell something he didn't design and can't explain. Every holding passes the same decision tree that any value investor would recognize. That transparency is part of the pitch.

Key Takeaways

  • OCTA targets undervalued energy companies benefiting from the "carbon risk premium" created by institutional divestment from traditional energy.
  • Portfolio construction follows a transparent decision tree: forward P/E, debt-to-equity, and capital return metrics across ~150 names to select ~30 holdings.
  • Only OECD-listed companies qualify. Allen explicitly avoids emerging market sovereign risk.
  • Weekly rebalancing with individual position limits to manage concentration risk across energy subsectors.
  • Energy is less than 4% of the S&P 500, creating a structural underweight in most portfolios that OCTA is designed to address.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Federico Brokate of 21Shares: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of “Behind the Ticker,” Federico Brokate, Head of U.S.
content:
Federico Brokate, Head of U.S. Business at 21Shares, joins Brad Roth to discuss the firm's digital asset ETFs, including ARKB (Spot Bitcoin) and CETH (Spot Ethereum). Brokate spent 10 years at BlackRock focused on U.S. ETFs before joining 21Shares, which was founded in 2016 in Zurich with a straightforward mission: make digital assets accessible to people who don't want to deal with wallets, keys, and encryption. The firm now has over 50 products in 16 global markets and has been one of the fastest-growing Bitcoin ETF issuers.

The Bitcoin ETF launch has been one of the biggest stories in the ETF industry. Around 75-80% of Bitcoin ETF assets are currently held by retail investors, which is unusual compared to the rest of capital markets. But Brokate sees institutional and wealth advisory interest building steadily. The education process with advisors follows a two-step pattern: first, make the case for why Bitcoin belongs in a portfolio at all. Second, show what happens to the risk-adjusted returns when you add even a small allocation. The volatility increase is real, but the return impact on a full-year basis often surprises advisors who assume it's purely speculative.

What sets 21Shares apart operationally is their multi-custodial model. Both ARKB and CETH use custodians like Coinbase, Anchorage, and BitGo to eliminate single points of failure. They're also the only issuer offering on-chain proof of reserves through a Chainlink partnership, so investors can independently verify what the products actually hold. That level of transparency is rare in the digital asset space. For CETH specifically, Brokate sees early adopters among crypto-native investors who were waiting for staking capabilities before entering ETF products, plus macro-oriented investors looking for growth exposure as traditional equity markets trade sideways.

Key Takeaways

  • 21Shares operates 50+ digital asset products across 16 global markets, founded in 2016 to bridge traditional finance and crypto.
  • ARKB and CETH use a multi-custodial model (Coinbase, Anchorage, BitGo) to reduce single points of failure in custody.
  • 21Shares is the only issuer offering on-chain proof of reserves via Chainlink, allowing independent verification of holdings.
  • 75-80% of Bitcoin ETF assets are held by retail investors, but institutional and advisor interest is growing steadily.
  • The advisor education process is two-step: why Bitcoin in a portfolio, then demonstrating risk-adjusted return impact of even a small allocation.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Nancy Davis of Quadratic Capital: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of “Behind the Ticker,” Nancy Davis, founder and CIO of Quadratic Capital, discussed her journey from Goldman Sachs to launching h
content:
Nancy Davis, founder and CIO of Quadratic Capital, joins Brad Roth to talk about IVOL and BNDD, two ETFs that give investors access to parts of the fixed income market that are normally reserved for institutional players. Davis spent 10 years at Goldman Sachs before founding Quadratic in 2013, starting with separate accounts and a hedge fund before entering the ETF space in 2018. The conversation gets into the specific problems with the AGG (Aggregate Bond Index) that most advisors don't think about, and why Davis built products to address them.

Here's the problem with the AGG that Davis identified: it gives you no inflation protection, and through its mortgage holdings, you're effectively short options to homeowners. When you own a financial mortgage, you're short volatility. It's not equity vol, it's fixed income vol, and most people don't realize their core bond holding has that embedded short optionality. IVOL was designed to complement core bond holdings by fixing both problems. The portfolio is about 80% TIPS (giving you inflation protection beyond just CPI) plus long options on interest rate volatility. So when rate vol spikes, like it did in March 2020, IVOL benefits instead of suffering.

BNDD is the mirror product for deflationary environments. It provides long-duration exposure to nominal treasuries with asymmetric exposure to interest rates, without the leverage risks common in other bond products. IVOL covers the inflation side, BNDD covers the deflation side. Together they address both directions of interest rate risk. Davis made a point that stuck: the biggest asset class in the world is interest rates, and most people have never had access to trade rate volatility directly. These are access vehicles, not replacements for core bonds. The correlation profile is extremely attractive precisely because the exposure looks different from everything else in a typical portfolio.

Key Takeaways

  • IVOL is about 80% TIPS plus long options on interest rate volatility, designed to complement the AGG by adding inflation protection and fixing the embedded short optionality problem in mortgage bonds.
  • BNDD provides asymmetric long-duration exposure to nominal treasuries for deflationary scenarios, without leverage risk.
  • Most investors don't realize that owning mortgage bonds in the AGG means they're short fixed income volatility.
  • IVOL and BNDD are complementary: IVOL handles inflation risk, BNDD handles deflation risk, together covering both directions.
  • The correlation profile is attractive because these products give access to interest rate volatility, which most portfolios have never had direct exposure to.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Mike Venuto of Tidal: The ETF Masters: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of “Behind the Ticker,” Mike Venuto, co-founder and CIO of Tidal Financial Group, shared insights into the company’s approach to l
content:
Mike Venuto, co-founder and CIO of Tidal Financial Group, joins Brad Roth to talk about what it takes to build, launch, and grow ETFs at scale. Tidal manages and supports over 160 funds with more than $19 billion in assets. Venuto's background goes back to WisdomTree and Global X, and the conversation covers everything from the economics of launching a fund to the trends reshaping the industry. If you're thinking about starting an ETF, this is essentially a masterclass from someone who's done it hundreds of times.

The cost and timeline differences between going independent and using Tidal's platform are stark. Starting an ETF independently can cost hundreds of thousands of dollars and take up to nine months. Tidal can launch most ETFs for under $70,000 in less than four months. Where the firm really stands out is in complex strategies: options, derivatives, leverage. Products like Yield Max and Return Stack use sophisticated structures that require deep operational expertise. Venuto noted that everything succeeding in the current market is active and complex. The passive thematic wave has largely run its course.

Venuto shared some specific success stories. A Sharia-compliant portfolio client came to Tidal with $80 million. They now have four ETFs and mutual funds totaling roughly $750 million, built over just under four years. He also gave practical advice for managers at wirehouses like Morgan Stanley or UBS who want to launch: start with a founders ETF or flagship concentrated portfolio of your top picks, then have your existing mutual funds buy into it. It sidesteps the platform friction. On the mutual fund-to-ETF conversion trend, Venuto says it's slowed but the demand for tax-efficient ETF structures keeps growing. He's also watching Wes Gray's tax-advantaged structures at Alpha Architect as a potential mega trend, given that taxes are likely going up.

Key Takeaways

  • Tidal supports 160+ funds with $19B+ in AUM and can launch most ETFs for under $70K in less than four months.
  • Everything succeeding in the current market is active and complex. The passive thematic wave has peaked.
  • Tidal's edge is in complex strategies: options, derivatives, leverage products that require deep operational expertise.
  • For wirehouse managers: start with a founders ETF of your top picks and have your existing funds buy into it to avoid platform friction.
  • Tidal maintains a low fund closure rate by only partnering with clients who have strong, viable product ideas.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Sylvia Jablonski on the QTUM ETF: Strategy, Process, and What Sets It Apart
metaDescription: In a recent episode of “Behind the Ticker,” Sylvia Jablonski, CEO & CIO of Defiance ETFs, shared her background in finance and the innovative strategi
content:
Sylvia Jablonski, CEO and CIO of Defiance ETFs, joins Brad Roth to talk about the firm's rapid growth and their quantum computing ETF, QTUM. Jablonski came from Direxion, where she spent about a decade working on leveraged ETFs, before joining Matt Bielski to launch Defiance. In four and a half years, they've built a lineup that spans thematic, income, and leveraged products. The conversation covers how QTUM works, why Defiance moved into single-stock leveraged products, and how a small firm competes against the giants.

QTUM launched in 2018, well ahead of the quantum computing hype cycle. Defiance worked with Bluestar to build an index of the top companies in quantum computing, AI, and machine learning. The fund holds around 70 equally weighted names, rebalanced semi-annually, and targets companies deriving at least 50% of revenue from supercomputing, machine learning, and AI. That includes the obvious names like Nvidia and Google, which act as ballast, alongside smaller pure-play companies like IonQ and D-Wave that carry the higher growth potential. Jablonski's argument for quantum computing is straightforward: the ability to process data exponentially faster than traditional computers has implications for biotech, aerospace, defense, and basically any industry built on complex data.

The leveraged single-stock products are a different animal. Defiance recently filed to increase leverage on their MicroStrategy product from 1.75x to 2x. Jablonski was direct about the reasoning: investors are asking for it. The demand for spicier products in the leveraged space is real. But the broader story is how Defiance punches above its weight. They built an in-house marketing and distribution platform using AI and digital marketing, and it was so effective that other ETF companies started hiring them to do their marketing. That business eventually spun out as its own entity. It's a good example of how being nimble and listening to market demands can offset the budget advantages of larger issuers.

Key Takeaways

  • QTUM holds 70 equally weighted companies deriving 50%+ revenue from quantum computing, AI, and machine learning. Rebalanced semi-annually.
  • Launched in 2018, QTUM was ahead of the quantum computing curve and includes both large-cap tech ballast and smaller pure-play growth names.
  • Defiance filed to increase MicroStrategy leverage from 1.75x to 2x based on direct investor demand for higher-octane products.
  • The firm built an in-house AI-driven marketing platform so effective that other ETF issuers hired them to run their marketing.
  • Defiance grew from a few ETFs to a full lineup spanning thematic, income, and leveraged products in four and a half years.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: James St. Aubin of Ocean Park Asset Management: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of “Behind the Ticker,” James St.
content:
James St. Aubin, Chief Investment Officer of Ocean Park Asset Management, joins Brad Roth to talk about DUKQ, their domestic equities ETF. St. Aubin's background includes stints at Smith Barney, Wilshire, and Ibbotson Associates, and Ocean Park's core philosophy since the mid-1980s has been focused on one thing: protecting against large drawdowns, particularly for retirees or people approaching retirement. DUKQ applies that same quantitative trend-following approach to U.S. equities.

The mechanics are clear. DUKQ holds 10 to 12 ETFs when fully invested, covering broad market, small cap, mid cap, and factor-based exposures. The firm uses banded moving averages to generate buy and sell signals for each holding. When a signal triggers a sell, that position gets eliminated. If other holdings still have buy signals, capital can be redeployed there. If everything is in a sell signal, the fund goes to 100% cash. That ability to sit in cash during broad market declines is the core value proposition. St. Aubin is clear that cash is not a target allocation. They don't like being there. But having the option during a meltdown is what gives the strategy its defensive edge.

Turnover is surprisingly low, averaging around two trades per year. The research process identified which ETF exposures were most complementary to each other and pre-defined the opportunity set. Ocean Park's decision to launch ETFs alongside their existing mutual funds (eight Sierra-branded funds) and managed portfolios was driven by advisor demand. Advisors want the tax efficiency and ease of use that ETFs provide. St. Aubin positions DUKQ as flexible: it can fill the entire domestic equity bucket for risk-averse clients, or it can be a portion of the equity allocation for those who want some downside protection alongside traditional long-only holdings.

Key Takeaways

  • DUKQ holds 10-12 ETFs covering broad, small cap, mid cap, and factor-based U.S. equity exposures, with the ability to go 100% cash if all signals turn negative.
  • The strategy uses banded moving averages for buy/sell signals, with average turnover of about two trades per year.
  • When individual holdings trigger a sell, capital can redeploy to other holdings still in buy signals before moving to cash.
  • Ocean Park's philosophy since the 1980s: protect against large drawdowns, especially for clients near or in retirement.
  • DUKQ can serve as a full domestic equity allocation for conservative clients or a partial allocation for those wanting downside protection alongside long-only holdings.

Listen to the full conversation on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or YouTube.

title: Springer Harris of Get ETF'd: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In a recent episode of “Behind the Ticker,” Springer Harris, the author of Get ETF’d: An Insider’s Guide to Starting and Running an ETF, shared his ex
content:
Springer Harris, author of Get ETF'd: An Insider's Guide to Starting and Running an ETF, joins Brad Roth to share what he's learned from 14 years as a portfolio manager and COO at Teucrium. Harris entered the finance industry without knowing what an ETF was. He spent his entire career at one firm, and after years of answering the same questions from aspiring ETF creators, he wrote the definitive guide for anyone thinking about launching their own fund.

The book covers the full process from understanding industry terminology to selecting service providers, regulatory structures, and marketing strategies. Harris stresses the three-year business plan as non-negotiable. Launching the ETF is the easy part. Marketing it, building the brand, and staying committed for the long haul is where most people fail. He's direct about this: if you're not prepared to invest in distribution and grind through the early years, don't bother launching.

Harris breaks down the three paths to market: build your own ETF business from scratch, partner with a white-label provider, or create a hybrid approach. White-label is faster and cheaper but comes with tradeoffs in control and profitability. When you do less, you make less. His biggest piece of practical advice: choose your service providers as if you were choosing business partners and employees, because that's effectively what they are. The relationship matters as much as the cost. He also acknowledged that closing an ETF is sometimes necessary, and entrepreneurs should view it as a learning experience rather than a failure. The ETF industry rewards persistence and adaptability, and Harris has seen enough launches to know what separates the ones that stick from the ones that don't.

Key Takeaways

  • A three-year business plan is essential before launching an ETF. The launch itself is the easy part; distribution and marketing are where the real work begins.
