He does allow that advisors operating at the very bottom end of the quality spectrum may face displacement — but characterizes that as a small cohort within the broader professional advisory space.

Notable Insights

"AI can't do any of that. It's terrible at context. It doesn't understand nuance. It's really great at smashing numbers. So I don't see it taking the advisory business away. I see it being a tool that RAs can use to get to answers more quickly."

"There's a trading strategy which is developed by humans. We've used it before, it's made us money. We teach the machine. Then there's this refinement period that we go through where within the guardrails we place — we tell it what it's trying to do with what we give it, and then we ask it for optimization output."

"I don't think I will ever forget that iteration of the system. We gave it a name. It was TJ. And TJ basically got drunk and drove off a cliff. I'm so grateful for that because it wasn't catastrophic — and it really paved the road to where we have gotten to now."

Key Takeaways

  • The fund is a fully AI-directed low-to-high mean reversion strategy that identifies artificially oversold stocks — all decisions at the endpoint are made by the AI, not a human portfolio manager
  • Kaiju's approach starts with a human-built investment ideology that is taught to the machine, with explicit guardrails on position sizing, risk, and strategy scope — it is not a pure blackbox system
  • The ARC (AI Risk Containment) system functions as a dedicated risk management layer running separately from the core trading AI, developed after an early system generated severe losses in a single month following five strong months of returns
  • The fund is one of six ETFs operating on the NYSE floor; Citadel's involvement tightened spreads to two to three cents, directly improving execution quality for retail investors placing market orders
  • Pannell views AI as a tool for financial advisors, not a replacement — AI cannot manage the context gap between what clients express and what they actually need
  • Pure blackbox AI systems typically pair extraordinary multi-year returns with 70% to 90% drawdown periods — a risk profile Pannell considers incompatible with client capital regardless of disclosed performance

What This Means for Advisors

The fund represents something genuinely uncommon: a publicly accessible, exchange-traded vehicle where investment decisions are made entirely by artificial intelligence, built by people with deep backgrounds in AI systems rather than financial marketing. For advisors interested in alternative-style, systematic strategies with explicit risk architecture, the fund offers a transparent description of how the system works, why the guardrails exist, and what happens when they do not. Pannell's commentary on AI and the advisory business is also worth the listen on its own terms — his argument that AI will augment rather than replace professional advisors is grounded in specific system limitations he has observed firsthand, not in general reassurance. Advisors fielding client questions about AI in portfolio management will find his framework for explaining the difference between what AI does well and what it cannot do particularly useful.

The full conversation with Ryan Pannell is available on Behind the Ticker. It covers the complete architecture of the AI system, the failure of TJ and what it taught the team about risk containment, the mechanics of floor-based ETF trading and why it matters for retail execution, and Pannell's detailed views on how artificial intelligence will reshape — but not replace — the financial advisory profession.

title: Alejandro Garza on Aseam's Rules-Based Strategy: Six Factors, 27 Stocks, and a Global Developed Markets Edge
metaDescription: Alejandro Garza of Aseam joins Behind the Ticker to walk through the six-factor model behind AZTD, its 27-stock portfolio construction, and why global develo...
content:
Most fund managers claim their process blends quantitative rigor with fundamental insight. Alejandro Garza built a firm specifically to make that combination work — not as marketing language, but as operational architecture. Garza is the founder and portfolio manager at Aseam, and when he joined Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker, he walked through exactly how a six-factor proprietary model selects just 27 stocks from a universe of roughly 4,000 companies across the global developed markets small and midcap space. The methodology is specific, the construction is deliberate, and the reasoning behind every design decision is traceable to a real problem the team set out to solve.

About Alejandro Garza and Aseam

Garza launched Aseam in 2016 after more than 20 years of experience in corporate finance, investment banking, and emerging markets asset management. He spent ten years at the Ashmore Group and prior to that worked at Emerging Markets Management, one of the pioneers of EM investing, based in Washington DC. When he started the firm, he had a specific thesis: combining quantitative analysis with fundamental analysis, applied to a concentrated portfolio of 15 to 20 names, was the best way to generate client returns — and it was structurally difficult to execute inside a large institution.

The firm name carries meaning. Aseam derives from the Nahuatl language — the original tongue of the Aztec people — and translates roughly as "place of heron." Garza is the Spanish word for heron, so the name connects the firm's Mexican heritage to Alejandro's own family name. "I wanted a name that conveyed Mexican origin and tradition," he explains, noting that he also wanted to distinguish Aseam from the English-named competitors that invest in Latin America without that heritage.

Today Aseam operates two business lines: an active SMA strategy where the team conducts full fundamental due diligence and meets directly with company management, and a rules-based ETF that serves as the quantitative, index-based version of that same process. The client base is evenly split between Mexico and the United States. The ETF was cross-listed in Mexico specifically because Mexican regulations do not permit actively managed ETFs from being listed there, and because listed ETFs benefit from a significant tax advantage — roughly 10% versus 30% or more on standard investment returns.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The strategy targets global developed markets small and midcap companies — defined as those with market capitalizations between $500 million and $10 billion — across three regions: North America (the US and Canada), Western Europe (including the UK and the Nordics), and Developed Asia (Japan, Australia, and New Zealand). Within each region, the model evaluates companies across nine of the eleven standard GICS sectors. Energy is excluded because of commodity-driven volatility. Real estate is excluded because many clients already carry direct real estate exposure in their portfolios. Pharmaceuticals and biotech are excluded as an industry due to the event-driven volatility that disrupts systematic stock selection in concentrated portfolios.

From the roughly 4,000 companies in that investable universe, the model runs each through a six-factor scoring system. Every factor is scored from zero to one — one representing the highest rank — and each factor is equally weighted at one-sixth of the total score. The six factors are: free cash flow (including yield, margin, and trend), return on equity, earnings growth (including top-line revenue growth), earnings revisions, value (price-to-earnings and earnings yield), and momentum (stock price action). The model then selects the single highest-ranked stock in each of the 27 region-sector combinations — three regions times nine sectors — producing a 27-stock equally weighted portfolio.

The index rebalances monthly. Garza chose monthly rebalancing deliberately, noting it differs from the typical quarterly or annual schedule of most rules-based products. Because the strategy is always targeting the top-ranked stock in each cell of a 27-cell matrix, maintaining those factor exposures requires frequent updating.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

Why Equal Weighting Won

In designing the portfolio construction methodology, Garza's team ran multiple iterations and back-tested numerous weighting schemes. The conclusion was counterintuitive: the simplest solution performed best. An equal weight across all 27 holdings systematically diversifies risk across regions and sectors simultaneously, without introducing the optimization artifacts that more complex weighting schemes can produce. "It's funny how, especially those of us who work in quantitative finance, sometimes and often the simplest solution ends up being the most effective," Garza notes. "We tend to overthink things and want to optimize everything, but it tends to be the simpler, the better."

The Connection Between the Index and the Active Strategy

The rules-based ETF and the active SMA business are not parallel operations that happen to share a name — they feed each other. When the monthly rebalancing process surfaces a stock that has held the number-one ranking in its region-sector cell across multiple periods, that name becomes a candidate for full fundamental due diligence in the active strategy. The team then meets with management, visits the company, and conducts the deeper analysis the rules-based model cannot capture on its own. "There's a very nice communication between the index rules-based ETF strategy and our stock-picking active strategy," Garza explains. The ETF serves as a continuously updated screen; the active strategy is where the team acts on the most persistent signals from that screen.

Why the Small and Midcap Space

Garza makes a specific case for why companies in the $500 million to $10 billion range in developed markets represent a genuine opportunity. Companies of that size tend to be under-followed by sell-side analysts and under-represented in institutional portfolios relative to mega-cap names. That limited coverage creates a valuation advantage — the market has simply done less work on these names. The global developed markets filter adds another layer: most client portfolios are heavily weighted toward US large-cap equities, leaving international small and midcap companies genuinely underexplored. The unusual combination of global coverage and concentrated selection is precisely what Garza's client base had been asking for — they had US exposure covered by indexed products, but no systematic, stock-selection approach to the rest of the developed world at the smaller end of the market cap spectrum.

Notable Insights

"Combining quantitative analysis with fundamental analysis — I think the combination of those two ways of approaching markets and investments really provides for a competitive advantage when you combine those two aspects in an efficient way."

"We apply the model to the entire roughly 4,000 stock investable universe in the global developed markets small and midcap space. We select the number one stock in each region-sector combination. We end up with a 27-stock portfolio with those 27 selections."

"Aseam literally means 'place of heron.' Heron in Spanish is 'garza,' and that's my last name. So I was able to fit in the family legacy with the Mexican origin."

Key Takeaways

  • The strategy selects exactly 27 stocks — the top-ranked company in each of 27 region-sector combinations — from an investable universe of approximately 4,000 global developed markets small and midcap companies
  • The six-factor model scores free cash flow, return on equity, earnings growth, earnings revisions, valuation, and momentum — all equally weighted
  • Energy, real estate, and pharmaceuticals/biotech are excluded from the investable universe for structural reasons specific to concentrated, systematic strategies
  • Monthly rebalancing keeps factor exposures current in a highly concentrated portfolio — a deliberate departure from the quarterly and annual schedules typical of rules-based products
  • The rules-based ETF and the active SMA strategy share the same underlying model, with persistent index signals feeding candidates into the active due diligence pipeline
  • The investor base is 50% US and 50% Mexico; cross-listing in Mexico provides significant tax advantages for local investors and reflects the firm's core client base

What This Means for Advisors

The strategy gives advisors something specific: a concentrated, rules-based approach to a part of the market — global developed markets small and midcap — that most client portfolios underweight significantly. The 27-stock construction means genuine concentration and potential for meaningful differentiation from broad index exposure. For advisors who want systematic international small and midcap exposure with a clear, explainable process, Garza's methodology connects quantitative scoring directly to fundamental logic in a way that holds up in client conversations. The equal weighting across regions and sectors also provides structural diversification without relying on market-cap concentration, which is a meaningful distinction from many global ETFs that end up dominated by US mega-cap names.

The full episode with Alejandro Garza is available on Behind the Ticker. It includes a detailed walkthrough of the factor model construction, the portfolio weighting methodology, and Garza's perspective on why global developed markets small and midcap companies represent a structurally underexplored source of potential returns for advisors building globally diversified client allocations.

title: David Schulhof on MUSQ: Building the First Pure Play Music ETF
metaDescription: David Schulhof, CEO of MUSQ, joins Behind the Ticker to discuss the first pure play music ETF, its 48-company index, and the forces driving music industry gr...
content:
David Schulhof has spent his entire career inside the music industry — not as an investor looking in, but as an operator, attorney, and builder who has rolled up catalogs, run public companies, and structured deals at the highest levels of the business. That background is precisely why his exchange-traded product, MUSQ, is unlike anything else in the market. When Schulhof joined Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker, the conversation covered everything from the mechanics of the index to the forces reshaping how music is consumed, monetized, and valued globally.

About David Schulhof and MUSQ

David Schulhof is the CEO and founder of MUSQ, the first pure play music ETF. His career reads more like a Hollywood biography than a fund manager's resume. After graduating from Georgetown University early, Schulhof's first job was with Jimmy Iovine at InScope Records — the same Jimmy Iovine who would later co-found Beats with Dr. Dre and sell the company to Apple for $3 billion. Schulhof went on to earn his law degree and was recruited by Disney to serve as music attorney at Miramax and Dimension Films, a role he held for seven years. During that time he built relationships across the entire music ecosystem: songwriters, publishers, labels, and concert promoters.

From there he launched Evergreen, a private equity-backed company that raised $100 million from Lehman Brothers to roll up music publishing, merchandise, and recorded music assets. The portfolio included everything from Billman Row to Death Row Records and spanned bluegrass, hip-hop, rock, and jazz catalogs. That company was sold to KKR and became BMG Rights Management. Schulhof later served as president of two additional music companies, including Live One — a public company that owned Slacker Radio and Podcast One — and was sponsor and board member on two SPAC vehicles before launching MUSQ.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The case for MUSQ rests on a clear observation: music is as fundamental to human life as food and water. Schulhof describes the fund as a non-correlated investment that gives investors broad public exposure to the entire music ecosystem through a rules-based, quarterly-rebalancing index.

The index includes 48 companies organized across five subcategories: streaming (roughly 35% of the portfolio, including the largest global platforms and regional services), content (major labels, K-pop conglomerates, and royalty trusts), live music and ticketing (global concert and ticketing operators), equipment and technology (instrument makers and production tools), and radio and broadcasting (satellite and terrestrial operators). Forty-five percent of holdings are domestic; 55% are international — giving retail investors access to companies they would otherwise struggle to purchase directly.

To qualify for inclusion, a company must meet at least one of three criteria: generate at least 50% of its revenues from music, rank as a top five player in one of the five subcategories, or control more than 10% of global market share in any category. The minimum market cap is $100 million and the minimum daily trading volume is $500,000. Companies with volume between $200,000 and $500,000 receive a half weight. The largest names — major technology platforms that qualify as top five streaming players — are capped at 7% individually. The index rebalances quarterly.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

AI as Complement, Not Threat

Schulhof is emphatic that artificial intelligence is a complement to the music industry, not a disruption. He draws on the historical pattern every decade or so brings a technology that appears threatening: compact discs, which ultimately doubled the industry; Napster, which gave way to paid streaming platforms. AI, he argues, follows the same arc. He points to Paul McCartney's recent work using AI to produce a track featuring John Lennon's voice, and notes that approximately 120,000 new songs are uploaded to platforms every single day. Rather than undercutting the industry, that volume reinforces the value of labels as curators — someone still has to find the good ones. And when AI-generated content crosses into forgery or piracy, the takedown infrastructure already exists: a fake Drake track was removed from Spotify within hours of a label complaint.

The K-Pop Factor

One of the more surprising dimensions of the index is its substantial exposure to K-pop companies. Schulhof describes how global content creation has democratized music consumption, with every country now hosting its own streaming services and local superstars. The index includes roughly ten K-pop companies — Hybe, JYP, SM Entertainment, Cube, and others — representing significant weight in the content subcategory. He puts it plainly: every country in the world now has some music service, whether it is cloud music in China, regional platforms in Japan or Korea, or growing markets in India. That global content creation is roughly 70% of the companies on the index.

Why the Index Beats Stock Picking Here

Schulhof makes a pointed case for why passive index exposure to music outperforms individual stock selection. An investor trying to build their own music portfolio faces an impossible choice: do you buy a live music and ticketing operator because tour revenues are up, or a major label because a platinum artist is on their roster? The index spreads that selection risk across the full value chain — streaming, content, live music, equipment, and radio — in a single vehicle. For the self-directed investor, the fund also provides access to international companies that would otherwise require opening foreign custody accounts and navigating complex purchase processes.

Notable Insights

"This is a non-correlated investment and it's as important in life as food and water. I don't think a human being can go through life without listening to music in some way. You celebrate music in good times at your weddings and your proms, and you use music in unfortunate times like COVID."

"Goldman Sachs just reported that the industry was going to double to $131 billion by 2030. A lot of that is driven by the top streaming companies like Spotify, Google, and Apple."

"Here we are, ten or fifteen years later with AI. What AI is doing is giving creators new tools to create new music. It's giving an opportunity to even the most amateur musician to create music. I think the benefits far exceed the threats or the costs associated with it."

Key Takeaways

  • MUSQ is the first pure play music ETF, offering exposure to 48 companies across five subcategories of the global music ecosystem
  • The index is 45% domestic and 55% international, providing access to global music companies otherwise difficult for retail investors to purchase directly
  • Inclusion criteria require that companies derive at least 50% of revenues from music, rank as a top five player in a subcategory, or hold more than 10% global market share in any category
  • Goldman Sachs projects the music industry will double to $131 billion by 2030, driven primarily by streaming growth across global markets
  • K-pop represents a significant and growing component of the index, with roughly ten companies included across content subcategories
  • Schulhof views AI as historically consistent with prior technological disruptions — each one appeared threatening and ultimately expanded the industry

What This Means for Advisors

MUSQ presents an unusual combination: thematic growth exposure and genuine diversification within that theme. Unlike sector funds concentrated in a handful of mega-caps, the index spreads across the full music value chain — from streaming platforms to instrument manufacturers to concert promoters — in a globally diversified package. For advisors building thematic allocations, it offers non-correlated exposure to an industry Schulhof argues is structurally growing regardless of the economic cycle. The product is also accessible at any account size, from a first-time retail investor to an institutional allocator seeking a dedicated music allocation. At the time of recording, the MUSQ index was up 22% for the year, outpacing the S&P 500 — a performance narrative advisors can anchor the conversation around.

The full episode with David Schulhof is available on Behind the Ticker. It covers the complete mechanics of the index methodology, Schulhof's firsthand account of building and selling what became BMG Rights Management, and his outlook on how AI and short-form video are reshaping music consumption and monetization over the next decade.

title: Adam Eagleston on Building a Single-Ticket Alternative and the OODA Loop Approach to Portfolio Construction
metaDescription: Adam Eagleston of Formidable walks through the Formidable Fund’s 11-sleeve alternative structure, top-down regime framework, and dynamic options overlay.
content:
Adam Eagleston, chief investment officer at Formidable, joined Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker to walk through the architecture of the Formidable Fund—a single-ticket alternative ETF with eleven sleeves, a top-down and bottom-up construction process, and an options overlay designed to cut off tail risk in severe market events. He also covered why he borrowed a decision-making framework from a fighter pilot, and how to think about a strategy that worked well in 2022 and struggled through 2023 for the same reasons.

About Adam Eagleston and Formidable

Eagleston’s path was shaped early by an unlikely start. He won a stock market competition in sixth grade and built his career around markets from there—despite coming from a family of teachers with no finance background to lean on. After working at a CPA firm out of college, he became the CIO of a large trust company in Memphis, where he ran due diligence on asset managers running separate accounts across value, growth, quant, and other strategies. The experience gave him a vantage point few CIOs have: seeing what works and what does not across a full range of approaches, before ever being in the seat where daily P&L accountability is yours.

In 2012, Eagleston moved to Opus Capital in Cincinnati, a quantamental shop where he developed a small-cap dividend strategy that became foundational to the bottom-up framework he still uses today. A sub-advised ETF launched in 2018 ended when Opus restructured; Eagleston resigned, ran into Formidable CEO Will Brown while clearing out his desk, and was hired before leaving the building. He joined a few months before COVID hit. The firm’s private fund, running since 2016, handled 2020 well through hedging already in place. That performance validated the approach for a broader launch, and the Formidable Fund came to market in 2021.

Formidable is structured as a hub-and-spoke model with compliance, operations, CFO function, and investments at the hub, and six to eight financial advisors plugging in around it.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The Formidable Fund was designed as a single-ticket alternative solution. The problem it solves: advisors building out an alternatives allocation often hold a dozen small positions—a percent and a half in REITs, 2% in listed private equity, 2% in merger arbitrage—creating administrative overhead and reliance on passively constructed ETFs that rarely hold up to scrutiny. Eagleston consolidates those sleeves into one vehicle with active management, dynamic weighting, and an options overlay.

The fund holds eleven sleeves, including merger arbitrage, listed private equity, royalties, metals and mining, and commodity-sensitive equities, among others. Construction is both top-down and bottom-up. The top-down process identifies which of four broad macro regimes the market is in—modeled after risk parity thinking—using seven factors: inflation, GDP growth, Federal Reserve policy, interest rates, credit spreads, stock multiples, and earnings growth. Each factor is placed on a continuum from negative to positive, and the combined reading informs how aggressively or defensively the fund is weighted across sleeves. In risk-on environments, higher-beta and inflation-sensitive positions are amplified. In risk-off, the fund shifts toward more defensive positioning. The top-down process runs through an investment committee monthly, with Eagleston and Will Brown making final decisions.

The bottom-up process focuses on three components of return: yield, growth, and multiple expansion. Research using Shiller’s data back to 1900 showed multiple expansion is a decade-dependent phenomenon, making dividend yield and earnings growth the more durable sources of long-run returns. Most holdings are identified through screens for dividend-paying companies with credible growth prospects.

Deeper Dive

The Options Overlay: Hedging and Exit Strategy Together

One of the fund’s structural differentiators is an options overlay that serves two purposes simultaneously. The primary hedge uses put options on a high-yield credit ETF. Eagleston’s rationale: the correlation between high-yield credit and equities during risk-off periods tends to be high, but implied volatility on those instruments is materially lower than on equity index options—meaning the same hedge costs less. In more severe risk-off environments, the fund adds put spreads on a small-cap index ETF, which Eagleston described as a belt-and-suspenders approach layered on top of the credit hedge.

The overlay also supports individual position management. The fund writes covered calls on names approaching price targets—when implied volatility is elevated and the position is near fair value, writing a call either harvests premium or results in the stock being called away at the target, a systematic exit without a discrete sell decision.

The OODA Loop Applied to Monthly Portfolio Decisions

The fund’s fact sheet references the OODA Loop—a framework developed by fighter pilot and military strategist John Boyd. Observe, orient, decide, act. Boyd designed it to describe how faster decision cycles outcompete slower ones in combat. Eagleston applies it to the monthly top-down process: the seven macro factors are the observation layer, rates of change drive orientation, sleeve weight decisions follow, and implementation is the act. The premise is that a smaller firm can complete this cycle faster than institutions constrained by size and committee overhead. Getting through the loop faster is a genuine edge when conditions are shifting quickly.

When the Framework Gets Tested: 2022 Versus 2023

The fund’s seven macro factors were predominantly in the negative camp heading into 2022, driving defensive positioning that worked well as markets sold off. In 2023, the same indicators stayed in negative territory but markets diverged from the historical relationships underlying the model—animal spirits in large-cap technology pushed returns in a direction the factor readings did not support. Eagleston was direct about it: the factors that drove 2022 results did not translate to 2023. His response was to hold conviction in the framework rather than adjust to short-term behavior. The distinction between a process working correctly and a cycle not favoring it is central to how he thinks about attribution.

Notable Insights

“We will build something if we think we can do it better. That was really Will’s impetus in building the firm, and it’s what prompted us to launch additional funds.”

“We wanted to have the ability to cut off the tail end of the distribution when you have market events like you had in 2020. To a lesser degree, like you had in 2022.”

“You think about the historical correlation among asset classes and if you were to just have a time machine and say this is what’s going to happen, what do you think would do well, you could very easily make that case anchored on those historical correlations. Which is why we prefer to use hedges—because those are mathematically a more assured way to do it. Now there is a cost associated with that, and we’re dynamic in terms of how we implement the hedges.”

Key Takeaways

  • The Formidable Fund consolidates multiple alternative sleeves into a single vehicle, solving the administrative burden and passive construction quality issues advisors encounter when building an alternatives allocation piecemeal
  • A seven-factor, four-regime top-down framework determines how aggressively or defensively the fund is weighted; the process is monthly, committee-based, with Eagleston and CEO Will Brown making final calls
  • The options overlay serves dual purposes: macro-level tail hedging through credit and small-cap put options, and individual position management through covered call writing for income and exits
  • The OODA Loop provides the operational cadence for top-down decision-making; the advantage is faster cycle time through smaller-firm nimbleness relative to larger institutions
  • Bottom-up security selection anchors on yield and earnings growth as the durable components of long-run equity returns, treating multiple expansion as a cyclical bonus rather than a base case
  • 2022 validated the defensive framework; 2023 tested it against animal spirits in large-cap growth—Eagleston’s response was to stay convicted in the process rather than adjust to short-term divergence

What This Means for Advisors

The Formidable Fund addresses a practical problem in alternatives allocation: small position sizes, passive construction quality, and administrative overhead make the traditional multi-sleeve approach inefficient. A single-ticket structure with active management and a built-in hedging overlay is a legitimate architectural alternative—assuming the top-down process holds up over a full cycle. The options overlay is also worth attention: the use of high-yield credit instruments as a hedge proxy—rather than equity index puts—reflects a cost-of-carry calculation that, when correlation holds, delivers more protection per dollar. The caveat Eagleston acknowledged is that 2022 and 2023 together illustrate the limits of historical factor correlation. The framework is systematic; the market does not always cooperate. Advisors should understand the difference between a process working correctly and a cycle not favoring it—they are two different conversations to have with clients.

Listen to the full conversation with Adam Eagleston on Behind the Ticker, available wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Neil Azous on Goals-Based Investing, Long-Short Equity, and Why Upside Capture Is Non-Negotiable
metaDescription: Neil Azous of Rareview Capital breaks down goals-based investing, why upside capture matters equally, and how his long-short ETF is built.
content:
Neil Azous founded Rareview Capital after building his background at Goldman Sachs, Donaldson Lufkin & Jenrette, and UBS Investment Bank—and he runs his firm around a philosophy that challenges the standard advisory model: that portfolios should be organized around goals, not risk tolerances. On this episode of Behind the Ticker, Azous explains how Rareview builds model portfolios, why their long-short equity strategy is designed to capture upside as aggressively as it protects on the downside, and what he has learned about the economics of the TAMP distribution channel after years of working within it.

About Neil Azous and Rareview Capital

Azous describes the asset management industry as falling into three buckets: disruption of the existing distribution framework (think robo-advisors), commoditization and scale through passive management (Vanguard, BlackRock, Dimensional), and specialization requiring genuine expertise in active management. Rareview fits the third category. The firm is an independent, SEC-registered investment advisor and ETF sponsor that delivers non-traditional investment strategies, model portfolios to third-party asset management providers, protective strategies requiring derivative expertise, customized interest rate solutions, and proprietary research. Critically, Rareview does not consume outside research—it produces its own, covering idea generation, risk mitigation, and portfolio construction.

Azous brings a multi-asset, global macro foundation to everything Rareview does. He describes himself as neither a bull nor a bear, neither an inflationist nor a deflationist, and agnostic on contentious debates like gold or China. That framework—process-driven, emotion-reduced—shapes how Rareview approaches each asset class differently depending on the instruments available and the probabilities at work. The firm manages a suite of four ETFs and operates a wealth management business alongside the asset management function.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Rareview’s model portfolios are organized around what Azous calls the wealth curve: four life-phase goals rather than four risk buckets. Clients want to grow money, preserve money, spend money in an income phase, or transfer assets to the next generation. The portfolio is built to achieve whichever of those goals applies—not to maintain a target allocation between stocks and bonds. Asset allocation still matters, but it is downstream of the goal, not the organizing principle.

Investment decision-making is fully model-driven, but each asset class uses a different model. On fixed income, Rareview uses a probability-based framework mapping the shape of the yield curve and the path of Federal Reserve policy on a zero-to-100 scale. When a probability reaches 80–90, they sell; when it falls to 0–10, they buy. The model is updated as Fed hiking or cutting cycle probabilities evolve, typically every six to eight weeks. On closed-end funds, a Python-based model identifies the cheapest security on an intra-sector basis across roughly 10–11 fixed income sectors. As relative valuations shift, the model prompts rotation. On equity, Rareview partnered with Rod Jones at GST Management—a former index methodology consultant who worked with some of the largest pools of capital in the world. That strategy is fully algorithmic, systematic, and quantitative, with no discretionary override and no human emotion.

Deeper Dive

Why Goals-Based Construction Beats Risk-Based for Most Clients

Azous is direct about why the traditional risk-based model falls short. When a portfolio is organized around a risk tolerance questionnaire, investment decisions are anchored to maintaining a certain allocation profile—not to achieving the outcome the client actually needs. The goals-based framework asks a simpler question first: what are you trying to do with this money, and when? The answer shapes everything from security selection to the acceptable time horizon for underperformance. What advisors often identify as a risk tolerance problem, Azous has found, is more accurately a goal mismatch: clients are not failing to understand volatility—they are invested in a strategy that was never calibrated to their actual objective.

The Long-Short Upside Capture Problem

The hedge fund industry’s role shifted significantly after the global financial crisis. Before 2008, hedge funds were alpha generators. After 2008, they became diversifiers—tools for managing downside risk that, by design, sacrifice upside participation. The typical long-short equity fund runs somewhere between 20% and 60% net long, averaging around 40% net long, which implies roughly 0.4 beta to the broad market. If the market is up 10%, they are up around 4%. Azous finds this an untenable client experience. When the market is up 20% and a hedged product is up 4%, advisors lose clients—not because the strategy failed but because it succeeded at the wrong objective. Rareview’s systematic equity strategy was designed to solve this: stay invested in bull markets, avoid catastrophic drawdowns (defined as south of negative 25%), and minimize the time back to break even. Azous frames this as the central investment challenge for any equity investor, not a product feature.

The TAMP Channel: Hard Economics and High Overhead

Rareview has delivered model portfolios to TAMPs and turnkey asset management providers, and Azous is candid about what that experience has revealed. The economics of the TAMP channel have deteriorated sharply. Fees available to a manager on a TAMP platform have compressed to 10 to 30 basis points—roughly a quarter to a third of other revenue streams. Platforms that once had manageable competition now host more than a thousand strategies, making the marketing challenge substantial. The regulatory and operational burden of managing models on multiple platforms to the same standard as a direct wealth management client is significant. Azous still participates in model delivery, but as a deliberate, resource-aware choice—not an assumption that platform presence drives allocations the way it once did five or six years ago.

Notable Insights

“I’m not a bull or a bear. I’m neither an inflationist or a deflationist and I absolutely have no religion when it comes to contentious topics like gold or China. They’re just another commodity or country to me. Ultimately I’m agnostic to the region or the asset class and the benefit to all that is that allows me to be process driven and reduce human emotion when it comes to investing.”

“If the stock market’s up 20% and you’re only up 4% because you’re running some type of hedged product, I tend to see people fire their financial advisors or replace that ETF with another one due to that significant underperformance. Upside capture to us is very important, if not equally as important as the downside capture.”

“The seminal investment issue for investors is how do you stay invested in bull markets, avoid the catastrophic stock market sell-off and bear market, and then minimize that time back to break even.”

Key Takeaways

  • Rareview organizes model portfolios around four life-phase goals (grow, preserve, spend, transfer) rather than risk tolerance buckets—shifting the central investment question from how much can you lose to what are you trying to achieve
  • Each asset class is managed through a distinct model: probability-based and yield-curve-driven for fixed income, Python-based for closed-end funds, fully algorithmic for the equity long-short strategy
  • The long-short equity strategy is built specifically to solve the upside capture problem that has defined post-2008 hedge fund products—Azous considers upside participation equally important as downside protection
  • The partnership with GST Management on the equity side brought in index methodology expertise from someone who has built strategies for some of the largest pools of capital in the world
  • The TAMP and model delivery channel has compressed economics and high operational demands; Rareview participates selectively with clear-eyed awareness of the ROI math
  • Rareview’s proprietary research—covering idea generation, risk mitigation, and portfolio construction—is made available to investors as a value-add, not marketed as a separate fee-bearing product

What This Means for Advisors

The goals-based framework Azous describes is worth taking seriously as an alternative to the traditional risk-tolerance-driven portfolio architecture. When you anchor around a client’s actual goal and time horizon rather than their tolerance for short-term volatility, you end up with different securities, different benchmarks, and—importantly—different conversations when markets get difficult. Clients who understand they are invested to meet a specific objective are more likely to stay the course than clients who understood only that they were in a moderately aggressive allocation.

The long-short equity discussion carries practical weight for advisors who have avoided the category because of bull-market underperformance. The post-2008 version of long-short was largely defensive by design. Products built specifically around the problem Azous identifies—participate on the upside, avoid catastrophic losses, get back to break even quickly—represent a different construction philosophy than the traditional 40% net-long hedge fund. Understanding which version you are evaluating matters more than the category label.

Listen to the full conversation with Neil Azous on Behind the Ticker, available wherever you get your podcasts.

title: John Forlines on Donoghue Forlines: Free Cash Flow, Real Assets, and the Behavioral Case for Risk Management
metaDescription: John Forlines of Donoghue Forlines on free cash flow screening, recession signals, and the behavioral case for risk-managed real asset investing.
content:
John Forlines, co-founder of Donoghue Forlines, joins Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker to make the behavioral and quantitative case for risk-managed investing. With a career spanning securities law, private equity at JP Morgan, technology investing, and more than two decades running systematic portfolio strategies, Forlines brings a practitioner's perspective that cuts through theoretical debates about buy-and-hold versus tactical management. The conversation centers on their yield enhanced real asset strategy—a free cash flow-driven fund designed to deliver meaningful income with built-in recession protection.

About John Forlines and Donoghue Forlines

Forlines started as an English and economics major before heading to law school, where he focused on the predecessors to today's ETFs—structured products that allowed institutions to take directional views with embedded yield. That early work with derivative vehicles, floors, caps, and collars gave him a foundation in portfolio engineering that has carried through his entire career.

After law school he joined JP Morgan as it was quietly assembling an investment banking operation ahead of the Glass-Steagall repeal. Forlines co-ran private equity there, with the firm's largest assignment being the liquidation of a major commercial bank's venture portfolio—his entry point into the technology world. He subsequently led technology, media, and telecom investing through the late 1990s and exited around the 2000 market peak.

He then started a family office focused on financial services and fintech opportunities, which eventually led to a quantitative ETF portfolio business. He notes the concept was novel in 2009, when roughly $600 billion in ETFs were outstanding and broker presentations required six or seven pages explaining what an ETF even was. He sold that firm to Donoghue in 2017, forming Donoghue Forlines—combining Donoghue's risk-on/risk-off mutual fund expertise with Forlines' systematic ETF portfolio engineering.

Today the firm operates across model portfolios, ETFs, and mutual funds. Their ETF suite includes the yield enhanced real asset strategy, a high yield fund, and a risk-managed innovation fund. The investment committee functions primarily as an asset allocation committee, with screening and timing logic embedded directly in the individual ETF structures. Forlines also teaches economics and decision science at Duke University one day per week each semester—a commitment he started in 2012 and has kept going well beyond his original two-year plan.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The firm's core philosophy is grounded in two realities: markets can produce extended periods of strong returns that mask building risks, and behavioral science tells us investors are significantly more sensitive to losses than gains. Rather than debating passive versus tactical, Donoghue Forlines builds strategies designed to keep advisors in the game with their clients through the full cycle—particularly through the large drawdowns that tend to rupture advisor-client relationships.

Their yield enhanced real asset strategy is the clearest expression of this philosophy. Rather than using traditional earnings screens, the fund applies a free cash flow methodology borrowed from private equity. The screen targets high-quality real asset companies—including REITs and commodity-oriented businesses—that generate cash that isn't susceptible to accounting manipulation. A rules-based recession signal is embedded in the strategy, shifting the portfolio defensively before or during significant drawdowns. The result is a fund designed to deliver meaningful yield while protecting against the left-tail events that most damage long-term compounding.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

The Behavioral Science Foundation

The case for risk management at Donoghue Forlines is not primarily quantitative—it's psychological. Forlines points directly to research showing that investors are roughly two and a half to three times more risk averse than they are gain-seeking when it comes to money and other high-stakes domains. That asymmetry explains why advisors running pure buy-and-hold strategies tend to lose clients during major corrections, even when the long-term math supports staying invested.

Forlines draws on work from GMO and Robert Shiller, both of whom challenged the simple long-termist case by reframing it around the actual business of managing client relationships. The theoretical argument for staying fully invested over long periods may be sound, but for client-facing advisors, every significant market dislocation is a survival event. That's the problem Donoghue Forlines builds to solve—not just generating returns, but generating returns in a way advisors can sustain with real clients who feel losses asymmetrically.

Free Cash Flow as the Starting Point

The yield enhanced real asset strategy begins with a private equity-style lens applied to public markets. Forlines argues that most public market screens are built around earnings—a number susceptible to accounting charges, special items, and management discretion. The firm instead screens for what he calls money that's available for investors: free cash flow.

Within the real asset universe, this means targeting REITs, real estate-adjacent businesses, and commodity-linked companies that meet a strict cash flow threshold. The result is a portfolio of high-quality, high-yielding opportunities where the yield is less suspect than what a dividend history or stated earnings screen might surface. The allocation across sectors—how much oil, how much real estate, how much in softer commodity names—is then determined through a rules-based process. Once the portfolio is formed, the allocation decisions become systematic rather than discretionary.

How the Risk Signal Works

Each ETF in the Donoghue Forlines suite carries a rules-based signal, and those signals vary in speed. The yield enhanced real asset strategy uses a slower-moving signal calibrated to recession risk—the logic being that the largest drawdowns tend to occur just before or during recessions. The signal is built around long-term historical index behavior, constraining when and how aggressively the portfolio shifts defensively.

Other ETFs in the suite use faster-twitch signals for more frequent volatility events. Forlines notes that the frequency of volatility has increased meaningfully over the past two decades—what used to arrive in distinct episodes now tends to persist. That structural change is why the firm maintains multiple strategies with different signal speeds rather than applying a single framework everywhere.

Notable Insights

"People are much more likely—about two and a half, three times more likely—to be risk averse with things like money and their health and other things that are important than gain seeking. That's really what we both built our philosophy around."

"If you're an advisor, a money manager, and you run your business like that, every time a business cycle shifts over or there's radical change in the market like a 2008, you're going to lose clients."

"A lot of the way screens and managers look at companies is wrong in our view in the public markets. We look at it like private market folks do—we look just at our version of free cash flow."

Key Takeaways

  • Donoghue Forlines was formed when Forlines sold his quantitative ETF portfolio firm to Donoghue in 2017, combining two complementary risk management philosophies under one roof.
  • The firm's behavioral finance foundation—investors are 2.5 to 3 times more risk averse than gain-seeking—drives the entire product design philosophy, not just the marketing.
  • Their yield enhanced real asset strategy screens on free cash flow rather than earnings, applying a private equity lens to public markets to surface high-quality, high-yielding positions where the income is structurally more reliable.
  • A rules-based recession signal is embedded in the strategy, designed to shift defensively before or during the drawdowns that most damage both long-term returns and client relationships.
  • The firm maintains multiple signal speeds across its ETF suite, matching calibration to the type of risk each strategy is built to manage—recognizing that not all volatility warrants the same response.
  • Forlines has taught economics and decision science at Duke University for over a decade, a consistent thread connecting the academic behavioral finance research to the firm's practical portfolio construction.

What This Means for Advisors

The Donoghue Forlines story is about building strategies advisors can sustain through a full market cycle with real clients. The free cash flow methodology and multi-speed signal architecture give Forlines a specific toolkit to act on the behavioral insight that most passive strategies ignore. For advisors seeking real asset exposure with yield and downside discipline built in, the yield enhanced real asset strategy offers a differentiated approach—one that borrows from private equity valuation, applies rules-based risk management, and is transparent enough to explain clearly before the next correction arrives.

The full conversation with John Forlines is available on the Behind the Ticker podcast. Hear more on how the firm's signal construction works across market cycles, how the 2017 merger reshaped both firms' product strategies, and what Forlines took away from two decades navigating markets from JP Morgan's private equity desk to systematic ETF management.

title: John Davi on After-Tax, After-Inflation Returns and How Astoria Advisors Builds Inflation-Aware Portfolios
metaDescription: Astoria founder John Davi on after-tax, after-inflation returns and how his inflation-sensitive strategy fits a core-satellite model portfolio framework.
content:
John Davi, founder of Astoria Advisors, brings two decades of institutional research and portfolio construction experience from Merrill Lynch and Morgan Stanley to a conversation about inflation, portfolio architecture, and the realities of running a model advisory practice. Brad Roth and John cover as much ground on investment philosophy as on the firm's inflation-sensitive strategy—and that turns out to be exactly the right context for understanding why Astoria built the product in the first place.

About John Davi and Astoria Advisors

Davi spent 20 years in institutional research before founding Astoria in 2017—a decade at Merrill Lynch and a decade at Morgan Stanley, beginning in quantitative research in the late 1990s at a time when quant was still considered a niche discipline. His focus throughout was consistent: how to build portfolios that deliver unique sources of risk and return for institutional clients, always through a macro lens. Starting Astoria in 2017 was the entrepreneurial step he had been building toward—combining the institutional caliber of that background with the flexibility of an independent structure.

Astoria targets independent RIAs with assets under half a billion dollars—a segment where advisors need research support and operational leverage but haven't yet built out internal CIO capabilities. The firm physically manages roughly $300 million in assets as a sub-advisor, handling trading, rebalancing, corporate actions, and tax-loss harvesting directly. A larger portion of the client base is served through OCIO arrangements, where Astoria builds and advises on portfolios that the advisor then executes. The positioning is deliberately different from large platform providers. Davi describes the goal as being "the Vanguard of the SMA world"—a lower price point, more hands-on, more customizable than what large institutional TAMP providers offer. Astoria provides blended ETF models, strategic asset allocation portfolios, and a suite of quantitative stock models that advisors can mix and match to target specific portfolio outcomes.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Astoria's investment philosophy runs on three principles: after-tax, after-inflation, risk-adjusted returns. Davi is clear these are not marketing language—they are the actual architecture around which every portfolio decision gets made.

After-tax is not an afterthought. Tax-loss harvesting at the ETF level is a meaningful return enhancer the firm optimizes consistently, a capability that large platform providers don't always prioritize at the same frequency. After-inflation reflects a view Davi has held since the firm's founding in 2017: inflation protection belongs in portfolios all the time, not just when inflation is the consensus conversation. The compounding math is simple—two percent per year over 30 years is material, and ignoring it is a slow leak. The firm has held some form of inflation-linked exposure—gold, physical commodity funds, and more recently its own inflation-sensitive strategy—in its models continuously since inception.

The risk-adjusted framing reflects a rejection of simple concentration in whatever is driving the market in any given period. The goal is a smoother portfolio experience across equities, fixed income, and alternatives rather than riding a handful of names in a momentum-driven market. The methodology is macro plus quant—a combination Davi notes is genuinely unusual, as most firms lean heavily in one direction. Astoria uses a cycle indicator framework of roughly 60 data inputs to assess where the economy sits, then tilts its core-satellite model accordingly. At the time of recording in late June 2023, Davi described bifurcated data: strong housing activity and building permits alongside leading economic indicators still looking for a floor, with rate cuts nowhere on the near-term horizon.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

Core-Satellite Construction in Practice

Half of any Astoria portfolio consists of core positions that don't change regardless of where the economy sits—structural exposures to factors and asset classes the firm believes in over the long term. The other half is the satellite, reviewed regularly and targeted for no more than 30 to 35% annual turnover under normal market conditions. The satellite is where cycle views translate into portfolio action: adding high-quality corporate credit when a soft landing seems plausible, extending duration when recession risk rises, trimming small cap exposure late in the cycle and reintroducing it when leading indicators turn. Alternatives occupy roughly half of the bond allocation—things like merger arbitrage, which performs best in lower-rate, high-deal-flow environments, and inflation-linked strategies. The framework for understanding alternatives is insurance, not performance: "You need an event to happen for that insurance to kick in," Davi said. "Like car insurance—you get into a car accident, your premium that you pay every year finally gets put to use." Expecting alternatives to compete with growth in a straight bull market misunderstands their role.

The Inflation-Sensitive Strategy and Astoria's Own Conviction

Astoria's inflation-sensitive ETF entered the firm's own model portfolios roughly a year to 18 months before this conversation—meaning the position was established around 2021 or early 2022, when inflation was moving but not yet the dominant market narrative. Davi articulated a strong view on inflation as far back as June 2020. The fact that the strategy now holds a meaningful allocation within Astoria's own models is a significant signal: the firm is allocating client money and internal model money to the same thesis simultaneously. For Davi, the inclusion of inflation protection has never been a product decision. It is a portfolio conviction that has been consistent since the firm's founding, expressed through whatever the best available vehicle happens to be at any given time.

The Performance-Chasing Problem

The most candid section of the conversation was Davi's account of the biggest frustration in running a model advisory practice: advisors who build diversified, multi-strategy allocations and then reallocate toward whichever sleeve just outperformed. Growth strategies underperformed significantly in 2022. Advisors who reduced that exposure appropriately were positioned for the rotation. As growth rebounded sharply into 2023—particularly unprofitable technology—many wanted to chase back in. "It's really frustrating to me when we sit down with an adviser and we lay out a really nice asset allocation between our models but one sleeve of that asset allocation does really well and they want to strategy chase it," Davi said. His answer to the frustration is structural: alternatives and inflation-linked strategies require time and events to perform. Pulling them after a difficult year defeats the purpose of the allocation.

Notable Insights

"We believe in after tax, after inflation, risk adjusted returns. Those are some pretty serious words."

"It's really frustrating to me when we sit down with an adviser and we lay out a really nice asset allocation between our models but one sleeve of that asset allocation does really well and they want to strategy chase it."

"Things like our inflation-sensitive strategy made it into our portfolios because we felt strongly about inflation. You do need years and years to go by for these views to play out."

Key Takeaways

  • Astoria's three-part mandate—after-tax, after-inflation, risk-adjusted—shapes every portfolio decision, not just product selection
  • The firm has included inflation protection in its models since inception in 2017, well before inflation became the consensus market concern
  • A core-satellite construction framework targets no more than 30 to 35% annual turnover, balancing structural factor exposures with cycle-aware tilts
  • Alternatives occupy roughly half the bond allocation and function as insurance—they underperform in straight bull markets and deliver when specific events occur
  • Performance chasing within multi-strategy allocations is identified as the primary challenge in managing a model advisory practice over time
  • The cycle indicator framework uses roughly 60 data inputs to assess economic positioning; at recording in June 2023, data was bifurcated with no near-term catalyst for rate cuts

What This Means for Advisors

The Astoria conversation is as much about portfolio philosophy as any specific product. Advisors who believe in after-inflation, after-tax thinking will recognize Davi's framework immediately and will likely find both the model business structure and the inflation-sensitive strategy worth closer examination. The firm's willingness to tell advisors plainly that alternatives underperform in bull markets—and that patience is the cost of admission for macro-driven strategies—is a useful counterpoint to performance-based product selection. For advisors whose clients are questioning why defensive or alternative allocations have lagged in a momentum market, this conversation provides both the language and the logic to explain the architecture. For advisors interested in learning more, Astoria publishes a cycle indicator deck on their website that walks through their full indicator set and current portfolio thinking.

Listen to the full conversation with John Davi on Behind the Ticker, available wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Michael Gayed on Risk Cycling, the Publisher-First Playbook, and What QE Did to His Strategy
metaDescription: Michael Gayed of ATAC Funds talks risk-on/risk-off cycles, the publisher-first growth model, and how a decade of QE challenged his systematic strategy.
content:
Michael Gayed, CEO and founder of ATAC Funds, built one of the more recognizable brands in the ETF industry not through wholesalers or steak dinners but through writing. His risk-on/risk-off and junk-on/junk-off strategies are extensions of a content machine he has been running for years, and on this episode of Behind the Ticker, he explains exactly how that model works, what cycles his strategies actually need to perform, and what it felt like to launch a fund the day before QE3 began.

About Michael Gayed and ATAC Funds

Gayed’s path into markets started with his father, an Egyptian immigrant who worked his way from a gas station to IBM, earned an MBA, and eventually joined Merrill Lynch on Bob Ferrell’s team in the mid-to-late 1980s. Ferrell is the technician behind a set of investment rules that still circulate widely in markets circles today. Gayed’s father later left to start his own investment advisory firm in 1991, growing it to $2 billion in assets under management with a large-cap growth mandate and his own hedge funds.

Michael joined the family business after college. His father passed in 2008—the same year Gayed earned his CFA charter, with the global financial crisis already underway. He spent a year at a family office, moved to a small RIA, and began writing prolifically for Seeking Alpha, Mark Faber’s Boom Doom Report, and pieces distributed to Barry Ritholtz and John Mauldin. In 2011, he made an aggressive public call for a summer market crash—writing 17 articles with the phrase in the title. The S&P fell roughly 17% in weeks. The media followed. Gayed launched the ATAC rotation mutual fund in late 2012 and has since added two ETFs: one built around rotating between risk-on and risk-off exposures, and another rotating between high-yield and investment-grade credit. He also runs the Lead Lag Report, a subscription research service with more than a thousand paying subscribers.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The ATAC rotation strategy operates on a binary signal. In risk-on environments, it rotates into equities—specifically tilting toward small caps and emerging markets, which Gayed argues are the only two ways to meaningfully outperform the broad market in an equity-only long-only framework absent leverage. In risk-off environments, the model moves to long-duration treasuries. The strategy is entirely rules-based. As Gayed described it, it would run the exact same way if he died tomorrow.

The track record reflects the strategy’s cycle dependence. The mutual fund had two exceptional years: 2017, a historically low-volatility year when small caps and emerging markets showed genuine drift and the fund returned 27%; and 2020, when the COVID crash sent the fund into treasuries early and it finished the year up 72%. Outside those periods, the strategy has struggled—because outside those periods, the dominant market cycle was one of QE-suppressed volatility, concentrated large-cap leadership, and almost no left tail to catch.

Deeper Dive

The Publisher-First Growth Model

Gayed is deliberate about separating the brand from the fund. His thesis is that the traditional wholesaler model—boots on the ground, relationship allocations, platform fees—is expensive and losing ground. His approach is to function as a publisher first: the Twitter content, Seeking Alpha analysis, podcast appearances, and video clips exist not to market the funds directly but to build Gayed as a recognizable analyst. People who discover him through the content eventually find the funds. He calls this the halo effect.

Different platforms get different versions of him. The podcast is professional. Seeking Alpha is analytical and chart-dense. Twitter—where he has 740,000 followers—is louder and more confrontational. He acknowledged the persona is calculated: different frameworks work for different platforms, and he adapts accordingly. Behind all of it, the goal is the same: build the brand name and presence, and let the funds benefit from the audience that results.

What QE3 Did to the Strategy

Gayed launched the ATAC rotation mutual fund the day before QE3 began in September 2012. The timing was structurally damaging, not because the model was wrong but because the cycle that followed suppressed exactly what his model needed. Quantitative easing killed off left tail risk. Every time the strategy signaled risk-off and rotated to treasuries, the market reversed and kept running. Whipsaws in the risk-off signal were the dominant pattern from 2012 through most of 2019. On top of that, the risk-on cycle that did materialize was concentrated in large-cap domestic equities—the one segment where a tilt toward small caps and emerging markets provides no advantage. The decade was, in Gayed’s own framing, exactly the wrong cycle for everything his model is designed to do. He has internalized this not as a flaw but as a feature of any rules-based approach: every strategy is built for a particular environment, and patience through the wrong one is the price of admission.

Relationships as Scratch Off Tickets

One detail that gets buried under the content volume is how much direct relationship work Gayed does. He described running 10 to 15 calls per day—with prospects, ETF launch candidates, research subscribers, and partners. He uses a public calendar link and lets people self-schedule. The philosophy behind the volume is pragmatic: you do not know which relationship will pay off, so you take all of them seriously. He applies this logic equally to his Lead Lag Report subscribers, doing individual outreach to offer a call with anyone who wants one. For a boutique manager without a distribution team, the volume is the strategy.

Notable Insights

“Life is a series of scratch off tickets. You don’t know which scratch off ticket is going to pay off for you. I view every relationship like a scratch off ticket. You don’t know who you’re going to help and who’s going to help you. Which is why I don’t believe in burning bridges.”

“I believe in being compliantly Machiavellian. The ends justify the means. If that’s what gets attention, that’s what I’m going to be as a persona. But everything is ultimately about trying to build that brand name, that presence, and hopefully have the halo effect.”

“Every strategy is a function of the cycle that you operate in. I need a very particular environment for really all my funds. I need an environment where there’s some left tail risk. I need an environment where it’s not just about large caps. We haven’t had that in a while.”

Key Takeaways

  • Rules-based strategies carry specific cycle dependencies; understanding when conditions favor or undermine the model is essential before allocating
  • Gayed’s publisher-first approach treats content as the top of the distribution funnel and ETFs as the downstream monetization—not the other way around
  • The ATAC rotation strategy needs both elevated left tail risk and an environment where small caps and emerging markets carry meaningful weight to produce the returns it was designed for
  • A decade of quantitative easing suppressed the exact conditions Gayed’s model requires, explaining its uneven track record between 2012 and 2021 outside of two standout years
  • Wire house channels have structural incentive misalignment with ETFs because 12B-1 fee structures favor mutual fund shelf placement—Gayed has built around this rather than into it
  • Boutique ETF managers without distribution teams can substitute volume: Gayed treats every relationship as a long-odds bet worth taking and does enough of them that the math starts working

What This Means for Advisors

Gayed’s strategy is not built for all-weather deployment. It was designed for markets with fat left tails and meaningful small cap and emerging market cycles. Advisors evaluating risk-on/risk-off approaches should understand that extended periods of low volatility and large-cap dominance will produce whipsaws that have nothing to do with model quality. The 72% return in 2020 is a useful reference point—but so is the difficulty of the years surrounding it. Setting the right expectations upfront is more than a relationship management exercise; it is the difference between a client who understands the cycle and one who exits at exactly the wrong time.

For advisors who follow content-driven managers, Gayed’s model also illustrates something worth understanding about where boutique manager brand equity actually comes from. It is not the performance page. It is the years of consistent public analysis that existed long before the fund launch—and the daily discipline of doing the work to maintain that presence.

Listen to the full conversation with Michael Gayed on Behind the Ticker, available wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Michael Venuto on Tidal Financial: What It Actually Takes to Launch and Grow an ETF
metaDescription: Michael Venuto of Tidal Financial on ETF launch infrastructure, the 114-question strategic assessment, and what separates successful funds from failures.
content:
Michael Venuto has been working in and around the ETF industry since before most of the people who now ask him for help entered the business. He interned on the floor of the New York Stock Exchange, worked at Horizon Kinetics when it was a handful of people, served as head of investments at Global X, and eventually built Tidal Financial Group into a $6.5 billion platform that has helped launch and grow approximately 80 ETFs. In this conversation with Brad Roth, Venuto covers what it actually takes to get a fund off the ground — and why so many smart people get it wrong.

About Michael Venuto and Tidal Financial Group

Venuto grew up in North Carolina, studied philosophy and religion, and landed an internship on the New York Stock Exchange expecting to write an exposé on Wall Street's greed. Instead, he said, he fell in love with the meritocracy he found there. From that internship he moved into a stockbroker role at what was then Wheat First Union — where, by his own account, he learned "everything not to do" — before joining Horizon Kinetics as one of its earliest employees.

At Horizon Kinetics, Venuto helped the firm develop an investment thesis around the ETF industry itself. That meant taking early positions in Wisdom Tree, financing what eventually became Columbia Threadneedle's ETF family through Emerging Global Advisors, and partnering with ICE Ventures before any of those were consensus bets. He left in 2012, incubated Tidal while spending roughly three years as head of investments and a partner at Global X, and over the following decade grew Tidal into a full-service ETF platform. He is also the portfolio manager of BLOK, the first blockchain ETF, which Tidal actively manages. The firm is in the process of spinning out its wealth management business — approximately $600 million in assets — into a separate entity called United Ethos so it can focus exclusively on the ETF platform.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Tidal is not an investment manager in the traditional sense — it is infrastructure and distribution support for other people's fund strategies. The firm operates across four main service tiers. The core advisory business is a full white-label operation inside Tidal's own trust: the client wouldn't know if anything was being outsourced, Venuto said, because Tidal oversees everything from SEC filings to compliance, legal, and portfolio management. The second tier is sub-advisory, where Tidal handles trading and basket management for external trust structures. The third is access to the ETF Think Tank, Tidal's research and media brand, which outside firms can subscribe to in order to reach a curated advisor audience. The fourth is consulting for organizations that are on a path toward launching a fund but not yet ready.

For core advisory clients, Tidal also offers an economics partnership on approximately 30% of relationships: the firm covers a portion of the fund's operating costs and in return takes an equivalent share of the profits once the fund becomes profitable. As Venuto framed it, the alignment is straightforward — he does not want to write checks to struggling funds. He wants clients to be receiving checks, not cutting them.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

The Strategic Assessment Process

Before Tidal takes on any new advisory client, it puts them through a 114-question assessment scored against the responses of more than 78 other ETF issuers the firm has evaluated. The output is a seven-page report covering strategy, product, sales, and marketing scores — each benchmarked against every other issuer in Tidal's history — along with a cost forecast and specific recommendations. Venuto described it as similar to a personality exam: structured, comparative, and diagnostic. The process runs both directions. Potential clients learn where they stand relative to successful launches; Tidal learns whether the partnership is likely to be viable. Occasionally, the assessment results in Tidal declining the engagement. "I don't want an ETF on my platform that doesn't succeed," Venuto said.

What Most People Get Wrong

Venuto was consistent on this point throughout the conversation: the things first-time ETF issuers fixate on are almost never the things that determine success. The investment thesis matters — but it's not the deciding factor. "I don't care that you've got the next great idea," he said. "Good for you. How are you going to market it? Where's your seed capital? Who are your partners? What's your story?" He has seen funds with elegant strategies fail because the ticker was off-putting to advisors. He has seen simpler products succeed because the distribution story was clear and repeatable. The pre-launch work — positioning, seed backing, distribution relationships — is where outcomes are most often determined, not in the investment committee room.

Building a Business Through Partners and Pivots

Venuto is frank about how many times Tidal's direction changed before it became what it is today. The firm started as a planned SMA business, pivoted to content and research to help ETF issuers grow, then turned that IP into a white-label launch-and-grow platform. The wealth management book that was originally meant to be the core business is now being spun out. His advice for entrepreneurs reflects that lived experience: know what you're not good at, find partners who cover those gaps, and align incentives clearly from the start. "Overnight success took 11 years," he said. "Everything's going to be so much harder than you think it's going to be and it's going to cost twice as much — maybe three times as much."

Notable Insights

"The things that people think are important are so much less important and the things that really are important they don't want to hear. I don't care that you've got the next great idea — good for you. How are you going to market it? Where's your seed capital? Who are your partners?"

"Preventing mistakes is way, way more important than what you do next. You can't — if you get the product wrong, or you get the launch date wrong, or the ticker wrong — I see it all the time. They get lost in things that we can help them prevent."

"I don't want to write you checks. When it's not succeeding, you're writing me checks. I want you to be receiving checks from me, not the other way around."

Key Takeaways

  • Tidal manages $6.5 billion in assets and has helped approximately 80 ETFs launch and grow across four service tiers
  • The strategic assessment — 114 questions scored against 78-plus prior ETF issuers — is the entry point for any advisory client relationship, and Tidal occasionally declines based on the results
  • Tidal takes an economics stake in roughly 30% of advisory clients, covering a share of operating costs in exchange for a share of future profits
  • Seed capital, distribution strategy, and product positioning are typically more determinative of fund success than the underlying investment thesis
  • The ETF Think Tank is Tidal's media and research brand, accessible by subscription to firms outside its trust as a way to reach a curated advisor audience
  • Fund launch from signed LOI takes approximately 90 days: 75 days for SEC review, 15 days for Tidal-side processing

What This Means for Advisors

For advisors evaluating newer or smaller ETF issuers, Venuto's conversation provides a useful lens. Funds on the Tidal platform have been through an explicit vetting process — the strategic assessment is not a formality, and Tidal has skin in the game on roughly a third of its lineup. That doesn't guarantee outcomes, but it does suggest a minimum level of operational and distribution thinking occurred before launch. As the ETF universe grows and due diligence requirements tighten, understanding the infrastructure behind a fund is increasingly relevant.

More broadly, Venuto's description of what separates surviving funds from failed ones — seed capital, distribution relationships, clear positioning — is directly applicable to advisor product evaluation. Funds without a coherent distribution story or adequate seed backing face structural headwinds that have nothing to do with the quality of the underlying strategy, and advisors who can identify those gaps early are better positioned to avoid adding products that won't be around in three years.

The full episode with Michael Venuto is available on Behind the Ticker.
