He covers the full evolution of Tidal from an SMA concept to a platform, the details of how active management works within the advisory structure, the origin of BLOK as the first blockchain ETF, and what he has observed across roughly 80 fund launches — including what the successful ones have in common.

title: Dan Cupkovic on Swan: A 90/10 Structure Built to Survive Market Extremes
metaDescription: Dan Cupkovic on Swan's 90/10 treasury-plus-options structure, its best and worst market environments, and how advisors are using it in portfolios.
content:
Dan Cupkovic had been managing the Swan strategy internally at Argie Investment Services for more than four years before it launched as an ETF in November 2018. The structure is straightforward by design: 90% in a treasury bond ladder, 10% in call options on the S&P 500. But the thinking behind it — and the specific conditions under which it performs best — requires a more careful read. Cupkovic, now a partner at City Partners, one of the country's largest RIAs at approximately $70 billion in assets, joined Brad Roth to explain how the product works and where it fits in a portfolio.

About Dan Cupkovic and Black Swan

Cupkovic came to portfolio management through the financial planning side, spending his first several years at Northwestern Mutual before transitioning to Argie Investment Services, where he served as director of investments for nearly a decade. He joined City Partners, now the seventh-largest RIA in the country by his account, and sits on its national investment committee. His day job involves managing portfolios at scale — but he has deliberately kept a small number of individual client relationships. "I think it's important when you're on the money management side to understand the struggle," he said, "and how important it is to see both sides of it."

The idea for Swan emerged in 2013 while Cupkovic was reading "Options as a Strategic Investment." He launched it internally in August 2014, subjected the methodology to two white papers — "Leaping Black Swans," written by Bill Trainer and Dr. Indu Chachi, and "The Barbell Approach," which Cupkovic co-authored — and eventually partnered with Amplify to bring it to the ETF market. Two sister strategies followed using the identical structure: one providing NASDAQ 100 equity exposure and another tracking international developed markets.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Swan's mechanics are best understood through Cupkovic's framing: "90% safety belt money and 10% home run money." At the time of each rebalance, 90% of the portfolio goes into an intermediate treasury bond ladder targeting five to ten years of duration — a bulleted ladder structure designed to provide stability. The remaining 10% buys call options split into two equal tranches, one expiring in June of a given year and one in December.

When June arrives, the expiring options are sold and rolled to the following year's June contracts. Any excess from option returns goes into the fixed income sleeve; shortfalls come from the same pool. The cycle repeats in December. There are no ad hoc adjustments between rebalances — the strategy is fully rules-based and does not make discretionary calls about market direction.

The expected long-term return profile, as outlined in the white papers, is approximately 40-50% upside capture of equity markets, combined with income from the treasury ladder. In very bad markets, the call options can eventually expire worthless, but the treasury position typically holds value — in many cases appreciating when equities fall hard. In strong markets, the call options grow as a proportion of the portfolio, amplifying the gain until the next rebalance. The environment where Swan struggles is the flat-to-mildly-positive range, where time value decay erodes call value without enough market movement to compensate.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

Where Swan Fits in a Portfolio

Cupkovic described a 5-20% allocation within a globally asset-allocated model as the range where Swan adds the most incremental risk-adjusted return. He has also seen it used as a substitute for outright treasury exposure — advisors who want the income characteristic of intermediate bonds but want to retain some equity optionality alongside it. "I've seen people use it in lieu of just owning treasuries outright," he said. "They're saying, hey, I want risk-off fixed income but I don't like that risk-return dynamic — I can still get most of that but still have a little bit of upside." He has also seen it used as an alternative to high-yield bonds, offering a similar return profile but with less left-tail exposure during credit spread blowouts.

The 2022 Stress Test and What It Revealed

Cupkovic was candid about 2022, the strategy's most difficult year since the ETF launched. The treasury ladder — normally the ballast that absorbs equity drawdowns — posted significant losses as interest rates rose sharply. That simultaneous decline in both the call options and the treasury sleeve exposed the strategy's primary structural vulnerability: a rising rate environment where neither component is positioned to absorb the other's losses. Cupkovic addressed it directly rather than explaining it away, noting that advisors considering Swan need to understand this correlation breakdown is possible, however uncommon it has been historically. In every other year up to that point, the treasury position had generally held its value or appreciated during periods of equity stress.

Reading the Market in Mid-2023

The conversation included a market discussion that illuminated how Cupkovic thinks about risk positioning in real time. He described the year-to-date equity rally as notably narrow — he referenced a statistic suggesting roughly seven names had accounted for the vast majority of market returns — and framed it as an echo trade driven by recency bias rather than a broad fundamental recovery. "We've had such a narrow market," he said. "It's sort of reinforcing that bias." On the question of a soft landing, he was measured, framing the probability as roughly even. He pointed to the earnings yield on equities and the yield on short-duration treasuries as approximately equivalent at the time, suggesting the case for moving from cash into equities was not yet clear-cut.

Notable Insights

"It's 10% home run money with those call options that give you uncapped upside to the S&P 500 — 90% safety belt money. The long-term analysis is it gets you about 40 to 50% of the upside of the market. And if you combine it, that's kind of the expected risk-return profile."

"In very, very good positive environments, those call options are going to sing beautifully. When the markets do very poorly and you're down 20 or 30%... the bleeding stopped after those call options go to zero. You're left with a basket of treasuries."

"There could be an environment where the market's flat and we're still down five or seven percent because that call option didn't pay off. That plus or minus one standard deviation environment is not as great."

Key Takeaways

  • Swan is 90% intermediate treasury bond ladder plus 10% long call options on the S&P 500, rebalanced twice per year in June and December
  • The strategy targets approximately 40-50% equity upside capture with limited downside once the call option premium is spent
  • Best environments are the extremes — strong bull markets and sharp bear markets — not sideways or mildly positive markets
  • 2022 was the clearest structural stress test: rising rates pressured the treasury sleeve at the same time the call options expired without value
  • Typical allocation is 5-20% of a globally asset-allocated model for maximum Sharpe ratio contribution
  • Two sister strategies offer the identical structure with NASDAQ 100 or international developed market equity exposure

What This Means for Advisors

Swan is one of the more straightforward option-based strategies to explain to clients — two components, two rebalance dates, a fixed allocation. But Cupkovic's conversation makes clear that the fit discussion is equally important. Advisors who use Swan as a short-term risk-off placeholder are approaching it differently than those treating it as a permanent portfolio allocation, and those two use cases carry different performance expectations. The short-term tactical framing can lead to disappointment in flat markets; the long-term capital allocation framing is where Cupkovic believes the product is most coherent.

His candor about 2022 is worth noting in advisor conversations. Rather than minimizing the year, he explained what broke and why — giving advisors the language they need to set accurate expectations with clients before adding it to a model, not after an uncomfortable quarter.

The full episode with Dan Cupkovic is available on Behind the Ticker. He walks through the complete mechanics of both the options structure and the treasury ladder, covers current market conditions including his views on the Fed and yield curve dynamics, and discusses how he sees small cap relative to large cap growth heading into the second half of 2023.

title: David Cohen on HANDLS Indexes: Building Income ETFs from a Closed-End Fund Playbook
metaDescription: David Cohen of HANDLS Indexes on income ETF mechanics, why total return beats yield chasing, and the thinking behind a $1 billion-plus strategy.
content:
David Cohen built his career inside the original wave of ETF innovation — working alongside the people who created PowerShares, Claymore ETFs, and Bullet Shares before launching HANDLS Indexes with his partner Matt Patterson. The index behind one of the most distinctive income ETFs in the market, which has accumulated more than $1 billion in assets, started not with a chart or a model but with a single demographic observation: 10,000 people a day are retiring, they need income, and the tools available weren't serving them well. Brad Roth sat down with Cohen to get the full story.

About David Cohen and HANDLS Indexes

Cohen came out of the Chicago-area ETF community that produced some of the most influential names in the industry. He worked with Bruce Bond at PowerShares, alongside Dave Hooton at Claymore ETFs — where the team launched the first BRIC ETF in 2006 and an early multi-asset dividend product called the Yield Hog — and alongside Christian Magoon, who later founded Amplify. That cross-pollination of talent from what Cohen calls the "west wing" of Chicago's ETF scene gave him a front-row seat to what was and wasn't working in the business.

His partnership with Matt Patterson, a securities lawyer, proved particularly formative. Together they developed the Bullet Shares concept — ETFs that behave like bonds rather than bond funds, with defined maturity dates — which became one of the most successful fixed income ETF product innovations of that era. HANDLS came out of the same product-development mindset: identify a real gap, build rules around it, and deliver it in an ETF structure that's accessible and efficient. To be clear, Cohen and Patterson come from the product development side, not money management. "We are not portfolio managers now," Cohen said. "We create indexes — and there's a reason why."

Investment Strategy and Approach

HANDLS is built on a conviction that total return is the only number that matters — not yield, not distribution rate in isolation. Cohen frequently references AT&T as a cautionary example: investors chased the dividend for years while total return deteriorated. The index is designed to make that mistake harder to make.

The strategy has two components. The Core side maintains a static 70% fixed income, 30% equity allocation — a split Cohen says produced the best risk-adjusted returns historically over 50-plus years, consistent with Harry Markowitz's modern portfolio theory. The Explorer side gives Dorsey Wright's momentum algorithms room to rotate among income-oriented categories, including high-yield bonds, investment-grade corporates, Build America bonds (taxable municipals), and covered call strategies. The whole index rebalances monthly.

The distribution target is 7% annually, paid out monthly as one-twelfth of that figure. Rather than chasing yield, the index is engineered to produce that number through the optimal portfolio — then adjusted with leverage to hit it. The 5-handle version is unlevered; the 7-handle runs at approximately 1.23x; the 10-handle at 2x. The leverage is built into the product tier by design, not triggered dynamically by market conditions.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

Why Rules Matter More Than Discretion

Cohen draws a sharp line between active management and rules-based indexing — and while he respects both, he's deliberate about why HANDLS uses the latter. "Indexes have rules and rules don't change, they don't deviate," he said. The consistency of the methodology is part of the value proposition, particularly for income-focused investors who need predictability. Dorsey Wright's momentum selection introduces some tactical variation, but within defined categories chosen to remain income-consistent regardless of market environment. He noted that in the recent past Dorsey Wright overweighted MLPs — and it worked — but he frames that as the process functioning as intended, not as a prediction.

The Closed-End Fund Inheritance

Much of what makes HANDLS unusual comes directly from Cohen's years working in and around the closed-end fund market. That background taught him two things: first, that leveraged income strategies have a legitimate place in retirement portfolios; second, that the ETF wrapper can deliver the same economic result far more efficiently. Monthly rebalancing that would be expensive and time-consuming for an individual investor is routine for an ETF. The tax treatment of distributions — including the potential to defer capital gains and receive a portion as return of capital — is another structural advantage Cohen says is underappreciated. "An ETF can basically, because of the way the rebalancing aspect works, shift your long-term capital gains," he explained. "You don't have to recognize them — you defer them to the future."

On Total Return and Behavioral Traps

Cohen was direct about why yield-chasing destroys wealth over time. The return of capital treatment that sometimes accompanies the HANDLS distribution can look alarming on a statement, but Cohen frames it differently: if the fund earns more than it distributes, the accounting classification doesn't change the math. The goal is always total return — and investors who confuse distribution rate with total return are working against themselves. He also cautioned against tactical trading in strategies designed as long-term holdings: time in the market, not timing the market, is what allows the smoothing effect to work over a full cycle.

Notable Insights

"If your fund is making more than you're distributing then you are doing well. If you're not, you're not. It doesn't matter how they treat it from a tax perspective."

"If you look at AT&T since over the last decade or so or even 20 years — people buy it because it's a yield stock, but the total return hasn't been particularly good, in fact horrible. They've just been returning your money to you over a period of time."

"Some things are designed to be long-term holdings and they're designed to look over the long term and smooth performance over a long time. When you trade you're working against yourself — you can be right, you can be wrong, or you can be dead."

Key Takeaways

  • HANDLS is built on the historically optimal 70/30 fixed income/equity allocation, not a yield target
  • The Explorer sleeve uses Dorsey Wright momentum algorithms to rotate among income-oriented categories within defined guardrails
  • Leverage is baked into the product tiers by design — not dynamically triggered — keeping the index rules-based throughout
  • Monthly rebalancing and distributions are efficient inside an ETF structure in ways that would be costly for individual investors to replicate
  • Return of capital treatment is not inherently a red flag — in the context of an ETF's capital gains deferral mechanics, it can represent a genuine tax advantage
  • Total return is the measure that matters; distribution rate alone does not indicate portfolio health

What This Means for Advisors

Cohen's conversation offers a useful reframe for advisors working with income-focused clients who fixate on yield. The case for total-return thinking is well documented, but products that systematically enforce it — delivering a monthly distribution while optimizing for aggregate return rather than maximizing yield at the expense of price — are relatively rare. HANDLS positions itself as that kind of vehicle, and Cohen's willingness to use AT&T as a concrete counterexample gives advisors a plain-language illustration to use with clients.

On the tax side, Cohen raises a point worth considering: return of capital is often misread as a sign of a poorly structured product, but in the context of ETF capital gains deferral mechanics, it can represent a genuine benefit. The degree to which that matters is individual — tax status, holding period, and estate planning all factor in — but advisors who understand the distinction can have a more complete conversation with clients about after-tax outcomes rather than just headline yield.

The full conversation with David Cohen is available on Behind the Ticker. Cohen covers the full mechanics of the HANDLS strategy, the history of closed-end fund packaging, and why he has spent his career on index rules rather than discretionary management.

title: Dave Mazza on the CHAT ETF: Building a Portfolio for the Generative AI Era
metaDescription: Roundhill's Dave Mazza explains how the CHAT ETF selects companies, why generative AI differs from AI broadly, and why they chose an active structure.
content:
When Roundhill Investments launched their generative AI ETF in late May 2023, the timing felt almost inevitable. ChatGPT had become the fastest-growing consumer application in history, Nvidia had just delivered a landmark earnings quarter driven by AI chip demand, and advisors were fielding client questions about AI exposure from people who had never heard of a large language model six months earlier. Dave Mazza, Chief Strategy Officer at Roundhill, joined Brad to explain what separates generative AI from AI broadly, how the fund's two-part selection process works, and why the team chose an active structure for a category moving too fast for any static index to capture cleanly.

About Dave Mazza and Roundhill Investments

Dave Mazza brings more than two decades of ETF industry experience to his role at Roundhill. He spent over a decade at State Street Global Advisers leading the ETF research team within the SPDR business, then moved to OppenheimerFunds to lead ETF sales and marketing before its acquisition by Invesco. From there he joined Direxion, leading product development and strategy across their leverage, inverse, and thematic lineup, before landing at Roundhill as Chief Strategy Officer—a role he describes as bringing all elements of his career together.

Roundhill was founded by Will Hershey and Tim Maloney on a mandate to build genuinely differentiated ETFs—products that provide access to emerging themes or put a distinct spin on established ones. The firm's earlier successes included the first video gaming ETF, the first sports betting ETF, and the first metaverse ETF. More recently, Roundhill launched a concentrated sector suite they call The Bigs, offering pinpoint exposure to just the six largest money center banks or the five largest technology companies—designed as precise, transparent tools for advisors doing sector rotation or investors who want targeted exposure without leverage. The CHAT ETF, actively trading for roughly two weeks at the time of this episode, represents Roundhill's entry into the generative AI category.

Investment Strategy and Approach

CHAT is a fully transparent active ETF—not index-based—built around a two-part selection framework Mazza describes as talking the talk and walking the walk. The first component is a transcript score: Roundhill systematically reviews publicly available company documents—filings, presentations, press releases—and scores companies based on how frequently and substantively they reference generative AI and related terms. The more deeply a company engages with the subject in its public communications, the higher its score.

The second component is a sector score grounded in fundamental analysis: revenue, profits, and research and development spending specifically directed toward AI. This layer is where actual business involvement gets assessed. The combination of the two is intentional—the transcript screen alone could surface companies that are simply loading up their language with buzzwords, as happened in the blockchain era when firms changed names or rewrote prospectuses to capture a trend. The sector score ensures that the portfolio reflects companies where the business fundamentals support the narrative.

The combined ranking drives portfolio construction. The fund generally holds between 25 and 50 securities, drawn from both US-listed companies and international names accessible through direct listings or ADRs. Nvidia, with both the highest transcript score and the most direct fundamental exposure to AI semiconductor demand, is the fund's largest holding. Microsoft, with its investment in OpenAI and the integration of AI across Bing, Office co-pilot, and other products, is another core position.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

Generative AI vs. AI: A Distinction That Matters for Portfolio Construction

Mazza drew a careful line between artificial intelligence broadly and generative AI specifically—a distinction he argues has real investment implications, not just semantic ones. AI in the broad sense has been evolving for decades and is already embedded across industries: healthcare diagnostics, autonomous vehicle systems, customer service chatbots, and the recommendation algorithms running quietly behind consumer applications. As an investment theme, its breadth makes it difficult to define with precision, and products built around it can end up holding widely divergent companies.

Generative AI is a more specific subset: systems that use machine learning and large language models to create new content—text, images, video, audio—in response to prompts. ChatGPT is the most visible current application, having reached 100 million users faster than any application before it. But Mazza identified healthcare, education, creative industries, and product design as areas where generative AI's impact is likely to expand significantly. The infrastructure powering this—semiconductors, software, network systems, services, and consulting for implementation—is where the fund focuses its attention.

Why Active Over Index

The choice of an active structure was a deliberate conclusion from the Roundhill team. For themes with an established body of companies and stable revenue streams—cloud computing, cybersecurity—an index built on relatively durable criteria can capture the category reasonably well. Generative AI, Mazza argued, is evolving far too quickly for that. The companies leading the space, the nature of their AI-related revenues, and the definition of meaningful AI involvement are all in active flux. A rules-based index compiled today could be materially outdated within months.

The decision to be fully transparent—disclosing holdings daily—was a commitment made alongside the active structure. Mazza views it as essential given the level of investor and advisor scrutiny around AI-related products. Investors can see exactly what the fund holds every day, without delay.

Nvidia at the Top: How the Scoring Actually Works

The conversation around portfolio construction centered at length on Nvidia, which Mazza used to illustrate how the two scores interact. Nvidia earned its position at the top of the portfolio on both dimensions independently. Its public communications have been dense with generative AI language, particularly around chip demand for AI training and inference workloads. Its fundamental business, specifically the portion of forward revenue tied to AI-driven chip sales, supports the highest sector score in the portfolio as well. The result of that double-high rank is the fund's largest active position. In the period surrounding Nvidia's most recent earnings report at the time of the interview, the fund's exposure contributed significantly to performance—an outcome Mazza attributed directly to the structure of the selection process rather than a discretionary call on the stock.

Notable Insights

"Generative AI takes that a step further. It's a type of artificial intelligence that actually uses machines and computers and programming language to create new content—text, images, videos."

"I hate to say that it's limitless, but I don't actually think we're at a point to truly understand how much generative AI can do, because so much is happening now in a short period of time."

"This is not something that you have to make a bet on and say I hope this happens ten years from now. This is today—everyone from a ten-year-old to grandmas and grandpas can use generative AI in some way, shape, or form."

Key Takeaways

  • Generative AI is a distinct subset of AI broadly—focused on systems that create new content using machine learning and large language models, not just systems that apply AI to existing processes.
  • The fund uses a two-part scoring model: a transcript score measuring how substantively companies discuss AI in public filings, and a sector score measuring actual business exposure through revenue, profits, and R&D.
  • The portfolio holds between 25 and 50 securities, including both US-listed companies and international names through direct listings or ADRs.
  • Roundhill chose an active structure because the generative AI space is evolving faster than a static index can track; the fund discloses holdings daily for full transparency.
  • Nvidia's top weighting reflects both the highest transcript score in the portfolio and the strongest fundamental exposure to AI-driven chip demand.
  • Mazza identifies healthcare, education, creative industries, and product design as expansion areas for generative AI beyond its current consumer applications.

What This Means for Advisors

For advisors fielding client questions about AI exposure, the CHAT ETF conversation offers a practical framework for evaluating how generative AI products are actually constructed. Not all AI-branded funds are built the same way—some cast very broad nets across the technology sector, while others, like this one, attempt to isolate companies with genuine operational and financial exposure to generative AI specifically. Understanding the selection methodology helps advisors make meaningful distinctions between products that are using similar language.

The dual-score approach Mazza describes—transcript analysis combined with fundamental screening—is a reasonable model for advisors doing their own due diligence on any thematic product. It distinguishes companies that are substantively engaged with a technology from those that are simply using the vocabulary. Full daily transparency makes ongoing monitoring and client reporting straightforward.

For advisors considering thematic allocations around AI, Mazza's observation on timing is worth taking seriously. This is not a speculative 10-year bet. The adoption curve for generative AI has already broken records, and the companies building and powering the underlying infrastructure are generating real, growing revenue from it now.

Hear the full conversation with Dave Mazza on Behind the Ticker, available on all major podcast platforms.

title: Garrett Stevens on Launching an ETF: What Issuers Get Wrong About Distribution and Growth
metaDescription: Exchange Traded Concepts founder Garrett Stevens on ETF launch costs, the SEC quiet period, awareness vs. sales campaigns, and the active vs. passive decision.
content:
Garrett Stevens has been in the ETF industry long enough to have made almost every costly mistake in the book—and built a business specifically out of helping others avoid them. As founder of Exchange Traded Concepts, Stevens runs one of the leading white label ETF platforms in the country, having guided dozens of fund launches from initial concept to first trade. On this episode of Behind the Ticker, Brad and Garrett walked through the full arc of the ETF launch process: regulatory timelines, distribution strategy, the active versus passive decision, and why patience may be the most underrated quality in the ETF business.

About Garrett Stevens and Exchange Traded Concepts

Stevens grew up in Oklahoma City and started his career as a retail investment advisor before eventually partnering to file his own family of ETFs in 2008. Back then, launching a fund required exemptive relief from the SEC—a process that cost close to a million dollars in legal fees and took nearly a year just to receive permission. His fund family was roughly 32nd in line for that relief. By the time the products actually launched, the capital intended for marketing had been largely consumed by legal and launch costs. The funds never gained meaningful traction.

That experience planted the seed for Exchange Traded Concepts. If other aspiring issuers could use an existing infrastructure and existing exemptive relief, they could reach the market in a fraction of the time and at a fraction of the cost. With ETC's regulatory framework already in place, the firm can take a fund from first conversation to first trade in as little as 100 days—a record they have actually hit—at a cost of roughly $90,000 all-in. ETC operates three separate multiple-series trusts, each with different service provider partnerships: US Bancorp, SEI, and BNY Mellon. Offering multiple trust options gives clients choice across service providers, with ETC guiding the match based on product complexity and existing relationships. Over the years, Stevens has brought portfolio management, legal, compliance, marketing, and accounting functions in-house, while continuing to outsource fund administration, custody, and distribution to third parties.

What New Issuers Get Wrong: Distribution and Expectations

When Brad asked Stevens what new issuers consistently misunderstand about the ETF business, the answer was immediate. The prevailing assumption—that a well-designed product will attract assets on its merits—is almost always wrong. Advisors don't automatically recognize that they need a new strategy. Themes need time to become front-of-mind. Track records need time to build. The issuers who succeed are the ones who plan for that runway.

Stevens's framework for distribution: treat it as an awareness campaign, not a sales campaign. Spending heavily on wholesalers or direct sales efforts to drive immediate ticket drops is expensive and frequently produces poor returns for newer funds. Building awareness through consistent, lower-cost activity—drip campaigns, content, trade press—is more durable and preserves capital for the moment when a track record, a market event, or a specific theme actually warrants a direct ask.

He offered a concrete example. ETC brought a fund to market that gathered $90–100 million in its first six months, then sat at that level for roughly four years. In year four, the fund picked up $2 billion. The issuers who succeeded were the ones who had both the capital and the patience to stay in the game until the theme aligned with market sentiment. Those who ran out of runway before that moment never saw the payoff.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

The Regulatory Timeline—and the Quiet Period

Under current rules, the SEC has 75 days from fund filing to declare a fund effective. Comments typically arrive around day 45, covering disclosure language, risk factor ordering, and other technical items. All-in, from a signed agreement with ETC to an actively trading fund, the realistic timeline is closer to 120 days.

What many new issuers don't anticipate: during those 75 days, the fund is an unregistered security. Marketing or promoting it publicly during that window is considered gun jumping by the SEC, and they can delay launch by six months if they determine it occurred. Stevens recommends a deliberate gap between the fund going effective and the actual listing date. That window—typically a few weeks—can be used to build the fund website, brief existing clients, and line up seed assets in a way that creates momentum rather than starting from zero on day one. Coordinating how seed assets enter the fund is something ETC spends significant time planning with each client, including guidance on how to move assets in gradually as volume rather than placing everything at once.

Active vs. Passive: The Line Is Blurrier Than It Looks

Stevens pushed back on some common assumptions about the active-versus-passive decision. The defining factor, he explained, is not trading frequency but how securities are selected. ETC has managed index-based products that turn over 80% of their holdings each week and are still considered passive because they track an established index. Meanwhile, an active fund with minimal turnover remains active because selection involves manager discretion.

The practical implications for issuers are significant. Custom indexes are not currently regulated the way funds are, so issuers who build around an index can discuss it more freely in marketing materials than they can the fund itself. Index strategies can show backtested performance; a fund cannot. For issuers who are not registered investment advisors, a custom index structure can sidestep the requirement to register with the SEC—saving both time and cost. Stevens also noted that many strategies managers think of as active can often be converted into rules-based index methodologies without meaningful loss of fidelity, simply by formalizing the decision rules already embedded in their process.

Service Providers and the ETC Infrastructure

Stevens described how ETC has evolved its own infrastructure over the years. What started as an effort to get their own funds to market more efficiently has grown into a platform with four full-time in-house portfolio managers, a full in-house legal and compliance team, and in-house marketing and accounting functions. The choice to offer three separate trusts with different custodial and administrative partners is unusual in the white label space—Stevens said it's specifically about giving clients options that fit their existing relationships and the complexity of what they're launching, rather than forcing every fund into the same structure.

Notable Insights

"The ETF business is not—if you build it they will come. Much to the chagrin of many people, they think they have this product and frankly a lot of people think they're not going to need to market it."

"It's an awareness campaign. There's a tendency to try and get out there and try to get people to buy the fund right now, which is expensive. You can do that in a much less expensive manner and have a higher success rate over time."

"It sat there for about four years, and in the fourth year it picked up $2 billion. You've got to wait, and you've got to be prepared to pay the bills and spend the marketing dollars."

Key Takeaways

  • Launching a fund through a white label platform like ETC now costs roughly $90,000 all-in and can be completed in approximately 120 days from agreement to first trade.
  • New issuers routinely underestimate how long asset gathering takes—building a track record and waiting for market sentiment to align is the norm, not the exception.
  • Effective distribution is an awareness campaign first; high-spend sales efforts often burn capital before a fund has the track record to justify the ask.
  • During the SEC's 75-day review window, public marketing of the fund is prohibited—planning a deliberate gap between effectiveness and listing allows a more controlled, momentum-building launch.
  • The active-versus-passive distinction turns on security selection methodology, not turnover frequency—and index structures carry distinct regulatory and marketing advantages.
  • Custom indexes allow issuers to discuss backtested performance, operate outside certain registration requirements, and talk about the strategy more freely than the fund itself permits.

What This Means for Advisors

For advisors, this episode offers a useful window into how the products they use are built and brought to market. Understanding the economics of ETF launches—the capital required, the typical growth arc, and the regulatory constraints around marketing—provides important context for evaluating newer products. A fund with $20 million in assets after two years isn't necessarily failing; it may simply be in the awareness-building phase, waiting for market conditions or a track record that makes the direct ask viable.

Stevens's comments on the active-versus-passive distinction are also worth noting for advisors conducting due diligence. The regulatory and marketing advantages of index-based structures mean that a product tracking a custom index may offer equivalent or superior strategy exposure to an active fund, with the added benefit of backtested performance commentary advisors can reference. Knowing how those structures work helps advisors ask better questions when evaluating ETFs built on either framework.

Catch the full conversation with Garrett Stevens on Behind the Ticker, available on all major podcast platforms.

title: Daniel Snover on the Adaptive Risk Parity ETF: A Systematic Framework for Every Market Cycle
metaDescription: PMV Capital founder Daniel Snover explains adaptive risk parity, why 60/40 fails at diversification, and the macro framework powering his systematic ETF.
content:
Daniel Snover came of age as a portfolio manager during the 2008-2009 financial crisis, and that formative experience shapes everything about how he thinks about risk. As founder of PMV Capital and portfolio manager of their Adaptive Risk Parity ETF, Snover has spent more than a decade refining a systematic approach designed to minimize market cycle exposure across stocks, bonds, and commodities. On this episode of Behind the Ticker, Brad sat down with Snover to unpack what risk parity actually means, why the 60/40 portfolio falls short, and how PMV's adaptive process integrates macroeconomic thinking into a fully systematic framework.

About Daniel Snover and PMV Capital

Daniel Snover started his career in portfolio management and asset allocation right as the financial crisis was unfolding. He credits that early experience with shaping his investment philosophy—specifically his focus on managing systematic market risk through diversified asset allocation. He went on to become a partner at his first firm, eventually selling the business in 2019. After spending nine months with the acquiring firm and finding the portfolio management work wasn't what he wanted, he brought two long-time colleagues together to launch PMV Capital in 2020.

PMV—the name itself is the equation for momentum—is built around a core belief in systematic, rules-based processes. Snover notes that even discretionary approaches, if disciplined enough, can ultimately be formalized into a system. At PMV, the strategy is expressed through their Adaptive Risk Parity ETF, launched in December 2022, alongside a series of SMA portfolios for advisors who want a more customized application of the same framework.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Risk parity, at its most fundamental level, is about removing systematic market risk from a portfolio—capturing the return premium of underlying assets while combining them so that each asset's systematic risk offsets the others. Where traditional portfolios concentrate the vast majority of their risk in equities, risk parity seeks genuine diversification by balancing risk allocations across asset classes. Snover points out that the correlation of a 60/40 portfolio to the S&P 500 is approximately 0.95—a figure that makes clear most investors are carrying far more equity risk than the headline allocation implies.

PMV's adaptive version takes this further by integrating the macroeconomic cycle directly into the process. Rather than maintaining static allocations across four economic environments defined by the intersection of growth and inflation, the strategy uses momentum signals to identify where the macro cycle sits at any given moment and adjusts positioning accordingly. PMV trades eight asset classes through a fund-of-funds structure—using other ETFs rather than futures contracts to preserve the tax efficiency of a custom basket creation process. Those eight asset classes include US equities, international developed markets, emerging markets, short-duration T-bills, long-duration Treasuries, broad commodities, and physical gold, which is separated out specifically for its behavior in stagflationary environments.

Each monthly rebalance starts from a clean slate. The model measures momentum across multiple lookback windows—from three to twelve months—to avoid over-fitting to any single time frame. Assets with positive momentum across those windows are candidates, and the portfolio then applies risk parity weighting among them, subject to a 40% maximum allocation to any single position.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

One Systematic Risk Drives Every Asset Class

A core insight Snover returns to repeatedly is that stocks, bonds, and commodities don't each carry their own independent risk—they're all driven by the same underlying economy and simply respond to it differently. Economic growth and inflation are the two primary variables, and assets differ in how they're affected by each combination. Commodities tend to perform best in high-growth, high-inflation environments, while long-duration Treasuries do well when growth is slowing and inflation is low. Understanding this matrix—the classic framework that Snover attributes to Bridgewater's research—is the intellectual foundation for everything PMV builds. The practical implication: there isn't a stock risk and a bond risk, there is one macro cycle expressing itself across different instruments.

The Adaptive Layer: Momentum as a Macro Signal

What distinguishes PMV from a static risk parity approach is the momentum overlay. The firm's thesis is that the broad momentum trends visible in asset class price behavior are themselves generated by the underlying macro environment. Looking at the spread between broad commodities and long-duration Treasuries, for instance, gives a real-time read on where growth and inflation are heading. From 2020 through mid-2022, commodities were sharply positive while long-duration Treasuries were deeply negative—a clear signal of high-growth, high-inflation conditions. By the time of this interview, those spreads had narrowed considerably, signaling the market was pricing in a slowdown. The model reads these trends rather than forecasting them independently, using multiple lookback windows specifically to capture persistent trends while filtering out noise.

Tax Efficiency Through Fund Structure

The decision to structure the portfolio as a fund of ETFs, rather than trading futures directly, was a deliberate one. Futures contracts subject investors to a 60/40 tax treatment regardless of holding period. By trading other ETFs—and leveraging the custom basket creation process available to ETF structures—PMV can minimize realized capital gains for investors. Snover acknowledges the marginal additional cost from the underlying ETF expenses, but frames the tax benefit as clearly worth it for the taxable investors the strategy is designed to serve.

Notable Insights

"The 60/40 portfolio—if the stocks are four or five times riskier than the bonds, then stocks are accounting for more than 90% of the risk of that portfolio. The correlation of a 60/40 portfolio to the S&P 500 is around 0.95, which is telling you you're not getting any diversification benefits."

"In reality, it's one systematic risk. They're all driven by the same underlying economy—they are just influenced differently by those tradeoffs."

"When our strategy will perform best is when we are in an environment when those trends are persisting—when more than one asset class is performing well."

Key Takeaways

  • Risk parity balances risk across asset classes rather than dollar allocations, targeting genuine diversification across economic environments.
  • PMV's adaptive process uses momentum signals from actual asset behavior to determine macro positioning—no top-down forecasting required.
  • The portfolio trades eight asset classes through a fund-of-funds structure, chosen for its capital gains efficiency relative to futures-based alternatives.
  • Monthly rebalancing uses multiple lookback windows spanning three to twelve months to avoid over-fitting and capture persistent trends.
  • Physical gold is held separately from broad commodities because the two diverge meaningfully in stagflationary environments.
  • A 40% maximum cap on any individual position guards against over-concentration even when multiple windows point in the same direction.

What This Means for Advisors

For advisors, the case Snover makes is direct: traditional 60/40 portfolios are not as diversified as they appear. When nearly all portfolio risk traces back to equity market exposure, investors are taking far more concentrated bets than the allocation split suggests—as 2022 made painfully clear when both stocks and bonds declined simultaneously. Risk parity offers an alternative architecture that spreads risk more genuinely across economic environments.

The adaptive version PMV runs adds a layer of responsiveness to macro trends, which can be useful for advisors who want systematic, rules-based exposure to a diversified strategy without relying on active manager judgment. The monthly rebalance frequency and transparent fund-of-funds structure make the strategy straightforward to explain and monitor.

For advisors running SMA portfolios, PMV also offers risk-parity-style allocation models that blend their ETF with static positions, allowing customization around individual client needs and risk profiles.

Hear the full conversation with Daniel Snover on Behind the Ticker, available on all major podcast platforms.

title: Patricia Lizarraga on Hypatia Capital: Investing in Female CEO Outperformance
metaDescription: Patricia Lizarraga of Hypatia Capital breaks down the investment thesis behind a female CEO-focused ETF and how she built it from scratch over 15 years.
content:
Patricia Lizarraga spent years as an M&A banker working primarily with CEOs and CFOs—almost all of them men. But occasionally a woman executive would come through her client roster, and the contrast was consistent enough to stay with her. "I would be extremely impressed both by her results and by her business acumen in general," she told Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker. That observation, repeated across years of deal work, eventually became an investment thesis—and then an ETF that gives any investor access to companies led by female chief executives.

About Patricia Lizarraga and Hypatia Capital

Patricia Lizarraga is CEO of Hypatia Capital, which she founded approximately 15 years ago. Her background in investment banking and private equity gave her direct access to senior executives across industries, and she used that network to build an advisory firm serving senior women executives before evolving toward investment products. The firm is named after Hypatia of Alexandria—the first published female mathematician on record, a fifth-century neoplatonist whose work predates modern Alexandria. Lizarraga is building a business around women who excel in environments not designed for them, and the name reflects that orientation.

Outside the office, she splits her time between J/24 keelboat racing and botanical illustration—taking classes through the New York Botanical Garden and Edinburgh's online programs. She describes the two hobbies as polar opposites, which she said suits her well.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The fund invests in all American public companies with female CEOs, spanning small cap to mega cap. At the time of recording, the portfolio held 124 companies across all 11 GICS sectors—including General Dynamics, Northrop Grumman, Occidental Petroleum, and a substantial biotech and pharmaceutical sleeve. The universe is intentionally broad.

The investment thesis is straightforward: the CEO role is harder to reach for women than for men, which means those who get there have cleared a higher bar. They are extraordinary performers within an already high-performing cohort—not because of a generalizable leadership trait, but because of what surviving and succeeding through more obstacles selects for. "The only thing that we find in common is that they're female—and that creates outperformance," Lizarraga said.

Index construction is designed to isolate that female factor by stripping out two confounding variables: size and industry. A market-cap weighted portfolio would effectively become a bet on large companies that happen to have female CEOs. An unconstrained industry allocation would overweight sectors with more female representation. The solution: equal weight all positions, and calibrate industry exposure to match the FTSE Wilshire Small Cap Index. If that benchmark holds 6% in energy, the fund targets 6% in energy—even if female CEO representation in that sector is thin. This construction makes the portfolio correlate most closely to a small-cap index, and Lizarraga positions it accordingly in advisor conversations: it fits in the small-cap sleeve.

Rebalancing happens monthly. Any company that appoints a female CEO enters the portfolio that month; any that loses one exits. Leadership changes drive the holdings in near real time.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

Building the Database From Scratch

One of the most concrete challenges Lizarraga faced was data. The investment thesis required a historical record of which companies had female CEOs, when tenures began, and when they ended. That data didn't exist in a form she could purchase. She approached multiple data providers, found nothing adequate, and built the database herself. "We had to recreate it from scratch," she said. That resource is now proprietary and underpins both the index and the firm's operational advantage in maintaining it. The service provider stack she assembled reflects the same care: Ultimus as the platform, Vident as sub-advisor, Wilshire as calculation agent, Brown Brothers Harriman as custodian, and Blank Rome as trust legal counsel.

Choosing NYSE Over NASDAQ

Both the NYSE and NASDAQ were serious listing candidates—both are led by female CEOs, which made the choice relevant to the fund's theme. Lizarraga chose the NYSE because approximately two-thirds of the companies in the fund's portfolio trade there. Listing where the portfolio lives created natural alignment and opened potential for content partnerships and events as the fund grew.

The Educational Initiative and Broader Mission

Lizarraga sees the fund as a vehicle for a broader argument: more money flows into early-stage female-focused private funds than into gender-lens public market products, which she calls "upside down." Public market investing should represent a far larger share of any portfolio than alternatives, and gender-lens exposure should logically start there. "The public side should be much larger," she said, "and it's appropriate for somebody who has a $1,000 investment portfolio and for somebody who has a multi-billion-dollar investment portfolio."

Her medium-term plan includes educational outreach to universities and women's organizations—positioning the fund as a first investment vehicle for younger women who have heard about the lack of female leadership but haven't had a clear financial entry point into the conversation.

Notable Insights

"It's harder for women to get to the CEO role, so the ones that do make it by definition are extraordinary in that CEO cohort. They have a harder time getting there, so they have to jump through extra hoops or do something that makes them extraordinary."

"The public side should be much larger, and that's also a part of your portfolio where it's appropriate for everybody—appropriate for somebody who has a $1,000 investment portfolio and for somebody who has a multi-billion-dollar investment portfolio."

"Looking at all 124 of these individuals, it was really hard to ascribe one leadership trait or one personality trait, because it really is dependent on the company. The only thing that we find in common is that they're female—and that creates outperformance."

Key Takeaways

  • The fund invests in all American public companies with female CEOs, spanning small to mega cap—approximately 124 holdings across all 11 GICS sectors.
  • Equal weighting eliminates size bias; industry neutrality benchmarked to the FTSE Wilshire Small Cap Index eliminates sector bias—isolating the female leadership factor specifically.
  • Monthly rebalancing keeps the portfolio current as CEO appointments change, making it among the most responsive passive-concept ETFs in its category.
  • Hypatia Capital built its own historical database of female CEO tenures from scratch after finding no adequate off-the-shelf data source.
  • The service provider stack—Ultimus, Vident, Wilshire, Brown Brothers Harriman, and Blank Rome—reflects a deliberate build that prioritized operational quality.
  • The firm has a planned educational initiative targeting young women, using the fund as a first investment vehicle to build early financial engagement.

What This Means for Advisors

The fund's thesis travels well across the political and values spectrum of a typical client base because it rests on merit, not ideology: women who become CEOs cleared a higher bar to get there, which selects for extraordinary performers. That argument requires no values alignment from the advisor or the client—just a willingness to follow the logic.

Practically, the small-cap correlation means the fund fits in that allocation as a replacement for or complement to existing exposure, not as a standalone thematic overlay. Monthly rebalancing keeps it current without significant transaction friction. For advisors serving women investors or clients who want their portfolio to reflect something real about how they think—the fund offers a product with a fifteen-year thesis behind it, built by someone who has spent that entire time thinking about nothing else.

Listen to This Episode

Patricia Lizarraga's full conversation with Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker covers her investment banking and private equity background, how Hypatia Capital evolved from advisory firm to ETF issuer, the mechanics of the index construction, the challenge of building a historical data set from scratch, and her plans for the fund's educational mission. For advisors who want to understand gender-lens investing beyond the surface—and for anyone interested in what it takes to build a niche ETF from a clear thesis over a very long runway—the episode is worth the time.

title: Tommy Mancuso on Building the Betting, Alcohol, and Drugs ETF
metaDescription: Tommy Mancuso, CEO of the Bad Investment Company, explains how he built a betting, alcohol, and drugs ETF as a transparent counter to ESG investing.
content:
Tommy Mancuso was working inside traditional financial services long enough to understand what it wasn't doing for a certain kind of investor. The pandemic sharpened what he was already seeing: retail investors chasing meme stocks, ESG products obscuring what they actually owned, and no investment product willing to say the obvious—that gambling, alcohol, and pharmaceutical companies have been around since the Roman Empire and aren't going anywhere. His answer was the Bad Investment Company and a fund built explicitly around those three categories.

About Tommy Mancuso and the Bad Investment Company

Tommy Mancuso is 31 years old and has spent nearly a decade in financial services. He started at a mutual fund shop in San Diego during college, earned his securities licenses, and moved to Merrill Lynch on the wealth management side. The wirehouse model wasn't for him. "I realized the wirehouse world was maybe not necessarily for me," he told Brad Roth during the conversation. He joined Prime Capital Investment Advisers in 2016, where being younger than most advisors in the room gave him permission to think differently.

The pandemic changed the trajectory of what he'd do with that perspective. Watching retail investors get into meme stocks, then watching ESG products explode with flows driven more by enthusiasm than transparency, he started sketching an alternative. He ran the acronym—Betting, Alcohol, Drugs—through Morningstar and Bloomberg, built a quick sample portfolio, back-tested it, and was surprised by what he found. After conversations with service providers and potential investors that generated strong early feedback, he partnered with EQ Indexes to formalize the index and launched in December 2021. He is the CEO of the Bad Investment Company.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The fund's portfolio is organized into four allocation sleeves. Betting gets 33.3%, covering gaming and casino companies—names like Wynn, MGM, DraftKings, and Penn Gaming. Pharmaceuticals and biotech get 33.3%, including major names like Johnson & Johnson, Pfizer, Eli Lilly, and Moderna. Alcohol gets 23.3%, covering major producers like Diageo, AB InBev, and Constellation Brands. Cannabis gets 10%, currently three holdings including Canopy Growth and Tilray—all Canadian-based companies that trade as foreign private issuers on U.S. exchanges.

The 10% cannabis allocation was carved from the original alcohol sleeve. Mancuso's reasoning: there aren't enough high-quality alcohol companies to fill a full third of the portfolio, while the pharmaceutical and biotech universe offered too many strong names to cut. Giving the 10% to cannabis made the construction tighter.

Within each sleeve, holdings are equal weighted. The portfolio holds approximately 54 positions, a number that has fluctuated between 52 and 57 since launch. A quarterly rebalance evaluates market caps across all holdings and typically produces one or two position changes per cycle. The minimum market cap for inclusion runs approximately $1 billion.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

The ESG Anti-Thesis—But Not a War on ESG

Mancuso is careful to distinguish the fund's positioning from outright ESG hostility. "I'm not going to say we hate ESG by any means," he said. "There's a place for it." What bothers him is opacity. ESG scoring, in his view, is manipulable—companies can check enough boxes to earn a green label that doesn't reflect their actual business, and investors can end up holding oil and gas inside funds they thought were environmental. The problem isn't the values; it's the transparency. "When you're trying to greenwash some stuff, when you're trying to manipulate the scoring criteria so you can attract a certain side and put a little bit of a mask on—that's the issue I see with ESG: the lack of transparency."

His fund answers that problem with complete legibility. What you see is exactly what you get: the largest gaming and casino companies, the major alcohol producers, the biggest pharmaceutical and biotech firms, and a small cannabis allocation. No surprises behind the label.

Recession Resistance as a Structural Feature

The fund's defensive characteristics are built into the thesis rather than added on top of it. Three categories that sound speculative on the surface carry structural recession resistance underneath. Gambling, drinking, and healthcare demand don't disappear in economic downturns—and in some cases, Mancuso notes, they increase. Pharmaceutical and biotech companies serve ongoing healthcare needs regardless of where the business cycle sits. Large alcohol companies have been using the current environment to acquire smaller competitors at discounted prices, consolidating market share away from micro-breweries and ready-to-drink brands that can't weather the same conditions.

The 2022 performance reflected this. The fund was down in a difficult year for equities broadly, but it outperformed the S&P 500 by a couple of percentage points and, in Mancuso's framing, "destroyed the NASDAQ." In a year when the popular technology-heavy indexes got hit hardest, the fund's defensive composition—value-tilted, cash-flow-generating businesses—held up relatively well.

Legalization as the Growth Engine

The fund's growth thesis doesn't rest on product innovation. It rests on policy expansion. Gambling is now legal in some form in approximately 26 states, and the trend is toward more, not fewer, states entering the market. Cannabis is following a similar arc, working through regulatory friction now but expanding its legal addressable market over time. "Our growth is due to legalization, not innovation, which can take time," Mancuso said. That distinction matters for how the fund behaves over a full cycle—it's not a technology bet that can unravel with a product cycle. It's a long-duration bet on the steady expansion of regulated markets in categories with consistent underlying demand.

Notable Insights

"I don't think social stigma should be a factor when it comes to investing. At the end of the day, we are investing to make money."

"Our growth is due to legalization, not innovation, which can take time. Look at the gambling space—we're seeing it's about 26 states now where there's some form of gambling legal—and then cannabis following the similar path."

"People are going to continue to get sick—and in some cases, again, more often during periods of financial stress."

Key Takeaways

  • The portfolio is organized into four sleeves: 33.3% betting, 23.3% alcohol, 33.3% pharmaceuticals and biotech, and 10% cannabis—with the cannabis allocation carved from the original alcohol sleeve.
  • Holdings within each sleeve are equal weighted; the total portfolio holds approximately 54 names with a minimum market cap of around $1 billion.
  • Quarterly rebalancing reviews market caps across all sleeves and typically produces one or two position changes per cycle.
  • The fund's defensive characteristics come from consistent demand for gambling, alcohol, and healthcare across economic cycles—demand that doesn't require a strong macro environment to persist.
  • The growth thesis is built on legalization expansion in gambling and cannabis, not on product innovation or technology cycles.
  • The anti-ESG positioning is about transparency, not ideology—social stigma shouldn't function as an investment factor, and the fund makes no attempt to disguise what it owns.

What This Means for Advisors

Mancuso sees the fund fitting most naturally in a large-cap value allocation—replacing that exposure rather than layering on top of it, while adding growth characteristics that pure value plays don't typically carry. The legalization tailwinds in gambling and cannabis create optionality that doesn't require a specific macro scenario to play out, just the continued expansion of regulated markets that is already underway.

For advisors serving clients who have grown skeptical of ESG-labeled products, the fund provides a credible alternative with complete transparency about what it owns and why. For advisors building out defensive equity allocations, the recession-resistant characteristics of the portfolio's core businesses—gaming, alcohol, and pharmaceuticals—offer a counterweight to more cyclically exposed holdings. The product is unconventional by design, but the portfolio construction logic is clear and the underlying businesses have a long track record of generating cash flow across market environments.

Listen to This Episode

Tommy Mancuso's full conversation with Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker covers his career path from the wirehouse world to launching an independent ETF, the mechanics of his index construction, how he thinks about distribution in the early innings of building an asset management firm, and why he's already reserving tickers for future products. It's a practical window into what it takes to bring a contrarian product to market and what makes the ETF structure the right vehicle for doing it. The episode is worth the time for any advisor evaluating alternative equity strategies or anyone curious about where the edges of ETF product innovation are heading.

title: Welcome to Behind the Ticker: Inside the ETF Industry with Brad Roth
metaDescription: Brad Roth launches Behind the Ticker, a podcast exploring the strategies, businesses, and entrepreneurs driving the ETF industry forward.
content:
Brad Roth has spent his career on the operating side of asset management—building strategies, managing portfolios, and navigating the systems most investors encounter only as finished products. Behind the Ticker is his attempt to open that up. Hosted by Roth, who serves as Chief Investment Officer of THOR Financial Technologies and portfolio manager of the THOR Low Volatility ETF, the show is built around a simple premise: there is a great deal that happens behind the ticker, and most of it goes unseen.

About Brad Roth and THOR Financial Technologies

Brad Roth is the Chief Investment Officer of THOR Financial Technologies, a systematic asset management firm. He also serves as portfolio manager of the THOR Low Volatility ETF, a rules-based strategy focused on managing downside exposure while staying invested in equity markets. His background spans both the practitioner and entrepreneurial sides of asset management—an unusual combination that shapes how he approaches the conversations on Behind the Ticker.

THOR Financial Technologies is built on a systematic, technology-driven investment philosophy. That discipline informs Roth's curiosity about how other managers—particularly those building ETFs from the ground up—think about their own process, their business model, and the decisions that led them to launch something new. He isn't approaching these conversations as an outside observer. He's an operator asking other operators to explain their work, which tends to produce more direct and substantive exchanges than standard fund marketing interviews allow.

Behind the Ticker is a natural extension of that professional curiosity. The show gives Roth a structured forum to ask the questions that don't appear in fund fact sheets or investor presentations—questions about how strategies were designed, what early mistakes cost real money, and what the day-to-day reality of running an investment firm actually looks like.

What Behind the Ticker Is Built to Do

The show covers portfolio managers and ETF service providers from across the industry—from boutique single-product issuers to established managers running multi-strategy lineups. The goal isn't performance commentary or market prediction. It's something harder to find: a genuine look at how these businesses operate, what drives the people building them, and what the experience of creating an investment product inside the ETF structure really entails.

Roth is explicit that entrepreneurship is a recurring theme. Many of the guests he plans to feature have taken meaningful professional and financial risk to bring a new product or service to market inside an industry that doesn't make that easy. Their experiences navigating service providers, regulatory processes, distribution channels, and capital formation are as instructive as their investment frameworks. Behind the Ticker treats both as worth examining.

Inside the Concept: What Makes This Show Different

The ETF Industry as a Subject

The ETF wrapper has become one of the most efficient vehicles in asset management, and the range of strategies housed inside it has expanded substantially over the past decade. Active ETFs, thematic products, factor-based strategies, and liquid alternatives now sit alongside the passive index funds that defined the category in its early years. That diversity creates genuine complexity—and genuine stories worth telling. Behind the Ticker is built on the belief that those stories deserve a dedicated home.

Roth's vantage point is particularly useful here. As someone who manages an ETF, he understands the mechanics of the product from the inside. He knows what questions matter for advisors and sophisticated investors—questions about index construction, rebalancing methodology, liquidity management, and the practical realities of distribution. That fluency shows up in how he steers each conversation: focused, specific, and attentive to the operational details that generic media coverage tends to skip.

The Entrepreneurship Thread

One of the more underappreciated stories in modern asset management is the number of practitioners who have left larger institutions to build something from scratch inside the ETF structure. The barriers to entry, while real, have come down meaningfully over the past decade. White-label platforms, outsourced compliance functions, and third-party index providers have made it possible for smaller teams—sometimes a single person with a clear thesis—to bring a product to market that would have required a much larger organization a generation ago.

Behind the Ticker is specifically interested in those builders. What made them leave? What did they underestimate about launching? How are they thinking about growth when assets are small and distribution relationships are still being built? These questions produce insights useful to anyone evaluating the people and firms behind the products they recommend—or anyone considering building something themselves.

Format and Audience

Behind the Ticker is a conversational interview show. Roth conducts extended one-on-one conversations with guests, covering their backgrounds, their strategies, their businesses, and the lessons they've drawn from years of building inside the industry. The content is designed to be both educational and engaging—a distinction Roth makes explicit from the outset.

The audience spans advisors looking for deeper context on products they're evaluating, investors trying to understand how ETFs actually function beneath the surface, and industry professionals interested in how peers approach their own businesses. The show is not positioned as investment advice. It is a resource for anyone who wants to understand the ETF world with more depth and candor than a fund prospectus allows.

Notable Insights

"Behind the Ticker uncovers the inner workings of the ETF industry. We will interview portfolio managers and ETF service providers to learn about their work lives and their businesses."

"Many of these individuals are entrepreneurs and will have unique and compelling insights to share."

"As much goes on behind the ticker."

Key Takeaways

  • Behind the Ticker is hosted by Brad Roth, CIO of THOR Financial Technologies and portfolio manager of the THOR Low Volatility ETF.
  • The show focuses on the ETF industry—interviewing portfolio managers and service providers about their strategies, businesses, and the work behind their products.
  • Entrepreneurship is a central theme: many guests have built investment firms or service companies from the ground up inside the ETF structure.
  • The format is conversational and designed for advisors, investors, and industry professionals who want more depth than standard fund marketing provides.
  • Content is produced for educational and entertainment purposes only—not investment advice.
  • Each episode goes beyond the strategy itself and into the lived experience of building and operating inside the ETF industry.

What This Means for Advisors

Advisors are increasingly expected to evaluate not just fund performance but the quality and durability of the firms behind the products they recommend. Understanding how an ETF issuer thinks, how they built their business, and what drives their decision-making is relevant due diligence—and it's the kind of context that is hard to find in standard product literature.

Behind the Ticker provides that kind of deeper evaluation. Each conversation gives advisors access to the perspectives of people actually running these strategies and businesses—not polished investor relations language, but the candid reflection that surfaces in a genuine interview. For advisors who want to stay current on where the ETF industry is heading and who is building inside it, the show is worth adding to the regular rotation.

The show also models the kind of diligence process advisors themselves should be running: go past the marketing materials, understand the people, ask about the hard parts. That's what happens behind the ticker.

Listen to Behind the Ticker

Behind the Ticker is now live. New episodes feature extended conversations with portfolio managers and ETF service providers from across the industry. Subscribe wherever you get your podcasts and follow along as Brad Roth uncovers what's really happening behind the ticker.
